<a id="paper-2604.26175"></a>
### [Hardware-Efficient Quantum Optimization for Transportation Networks via Compressed Adiabatic Evolution](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26175v1)

**Authors:** Talha Azfar, Ruimin Ke, Sean He, Cara Wang, José Holguín-Veras  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26175v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26175_figures/2604.26175_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Hybrid hardware-efficient quantum optimization architecture. A digitized quantum annealing circuit is decomposed into segments with different computational roles. The early portion of the evolution is compressed using approximate quantum compilation (AQC), while the remaining gap-critical portion is replaced by structured variational layers such as QAOA or LC- QAOA. This design reduces circuit depth while preserving optimization capability.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26175_figures/2604.26175_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Spectral-gap-aware intuition for hybrid circuit design. The adiabatic evolution path exhibits regions with different spectral-gap structures. Large-gap regimes are generally more robust and can tolerate circuit compression, whereas the gap-critical region near the minimum spectral gap Δmin is more sensitive and motivates retaining expressive variational circuit layers.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26175_figures/2604.26175_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Two-qubit circuit depth (left axis) and number of feasible solutions (right axis) as a function of compressed prefix length 𝑚for annealing circuits across transportation problem classes: (a) facility location, (b) vehicle routing, and (c) traveling salesman. The results illustrate the tradeoff between circuit depth and feasibility, showing that moderate prefix compression can reduce hardware cost while maintaining or improving feasible-solution discovery.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26175_figures/2604.26175_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Two-qubit depth is shown as color, and marker radius indicates the number of feasible solutions sampled from the circuit.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.26175_figures/2604.26175_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: Diagnostic analysis of compressed annealing prefixes for the VRP instance.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 6</summary>

<img src="2604.26175_figures/2604.26175_fig6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 6: Effect of AQC prefix compression on feasible solution generation from QAOA for the VRP instance. Moderate com- pression can improve feasibility and diversity, while aggressive compression shows mixed behavior across depths.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 7</summary>

<img src="2604.26175_figures/2604.26175_fig7.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 7: Performance of the hybrid AQC+QAOA approach across the (𝑚, 𝑝) parameter grid. Results show tradeoffs between circuit depth, optimization effort, feasibility, and solution quality under varying prefix compression and QAOA depth.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 8</summary>

<img src="2604.26175_figures/2604.26175_fig8.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 8: AQC prefix compression on feasible solution gen- eration with LC-QAOA for the VRP instance. The mismatch of the ansätz from adiabatic evolution causes a sharp drop in performance with compression.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 9</summary>

<img src="2604.26175_figures/2604.26175_fig9.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 9: Performance of AQC compressed layers as initial state circuits for LC-QAOA across the (𝑚, 𝑝) grid. Results show that LC-QAOA works best without any adiabatic initialization.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Developing hardware-efficient quantum optimization algorithms for large-scale transportation problems (TSP, VRP, FLP) that can run on NISQ-era hardware despite high circuit depth and noise.

**Main result.** Moderate prefix compression using Approximate Quantum Compilation (AQC) reduces two-qubit gate depth while maintaining or improving feasible solution discovery, though effectiveness depends on the compatibility between the compressed prefix and the variational ansatz.

**Method.** A hybrid framework combining AQC-based prefix compression of early adiabatic evolution with variational layers (standard QAOA or LC-QAOA) optimized using the CVaR objective function.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a hardware-aware hybrid algorithm for solving transportation logistics problems on near-term quantum computers. By using Approximate Quantum Compilation to compress the early, robust stages of adiabatic evolution, the authors significantly reduce the required two-qubit gate depth. The framework demonstrates that this compression can maintain or even enhance the generation of feasible candidate solutions for problems like the Vehicle Routing Problem. However, the study also reveals that the success of this method depends heavily on the compatibility between the compressed prefix and the subsequent variational layers.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study uses IBM gate-based quantum hardware to solve combinatorial optimization problems (TSP, VRP, FLP) mapped to QUBO/Ising Hamiltonians in the Pauli-Z basis.

**Key observables.** Circuit depth (two-qubit gate depth), solution feasibility probability, objective value, solution diversity, energy variance, and perturbative susceptibility.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Prefix compression length (m), QAOA depth (p), penalty weight (lambda), and the CVaR parameter (alpha).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on small-scale, tractable problem instances (12-16 qubits) and assumes the utility of quantum computing as a candidate generator within a larger classical-quantum pipeline.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the hybrid AQC-QAOA architecture; Figure 2 shows the intuition for spectral-gap-aware design; Figure 5b relates driver energy to feasible solutions; Figure 8 demonstrates performance drops in LC-QAOA due to Hamiltonian mismatch; Figures 9a-d show trends in gate depth, optimization iterations, and objective values.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the transportation optimization problem, details the hybrid AQC-QAOA architecture and compression methodology, describes the mapping of TSP/VRP/FLP to Ising Hamiltonians, presents experimental results on IBM hardware comparing different ansatzes and compression levels, and concludes with a discussion on the trade-offs between depth and feasibility.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Transportation systems such as urban logistics, vehicle routing, and infrastructure planning require solving large-scale combinatorial optimization problems under complex constraints. Problems such as the vehicle routing problem (VRP), traveling salesman problem (TSP), and facility location problem (FLP) involve large discrete search spaces and the need to generate multiple feasible solutions in real time. In this work, we develop a hardware-grounded hybrid quantum optimization framework that uses Approximate Quantum Compilation (AQC) to compress early segments of digitized adiabatic evolution into shallow circuits. The compressed prefix is combined with variational layers, enabling a systematic study of how initialization, circuit depth, and expressivity interact on near-term quantum hardware. All experiments are performed on an IBM gate-based quantum computer, and circuits are evaluated as stochastic generators of candidate transportation plans. Results show that moderate prefix compression reduces two-qubit gate depth while maintaining or improving feasible solution discovery, particularly for routing problems. These benefits depend on compatibility between the compressed prefix and the variational ansatz: while standard QAOA effectively leverages AQC initialization, linear-chain QAOA shows limited improvement. Overall, this work demonstrates that hybrid AQC-QAOA methods provide a practical pathway for hardware-efficient quantum optimization, positioning quantum algorithms as candidate generators within transportation decision-making workflows.

</details>

### [Large-Scale Quantum Circuit Simulation on an Exascale System for QPU Benchmarking](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26423v1)

**Authors:** J. A. Montanez-Barrera, Kristel Michielsen  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26423v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26423_figures/2604.26423_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. Noise in LR-QAOA. (a) Simulation of LR-QAOA under different levels of depolarizing noise as a function of circuit depth. (b) Overlap between the noisy and noiseless approximation ratios for 8-, 10-, and 12- qubit simulations. The solid lines correspond to the prediction from Eq. 8. Three algorithm performance regimes are highlighted with boxes.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26423_figures/2604.26423_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Sampling distribution for the 40-qubit LR-QAOA benchmark at p = 3. KEDs of the mean approximation ratio ¯r are shown for the random sampler at 10 and 100 shots, and for the noiseless simulation at 10 shots. The dashed line indicates the Helios-1 QPU result.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26423_figures/2604.26423_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. Time scaling of LR-QAOA circuits on JUPITER. (a) Runtime as a function of problem size. (b) Strong scaling for the 40-qubit problem. (c) Hardware performance comparison between H100 (JUPITER) and A100 (JUWELS Booster) GPUs. (d) Performance comparison between JUPITER and JUWELS Booster for a 40-qubit problem as a function of LR-QAOA depth.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26423_figures/2604.26423_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. LR-QAOA (p = 3) benchmarking of Helios-1 approximation ratio vs. number of qubits. (a) Comparison with noiseless classical simulations on JUPITER and with the previous-generation Quantinuum QPUs (H2-1). (b) Helios-1 experiments for problem sizes beyond the reach of exact JUQCS simulations on JUPITER. The solid dark green line indicates the true approximation ratio r.(c) Number of two-qubit gates as a function of problem size. The green shaded region and dashed line indicate the largest system size for which Helios-1 samples remain statistically distinguishable from random sampling.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Identifying the boundary between the noise-tolerant and random regimes in large-scale quantum processors and validating hardware performance at the edge of classical tractability.

**Main result.** The Quantinuum Helios-1 processor maintains coherent performance up to 93 qubits, with outputs becoming statistically indistinguishable from random sampling at 95 qubits.

**Method.** The study uses the Linear Ramp Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (LR-QAOA) for benchmarking, combined with large-scale noiseless simulations on the JUPITER exascale supercomputer.

**Summary.** This paper presents a large-scale benchmarking of the 98-qubit Quantinuum Helios-1 trapped-ion processor. By utilizing Europe's first exascale supercomputer, JUPITER, the researchers performed massive noiseless simulations to provide a reference for quantum hardware performance. They successfully identified the transition point where quantum processor outputs become indistinguishable from random noise, finding that coherent performance persists up to 93 qubits. This work establishes a vital framework for validating large-scale quantum computers at the limits of classical simulation capability.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The experimental platform is the Quantinuum Helios-1, a 98-qubit trapped-ion QPU using a QCCD architecture with 137Ba+ hyperfine qubits. The problem model is the Weighted Max-Cut (WMC) on fully connected graphs.

**Key observables.** Approximation ratio (r), overlap (r_ovl), and statistical distinguishability from random sampling using a mean-of-means resampling procedure.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Qubit counts (up to 98), circuit depth (p), number of two-qubit gates (up to 12,834), and the 3-sigma threshold for noise classification.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The benchmarking assumes a non-variational, platform-agnostic protocol (LR-QAOA) to avoid classical optimization complexities and uses a uniform depolarizing error model for analysis.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1(a) shows the simulation of the LR-QAOA approximation ratio versus circuit depth under varying noise levels; Figure 3 illustrates the time scaling of the simulations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the LR-QAOA benchmarking framework, describes the experimental and exascable simulation platforms, details the statistical methodology for regime classification, presents the performance results for Helios-1, and concludes with a hardware-specific cost and scaling analysis.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a quantitative boundary for the utility of NISQ devices and demonstrates how exascale classical computing can be used to validate the reliability of large-scale quantum hardware.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recent advances in quantum computing have enabled the development of quantum processors with hundreds of qubits. However, noise continues to limit the amount of useful information that can be extracted from these systems, making it essential to identify the regime in which experimental outputs remain reliable. In this work, we benchmark Quantinuum Helios-1, a 98-qubit trapped-ion quantum processing unit, using the linear ramp quantum approximate optimization algorithm (LR-QAOA). To this end, we perform large-scale noiseless simulations on JUPITER, Europe's first exascale supercomputer, for circuits of up to 48 qubits and 3,384 two-qubit gates. These simulations, executed on 4,096 nodes equipped with 16,384 GH200 superchips and high-bandwidth CPU-GPU interconnects, provide a reference for validating experimental results at the edge of classical tractability. We find that, up to 48 qubits, Helios-1 remains in a noise-tolerant region, i.e., its samples cannot be clearly distinguished from those coming from a noiseless simulation. We then extend the analysis to larger system sizes using experimental data only, and apply a mean-of-means resampling procedure with a 3$σ$ threshold to determine whether the QPU output is statistically distinguishable from random sampling. This analysis identifies a regime of coherent performance up to 93 qubits (12,834 two-qubit gates), beyond which, at 95 qubits, the outputs become statistically indistinguishable from random sampling. These results demonstrate how exascale classical simulation can be used to validate quantum processors, and provide a quantitative boundary between noise-tolerant and random regimes in quantum processors.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26413"></a>
### [Quantum Gatekeeper: Multi-Factor Context-Bound Image Steganography with VQC Based Key Derivation on Quantum Hardware](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26413v1)

**Authors:** Sahil Tomar, Sandeep Kumar  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26413v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26413_figures/2604.26413_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. Overall architecture of the proposed Quantum Gatekeeper framework. User-defined inputs are transformed into context-bound seed material driving quantum gate derivation, authenticated payload encryption, and dual-region image embedding. The stego image is decodable only by reconstructing the same contextual state and completing authenticated recovery.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26413_figures/2604.26413_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Embedding and extraction workflow of the proposed Quantum Gatekeeper framework. During embedding, an image-bound signature and internal seeds parameterize the VQC and authenticated encryption. During extraction, the same contextual inputs reconstruct the gate key, recover embedded regions, and perform AES-GCM verification.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the lack of extraction-path control in traditional steganography and the challenge of implementing reliable quantum-based key derivation on noisy NISQ hardware.

**Main result.** The 'Quantum Gatekeeper' framework achieves successful, deterministic, all-or-nothing payload recovery and high image fidelity, demonstrating that the VQC-driven extraction path remains stable despite hardware noise.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical approach using Variational Quantum Circuits (VQC) for gate-controlled key derivation, combined with AES-GCM encryption and a dual-region LSB embedding strategy.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new steganography framework called Quantum Gatekeeper that uses quantum circuits to control how hidden data is extracted. By requiring a specific context (like a password and an image signature) to reconstruct the quantum gate key, it ensures that unauthorized users cannot even partially recover the hidden message. The authors demonstrate that while real quantum hardware is noisy, the most probable quantum state remains stable enough to allow for reliable, deterministic data recovery.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system utilizes a Variational Quantum Circuit (VQC) implemented via exact statevector simulation and executed on IBM superconducting quantum hardware (ibm_pittsburgh) using the PennyLane framework.

**Key observables.** Shannon entropy, Total Variation Distance (TVD), Cross-Entropy Benchmarking (XEB), and peak bitstring consistency between simulator and hardware.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The recovery tuple (password, shared secret, context string, and image signature) and the VQC parameters derived from cryptographic hash expansion.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system assumes the cover image remains unaltered (no recompression) and relies on statevector simulation for the primary extraction key to avoid the stochastic nature of hardware sampling.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the overall architecture and context-bound seed derivation; Figure 2 details the embedding and extraction workflow involving the image-bound signature.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies security gaps in steganography, proposes the Quantum Gatekeeper framework, details the hybrid quantum-classical implementation, provides experimental validation on IBM hardware, and concludes with an ablation study and error analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper presents Quantum Gatekeeper, a context-bound image steganography framework where successful payload recovery depends on both cryptographic decryption and the reconstruction of a precise extraction path. The system integrates lossless least significant bit (LSB) embedding with a deterministic variational quantum circuit (VQC)-derived gate key, multi-factor contextual binding, and authenticated encryption. Payload extraction is contingent upon four requisite factors: a password, a shared secret, a user-supplied context string, and a reference image signature. Any deviation in these factors causes the system to read from an incorrect pixel sequence or fail authentication, resulting in silent rejection rather than partial disclosure. The proposed method derives a gatecontrolled extraction key from a seed-conditioned variational circuit, with parameters generated via cryptographic hash expansion and context-dependent image features. To ensure encode/decode consistency, the cryptographic key path is generated via exact statevector simulation; concurrently, IBM superconducting quantum hardware is utilized to evaluate the statistical behavior of the circuit family under physical noise. We introduce a dual-region image layout to resolve the nonce bootstrapping dependency, separating header recovery from payload recovery through independently derived keys. Experimental results confirm successful end-to-end message embedding and recovery on PNG images, demonstrating deterministic success under correct conditions and failure otherwise. The framework supports both text and image payloads; in the image-in-image configuration, a secret image is resized to a fixed resolution prior to embedding, enabling exact pixel-level recovery under correct contextual reconstruction.

</details>

### [Over forty years of research towards the understanding of Quantum Brownian Motion -- the contributions of A. O. Caldeira](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26524v1)

**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26524v1>

**Main problem.** The paper reviews the forty-year evolution of the theory of Quantum Brownian Motion (QBM), focusing on how dissipation and noise influence quantum dynamics, tunneling, and decoherence.

**Main result.** It traces the development from the Caldeira-Leggett model to advanced scattering models and demonstrates how these theories underpin modern quantum decoherence and quantum thermodynamics.

**Method.** The review synthesizes various theoretical frameworks, including path-integral formulations (Feynman-Vernon influence functional), Langevin equations, and quantum master equations.

**Summary.** This review article chronicles the scientific contributions of A. O. Caldeira to the field of Quantum Brownian Motion. It details the transition from classical to quantum dissipative dynamics, highlighting the importance of the Caldeira-Leggett model in understanding tunneling and decoherence. The authors also explore advanced models involving scattering and the implications of these theories for modern quantum thermodynamics and the NISQ era. Ultimately, it connects foundational work in dissipation to contemporary problems in quantum information and many-body dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The primary models discussed are the Caldeira-Leggett model (system coupled to a harmonic oscillator reservoir) and scattering models (momentum-based bilinear coupling), applied to systems like Josephson junctions, LCR circuits, and solitons.

**Key observables.** Tunneling rates, diffusion coefficients, quantum coherences, entropy production, and heat current.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (T), friction/damping coefficient (eta), coupling strength, and frequency scales (thermal vs. friction scales).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumptions include the use of minimal system-plus-environment models and, in some cases, the neglect of initial system-environment correlations (factorizing initial conditions).

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a historical and thematic progression: starting with the foundational Caldeira-Leggett model and its impact on tunneling, moving to alternative scattering-based approaches, and concluding with applications in decoherence theory and quantum thermodynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a comprehensive historical and theoretical roadmap for anyone studying open quantum systems, specifically regarding the transition from quantum to classical regimes and the challenges of defining thermodynamics in the strong-coupling regime.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This article presents a brief account of Amir O. Caldeira's contributions to the theory of quantum Brownian motion. Motivated by its importance, we outline the description of Brownian motion in the quantum regime following Caldeira's first works. In this context, we particularly highlight the effect of dissipation on the tunneling rate out of a metastable state. We then journey along the alternative ways to approach quantum Brownian motion developed by Caldeira during his career, which go beyond the so-called Caldeira-Leggett model. We conclude by summarizing some of Caldeira's contributions to contemporary fields such as the theory of quantum decoherence and quantum thermodynamics, that were strongly inspired by his eponymous approach to quantum Brownian motion.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26601"></a>
### [Quo vadis, stochastic thermodynamics?](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26601v1)

**Authors:** Jan Korbel, Artemy Kolchinsky, Sarah A. M. Loos, Gonzalo Manzano, Rosalba Garcia-Millan, Olga Movilla Miangolarra, Édgar Roldán  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26601v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26601_figures/2604.26601_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Illustration of the ‘dream’ of early developments of stochastic thermodynamics. A tiny nano-sized motor navigates through a biological tissue, tracing a stochastic trajectory. Such motion, powered by fuel and controlled through feedback upon information retrieval, is converted partially into useful work while the rest is dissipated to the surrounding media as heat. The first decades of stochastic thermodynamics research aimed to understand the statistical constraints for various dynamical and thermodynamical properties of such small-scale non-equilibrium processes. AI-generated image using Gemini, inspired by a presentation by Ignacio A. Martínez and on the movie Innerspace (1987).</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The paper explores the expansion of stochastic thermodynamics from its traditional focus on microscopic fluctuations to complex, large-scale, and non-physical systems, specifically addressing the decoupling of statistical irreversibility from physical energy dissipation at macroscopic scales.

**Main result.** The authors review how the framework has evolved to include memory effects, hidden degrees of freedom, and active matter, while highlighting the challenge that thermodynamic bounds become increasingly loose and disconnected from energy dissipation in macroscopic/social contexts.

**Method.** The review synthesizes various theoretical frameworks including Langevin/Fokker-Planck dynamics, Doi-Peliti field theory, optimal transport (Wasserstein distance), and information-theoretic approaches.

**Summary.** This Perspective reviews the evolution of stochastic thermodynamics from a theory of small-scale fluctuations to a broader framework for complex systems. It covers recent progress in handling memory effects, hidden variables, and interacting active matter. Crucially, it addresses the 'decoupling' problem where statistical irreversibility no longer directly quantifies energy dissipation in macroscopic systems. Finally, it explores the application of these thermodynamic principles to biological, computational, and social dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The scope covers a wide range of systems including molecular motors, active matter (run-and-tumble particles), biological networks (cytoskeleton, brain dynamics), and non-physical systems like computational and social dynamics.

**Key observables.** Entropy production (housekeeping and excess), work, heat, dissipation, particle currents, and time-reversal asymmetry.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Microscopic vs. macroscopic scales, Markovian vs. non-Markovian regimes, and the signal-to-noise ratio in currents.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The paper notes that the link between statistical irreversibility and thermodynamic dissipation is less direct in coarse-grained or macroscopic systems, and that embedding hidden variables can lead to non-unique interpretations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides an illustration of a nano-sized motor in biological tissue using fuel and feedback to perform work while dissipating heat.

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a historical and thematic progression: starting with the foundations of fluctuation theorems, moving to recent extensions (memory, hidden variables, active matter), discussing geometric formulations (optimal transport), addressing the challenges of scaling to macroscopic/complex systems, and concluding with applications in non-physical domains.

**Why it may be interesting.** It is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it discusses the fundamental limits of dissipation, the role of memory/non-Markovianity, and the geometric structure of non-equilibrium processes.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Stochastic thermodynamics is a framework for describing non-equilibrium processes at the level of fluctuating trajectories, where the state of a system evolves as a stochastic time series, allowing thermodynamic quantities such as work, heat, and entropy production to be defined along individual realizations rather than at the ensemble level only. Over the past three decades, the field has yielded fundamental results, including fluctuation theorems and several universal bounds, such as thermodynamic uncertainty relations, speed limit theorems, and many others. Many of them have been tested on a range of experimental platforms. This Perspective reviews recent developments in stochastic thermodynamics that extend its scope beyond its traditional domains, including systems with memory and hidden degrees of freedom, microscopic approaches to interacting and active matter, and geometric formulations based on optimal transport. Next, the Perspective surveys the challenges that arise when applying these ideas to macroscopic and complex systems, where the link between statistical irreversibility and thermodynamic dissipation becomes less direct. Finally, emerging applications in non-physical contexts are highlighted, including computation, biological systems, and social dynamics. Transcending the traditional boundaries of physics, these developments catalyze an unorthodox framework to tackle the thermodynamics of complex systems.

</details>

(b) For a system of charge-1 spinless fermions at ν = 1/2 a charge-2 boson can be made by Cooper pairing. To get a half-charged background anyon then requires taking a quarter of the Cooper pair. To find the flux (mod 4) emanating from a vertex subtract the value on an incoming arrow from the value on an outgo- ing arrow and take the result (mod 4). These are indicative of gaplessness, coming from a U(1)3 symmetry. The symmetry can be seen by partitioning the lattice into four sublattices, indicated by the different colors in the inset, and is discussed in the main text. Because of the geometry of the triangular lattice different layers of the transfer matrix must be shifted by half a lattice site relative to one another. (b) For ease of computation the rank-6 tensor T can be decomposed into two rank-4 tensors contracted to- gether, TA and TB. Thus by doubling the number of tensors we can return our transfer matrix to the square lattice form. In addition to this decomposition we must then add a shift to ensure TA is coupled to TB in the next layer.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 6</summary>

For L = 8, 12 the bond dimensions χ = 200 and 300 are found to agree, while there is a large disagreement in correlation length for L = 16. Going to larger bond dimen- sion χ = 400 at L = 16 appears to be closer to the ξ value obtained at χ = 300, and appears consistent with a saturating correlation length. Unfortunately it was not feasible to run further analysis for L = 20, where attempting χ = 400 took several months before timing out.</sub>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to construct a microscopic model for an electronic quantum charge liquid (QCL), a translation-symmetric phase intermediate between a Wigner-Mott insulator and a metal.

**Main result.** The authors identify a gapped state in a tetramer model that realizes minimal Z4 topological order and demonstrate that certain configurations are gapless due to emergent U(1) symmetries.

**Method.** The study uses tensor network methods, specifically representing wavefunctions as Projected Entangled Pair States (PEPS) and analyzing them via a transfer matrix approach on cylinders.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a microscopic model for an electronic quantum charge liquid using a tetramer model on a lattice. By mapping fermions to bosons, the authors investigate a state that preserves translation symmetry but possesses topological order. Using tensor network simulations, they identify a gapped phase with Z4 topological order and a gapless phase driven by emergent symmetries. The work also extends these findings to triangular lattices and suggests potential realizations in Rydberg atom systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The model consists of spinless fermions at filling nu=3/2 on a square or triangular lattice, which is mapped to a bosonic tetramer model (a generalization of the dimer model) at filling nu=3/4.

**Key observables.** Correlation length, spectral gap, Z4 topological order, and local Z4 flux conservation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The parameter theta (controlling tetramer configuration weights), cylinder circumference L, and bond dimension chi.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Numerical results are limited by finite system sizes (L) and finite bond dimensions (chi); the gap in the equal mixture state may be too small to resolve.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 explains QCLs and tetramer models; Fig 2 shows the RVB wavefunction and transfer matrix; Fig 3 details the transfer matrix tensor; Fig 4 compares correlation length scaling for straight vs. bent configurations; Fig 5/6 illustrate the transfer matrix construction and scaling on a triangular lattice.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QCL concept, defines the fermionic-to-bosonic mapping and the tetramer model, presents numerical results for square lattice configurations (gapless vs. gapped), extends the analysis to triangular lattices, and discusses implications for Rydberg atoms.

**Why it may be interesting.** The discovery of a microscopic model for a new type of topological order (Z4) and the connection to Rydberg atom constraints provides a potential platform for studying exotic many-body phases in programmable quantum simulators.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We provide a route to constructing an electronic quantum charge liquid (QCL), a state made up of fermions at fractional filling of a lattice that does not break translation. Starting with spinless fermions at filling $ν=3/2$ we pair them to get bosons at filling $ν=3/4$ per unit cell. The tetramer model, a generalization of the dimer model, on the square lattice is evaluated as a candidate bosonic QCL at filling $ν= 3/4$. It is shown that these models exhibit a local $\mathbb{Z}_4$ symmetry. Upon numerical study of a family of tetramer wavefunctions it is found that while one is gapless due to $\mathrm{U}(1)^3$ symmetry at least one other can be definitively shown to be gapped. The gapped nature of this state, along with its $\mathbb{Z}_4$ symmetry, leads us to propose that it is an example of the elusive bosonic QCL displaying the minimal $\mathbb{Z}_4$ topological order. We conclude by discussing possible extensions to other lattice geometries, electronic QCLs, and to Rydberg atoms.

</details>

**Main problem.** Determining the computational boundary between classically tractable and intractable fermionic systems when using non-Gaussian 'magic' inputs in free-fermion circuits.

**Main result.** The authors identify a tractable intermediate regime where essential simulation primitives for paired non-Gaussian states can be efficiently approximated to additive error using a mixed-Pfaffian reduction.

**Method.** An algebraic reduction technique that compresses multi-particle interference into a single coefficient of a multivariate Pfaffian polynomial, combined with a randomized Monte Carlo estimator using discrete Fourier filtering.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the challenge of simulating fermionic systems that use non-Gaussian 'magic' inputs, which are typically computationally hard. The authors demonstrate that for a specific class of paired-electron states, complex many-body dynamics can be efficiently simulated on classical computers using a new Pfaffian-based method. This approach allows for the efficient estimation of key observables like transition amplitudes and number correlators with additive error. The results establish a practical classical benchmark for recent trapped-ion quantum simulation experiments and help define the limits of quantum advantage in fermionic systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Fermionic systems involving number-preserving Gaussian evolution (passive FLO) acting on structured non-Gaussian inputs, specifically block-product paired states and Antisymmetric Pair-Slot Gaussian (APSG) states.

**Key observables.** Transition amplitudes, overlaps, arbitrary-weight number correlators, transition reduced density matrices (RDMs), and high-weight Wilson-loop observables.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Additive error epsilon, failure probability delta, number of measurement shots K, and the particle-number-preserving nature of the evolution.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The efficiency is proven for a specific class of paired non-Gaussian states; the results focus on additive-error estimation rather than exact calculation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the workflow from magic inputs through FLO to Pfaffian reduction; Figure 2 illustrates the sampling process; Figure 3 visualizes the mixed-Pfaffian extraction as a colored perfect matching problem; Figure 5 shows RMSE scaling.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of non-Gaussian complexity, presents the mixed-Pfaffian algebraic reduction, proves theorems for transition amplitudes and overlaps, extends the framework to quantum chemistry subroutines and non-unitary maps, and provides benchmarks for trapped-ion experiments.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous classical benchmark for near-term fermionic quantum simulators and clarifies the precise boundary where quantum advantage becomes necessary in many-body dynamics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Establishing the precise computational boundary between classically tractable fermionic systems and those capable of genuine quantum advantage is a central challenge in quantum simulation. While injecting non-Gaussian ``magic" inputs into free-fermion circuits is widely expected to generate intractable complexity, we identify a physically motivated intermediate regime. Supported by rigorous bounds and numerical evidence, we show that for a class of paired non-Gaussian fermionic states, essential quantum simulation primitives -- transition amplitudes, overlaps, and arbitrary-weight number correlators -- can be efficiently approximated to additive error under free-fermionic dynamics. This tractability stems from an algebraic reduction that compresses exponentially large multiparticle interference into a single coefficient of a multivariate Pfaffian polynomial. Because these classical estimators match the intrinsic $O(1/\sqrt{K})$ statistical uncertainty of quantum hardware utilizing $K$ measurement shots, they constitute a practical benchmark. Building on this foundation, we construct an additive-error estimator for high-weight Wilson observables in the noninteracting quench of recent trapped-ion experiments, providing a rigorous classical benchmark. Extending this to quantum chemistry, we demonstrate that core overlap-based subroutines for antisymmetrized products of strongly orthogonal geminals admit exact Pfaffian reductions. Ultimately, these results sharpen the boundary of quantum advantage, establishing that the paired-electron scaffold is effectively dequantized and clarifying exactly where quantum resources are indispensable.

</details>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Main problem.** Investigating how long-range shortcuts and stochastic resetting affect transport efficiency, specifically the non-monotonic behavior of Mean First-Passage Times (MFPT) in ring networks.

**Main result.** The study reveals that shortcuts do not monotonically enhance transport; instead, they create a complex, self-similar, and fractal-like MFPT landscape with local maxima and minima driven by commensurability. Stochastic resetting further amplifies these oscillations and induces non-uniform stationary distributions.

**Method.** Analytical derivation using spectral properties of the transition matrix and Fourier analysis, supplemented by numerical Monte Carlo simulations and box-counting analysis to determine fractal dimensions.

**Summary.** This paper explores transport in ring networks containing long-range shortcuts and stochastic resetting. It challenges the assumption that shortcuts always improve transport, demonstrating instead that certain shortcut lengths can significantly hinder it through a complex, self-similar pattern of maxima and minima. The researchers identify distinct scaling regimes and show that stochastic resetting amplifies these transport anomalies. The findings highlight how the spatial organization of connections is critical for determining transport efficiency.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A discrete-time random walk on a periodic ring network of N nodes with degree k=4, where nodes are connected to nearest neighbors and to nodes at a fixed shortcut distance r, subject to stochastic resetting to a specific node.

**Key observables.** Mean First-Passage Time (MFPT), Mean Squared Displacement (MSD), stationary occupation probability distribution, and spectral gap.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Ring size (N), shortcut length (r), transition probabilities (p, q), resetting probability (p_sr), and the bias ratio (q/p).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The network is homogeneous and undirected, and the shortcut length r is fixed for all nodes.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows network topologies for different r; Figure 3 shows scaling boundaries for r/N; Figure 5 illustrates non-uniformity of stationary distributions; Figure 6 shows MFPT oscillations under resetting; Figure 7 shows the fractal structure of MFPT extrema; Figure 8 shows power-law scaling in different regimes; Figure 9 shows the effect of the bias ratio q/p.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the model and topology, provides analytical derivations for transport observables using spectral methods, analyzes scaling regimes and the fractal nature of the MFPT landscape, investigates the impact of stochastic resetting, and concludes with an analysis of the bias between local and long-range jumps.

**Why it may be interesting.** The discovery of a fractal-like transport landscape and the interplay between spatial periodicity and long-range jumps is highly relevant to understanding transport in open quantum systems and non-equilibrium dynamics in complex networks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The transport properties of discrete-time random walks on ring networks with deterministic shortcuts are investigated through analytical and numerical methods. The network consists of a periodic chain where each node is connected to its nearest neighbors and to nodes located at a fixed distance $r$. Using the spectral properties of the transition matrix, we derive explicit expressions for the occupation probabilities and mean first-passage times (MFPTs). Contrary to the common expectation that shortcuts monotonically enhance transport, we find that the MFPT between distant nodes develops a highly non-monotonic dependence on the shortcut length. Beyond a threshold value, the MFPT landscape exhibits a hierarchy of maxima and minima organized in a self-similar pattern associated with commensurability relations between the shortcut length and the system size. The scaling behavior of these extrema reveals regimes where transport efficiency is either strongly enhanced or suppressed. We further analyze the mean squared displacement and the influence of stochastic resetting, showing that resetting amplifies the oscillatory MFPT structure and induces strongly nonuniform stationary distributions. These results demonstrate that the spatial organization of long-range connections plays a crucial role in determining transport efficiency in networks.

</details>

### [Continuous Noise Model for Quantum Circuits](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26008v1)

**Authors:** Yunos El Kaderi, Andreas Honecker, Iryna Andriyanova  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26008v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the gap between standard discrete Pauli noise models and the continuous, structured coherent errors (like control drift) found in real hardware, specifically investigating how these errors accumulate and impact quantum error correction.

**Main result.** The authors find that continuous coherent noise can degrade logical performance more severely than Pauli noise in stabilizer codes, though the opposite is true for Grover's search. They also develop an efficient analytical method for tracking error distributions in Clifford circuits.

**Method.** The study uses Monte Carlo sampling of a von Mises-Fisher noise model and compares it to a Pauli model using an entropy-matched scheme. An approximate analytical method is also developed to propagate error distributions through Clifford gates without full sampling.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a continuous noise model using the von Mises-Fisher distribution to better represent coherent gate errors in quantum computing. By comparing this model to standard Pauli noise through an entropy-matching scheme, the authors demonstrate that coherent noise can be more damaging to error-correcting codes. They also present a computationally efficient analytical method to track error propagation through Clifford circuits. The work highlights the limitations of simplified noise models in predicting the performance of complex quantum error-correction protocols.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of quantum circuits including [[5,1,3]] and [[7,1,3]] stabilizer codes, Grover's search algorithm, and random Clifford circuits. The noise is modeled as continuous, small-angle random rotations on the Bloch sphere using a von Mises-Fisher distribution.

**Key observables.** Logical error rates, fidelity, error probability (1 - P_success), and binary entropy at readout.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Rotation angle (theta), rotation axis (mu), concentration parameter (k), noise strength (sigma), and circuit depth (m).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes small-angle, isotropic Gaussian errors in the limit; single-qubit gates are the primary source of noise; CNOT gates are noiseless but propagate errors; and the analytical approximation ignores multi-qubit error correlations.

**Figures summary.** Figures show QEC circuit workflows, logical error probabilities for [[5,1,3]] and [[7,1,3]] codes comparing Pauli vs. continuous noise, Grover search performance scaling, and heatmaps validating the analytical approximation's accuracy in Clifford circuits.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the inadequacy of Pauli models, introduces the von Mises-Fisher continuous noise model, establishes a matching scheme for fair comparison, presents an efficient analytical propagation method, and validates this method against brute-force simulations.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides a more realistic framework for modeling how continuous environmental coupling and control imperfections accumulate and degrade quantum information.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum noise is a central challenge in quantum computing across many applications. Extensive work has examined how qubits couple to their environment, leading to decoherence and relaxation, which is irreversible. Current studies focus on coherent gate errors caused by control misalignment, which accumulate with circuit depth but can, in principle, be corrected. This work studies a continuous coherent noise model for quantum circuits and compares it with a discrete Pauli model. The focus is on small coherent gate errors that build up across circuit depth. These errors are modeled as random rotations on the Bloch sphere using a von Mises-Fisher distribution. In the small-angle limit, the model reduces to an isotropic Gaussian distribution. We test the model on quantum error-correction circuits based on the [[5,1,3]] and [[7,1,3]] codes. A variant of Grover's search circuit with different qubit counts is also examined. To enable fair comparison, we introduce a model-independent matching scheme. Pauli and continuous noise channels are aligned using the binary entropy at readout. This isolates the effect of noise structure at fixed uncertainty. An approximate analytical method for coherent error propagation is also developed. The method tracks error distributions through Clifford circuits without full Monte Carlo sampling. It reduces simulation cost while preserving accuracy for circuit-level error estimates. The approximation is validated against brute-force simulations, identifying its regime of validity with Clifford circuits and limits under error correction. Our results show that continuous coherent noise can degrade logical performance more strongly than Pauli noise. They also clarify when simplified propagation models succeed and when they break down.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26871"></a>
### [Ballistic Exciton Flow Driven by Intertwined Exciton-Electron Orders in a Moiré Superlattice](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26871v1)

**Authors:** Shibin Deng, Jonas M. Peterson, Jonas Reimann, Heonjoon Park, Ammon Fischer, Takashi Taniguchi, Kenji Watanabe, Xiaodong Xu, Dante M. Kennes, Libai Huang  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26871v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **2/5** · `quantum optics experiment` **2/5**

**Main problem.** Understanding how the interplay between bosonic excitons and correlated electron lattices in TMD moiré superlattices drives collective transport and phase transitions.

**Main result.** Strong exciton-electron and exciton-exciton repulsions enable energy-selective ballistic exciton transport, which is enhanced at fractional electron fillings associated with generalized Wigner-crystal orders.

**Method.** Ultrafast, time- and energy-resolved photoluminescence (PL) and reflectance microscopy combined with 1D Bose-Fermi Hubbard model simulations using DMRG.

**Summary.** This paper investigates the collective transport of excitons in TMD moiré superlattices. The researchers show that repulsive interactions between excitons and electrons drive a ballistic expansion of excitons, particularly when electrons form Wigner-crystal orders. Using both ultrafast microscopy and DMRG simulations, they demonstrate how the electron crystal can accelerate the melting of the exciton Mott background. This work establishes moiré materials as a versatile platform for studying strongly interacting Bose-Fermi mixtures.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A WSe2/WS2 heterobilayer moiré superlattice acting as a strongly interacting Bose-Fermi mixture where bosonic excitons coexist with correlated electron lattices.

**Key observables.** Exciton mean-squared displacement (MSD), time-dependent photoluminescence, and reflectance microscopy signals.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fractional electron fillings, exciton-electron repulsion, exciton-exciton repulsion, and intersite hopping rates.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The 1D Bose-Fermi Hubbard model qualitatively captures the complex 2D moiré physics.

**Paper structure.** The paper presents experimental observations of ballistic exciton transport via microscopy, analyzes the time-dependent MSD, relates the transport to electron filling and Wigner-crystal formation, and validates these findings with DMRG simulations of a Bose-Fermi Hubbard model.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates real-time control of intertwined bosonic and electronic orders and provides a platform for studying the transition between exciton insulator and fluid phases in a highly tunable many-body system.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Moiré superlattices of transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) host strongly interacting Bose-Fermi mixtures in which bosonic excitons coexist with correlated electron lattices. Using ultrafast, time- and energy-resolved photoluminescence (PL) and reflectance microscopy, we show that strong exciton-electron and exciton-exciton repulsion can enable collective ballistic exciton transport in a WSe$_2$/WS$_2$ heterobilayer. The ballistic transport is energy-selective: repulsive interactions drive excitons into a higher moiré exciton band, where enhanced intersite hopping enables rapid spatial expansion. Correspondingly, the exciton mean-squared displacement (MSD) exhibits a quadratic time dependence ($\propto t^2$). This ballistic expansion is enhanced at fractional electron fillings where the electrons form generalized Wigner-crystal (GWC) orders. Afterwards, the system transitions into a mixed electron-exciton Mott state as Auger recombination and density depletion conclude the ballistic expansion. A one-dimensional Bose-Fermi Hubbard model solved using density-matrix renormalization group (DMRG) qualitatively reproduces the measured exciton transport and time-dependent response. It further confirms that strong cross-species interactions allow the electron crystal to perforate the exciton Mott background, accelerating its melting and enhancing exciton motion. Our results establish moiré TMDs as highly tunable platforms for realizing strongly interacting Bose-Fermi mixtures, which we employ here to demonstrate real-time control of intertwined bosonic and electronic order and to establish a route to the exciton insulator-fluid transition.

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating the quantum Shannon theory of bosonic fading channels, specifically determining their communication capacities and structural properties when transmissivity fluctuates stochastically.

**Main result.** Proved that non-Gaussian Fock-diagonal states strictly outperform Gaussian thermal states for entanglement-assisted classical capacity and can 'activate' the channel for quantum communication in regimes where Gaussian states yield zero capacity.

**Method.** Analytical proofs using Stinespring dilation and Choi matrix rank, alongside a numerical iterative variational algorithm to optimize photon number distributions.

**Summary.** This paper explores the information-theoretic properties of bosonic channels subject to transmissivity fluctuations, known as fading channels. The authors show that these channels are fundamentally non-Gaussian and that standard Gaussian (thermal) states are sub-optimal for communication. Crucially, they prove that optimized non-Gaussian states can enable quantum communication in high-loss regimes where Gaussian encodings fail entirely. This 'channel activation' is particularly relevant for practical applications like satellite-to-ground quantum networks subject to atmospheric turbulence.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A bosonic fading channel modeled as a convex combination (mixture) of pure-loss channels, representing physical scenarios like atmospheric turbulence in free-space quantum communication.

**Key observables.** Coherent information, entanglement-assisted classical capacity, quantum mutual information, and secret key capacity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Transmissivity (lambda), probability density of fluctuations (p(lambda)), mean energy constraint (E), and LNW distribution parameters (R and gamma).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The optimization is restricted to phase-insensitive (Fock-diagonal) input states, and the model assumes negligible thermal background noise.

**Figures summary.** Circuit representations of the fading channel, parameter space plots for non-antidegradability, and maps showing the capacity gain and channel activation of non-Gaussian states over thermal benchmarks.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the fading channel model, establishes its structural properties (non-degradability/distillability), analyzes specific fading distributions (binary and LNW), demonstrates the sub-optimality of Gaussian states, and presents a variational algorithm for capacity optimization.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides fundamental insights into the limits of quantum communication in realistic, fluctuating environments and demonstrates how non-Gaussianity can be leveraged to overcome noise-induced capacity vanishing.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The pure-loss channel is a fundamental model for describing noise in bosonic quantum platforms. It is characterised by a single parameter, the transmissivity, which quantifies the fraction of the input energy that reaches the output of the channel. In realistic scenarios, however, such as free-space quantum communication, the transmissivity is not fixed but fluctuates from one channel use to another. In this setting, the overall channel is effectively described as a convex combination of pure-loss channels, known as a fading channel. Despite its practical relevance, the quantum Shannon theory of the fading channel has remained largely unexplored. Here, we address this gap, specifically investigating degradability, anti-degradability, entanglement breakingness, and capacities of the fading channel. Of particular relevance to practical quantum-internet applications, we prove that entanglement distribution and quantum key distribution can always be achieved at a strictly positive rate over any fading channel, no matter how noisy it is or how strongly the transmissivity fluctuates, provided the channel is not completely noisy. Moreover, we prove that thermal states, which are optimal for a broad class of static bosonic Gaussian channels, fail to achieve the entanglement-assisted classical capacity of fading channels: non-Gaussian Fock-diagonal states strictly outperform all Gaussian encodings. Most strikingly, we identify regimes where the coherent information of thermal inputs vanishes, while optimized non-Gaussian states achieve strictly positive values, thereby activating the channel for quantum communication. For a paradigmatic binary fading model we establish this result analytically, deriving the exact capacity-achieving state in closed form. For general fading distributions, we design an iterative variational algorithm to optimize the coherent and mutual information.

</details>

**Authors:** Tomasz Śmierzchalski  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26534v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Here, the configuration of each spin is represented by a blue arrow pointing upwards (+1) or a red arrow pointing downwards (−1). Additionally, hi ∈R is the external field acting on the i−th spin and Jij denotes coupling strength between spins i and j.</sub>

<img src="2604.26534_figures/2604.26534_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1.2: Schematic representation of parallel annealing algorithm. The analog spin variables x are projected onto the binary domain using the sign function. Thus, the obtained real spin configuration s is used to calculate the gradient and update the analog variables. This process continues until a specified number of steps is reached.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26534_figures/2604.26534_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1.3: Time to obtain ground state for each instance. Due to the small size of the instances, we were able to certify the ground state using exhaustive search. For brute-force, we used GPU acceleration [46], as CPU-only time was two orders of magnitude higher (around 2 hours). With the rest of the solver, we used CPU-only versions. We used the time-to-solution metric (Eq. 4.1), which accounts for the probability of obtaining the ground state in the given sample. Each heuristic solver was run with the same number of steps and sample size.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26534_figures/2604.26534_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2.2: Example of schedule functions A(s) and B(s) for a representative D-Wave QPU (values shown as frequencies in the manufacturer’s convention). The shaded mid-anneal window indicates the region where the driver and problem energy scales compete most strongly (A(s) ≈B(s)), often coinciding with an increased probability of diabatic excitations [16].</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.26534_figures/2604.26534_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 6</summary>

<img src="2604.26534_figures/2604.26534_fig6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2.4: Example minor embedding of the complete graph K3 onto a Chimera unit cell. Here, vertex A is mapped to the chain KA consisting of qubits 1 and 4, while B and C are mapped to single qubits (3 and 5). <details><summary>📷 Fig 7</summary>

<img src="2604.26534_figures/2604.26534_fig7.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3.1: Branch-and-bound search in probability space. The solution is built sequentially by assigning spins. For each partial assignment s1:k (green), the algorithm branches by appending sk+1 ∈{−1, +1} and evaluating the corresponding conditional probabilities p(sk+1 | s1:k) (equivalently, the marginals p(s1:k+1)). The bounding step retains at most M partial assignments with the largest probabilities (green) and discards the remainder (red). The figure shows M = 3 for clarity. In SpinGlassPEPS.jl, the required marginals are obtained from approximate tensor-network contractions.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 8</summary>

<img src="2604.26534_figures/2604.26534_fig8.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3.2: Execution flow of SpinGlassPEPS.jl. (a) An Ising instance on the native interaction graph is transformed into (b) a local Potts Hamiltonian on a king’s graph via clustering. (c) The resulting (Potts) partition function is represented as a PEPS tensor network on a square lattice. (d) Conditional marginals required by the search are computed by approximately contracting the PEPS. (e) A branch-and-bound sweep yields a candidate configuration, which corresponds to a ground-state configuration of the original Ising model. (f) The same machinery can be used to find localized low-energy excitations around the returned configuration.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 9</summary>

<img src="2604.26534_figures/2604.26534_fig9.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3.3: Transforming hardware graphs into Potts clusters. Black dots denote physical spins, and colored edges denote couplers: orange for intra-row/column links and blue for diagonal and inter-cell links (schematic). Each circled block indicates a cluster, which is replaced by a single Potts variable xn whose state enumerates the cluster’s binary microstates.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 10</summary>

<img src="2604.26534_figures/2604.26534_fig10.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3.4: Edge-specific projections for compressing Potts pair energies. A naive repre- sentation of Exmxn scales as dm × dn with dn = 2kn (up to 224 for Pegasus and 216 for Zephyr in the maximal case). Since only boundary spins couple across a given edge (m, n), we project each cluster microstate xm to a reduced index ¯xm = P mn(xm) that retains only the boundary-spin subconfiguration relevant to that edge. The pair term is then stored as a compressed matrix ¯E¯xm¯xn (Eq. (3.5)). Panel (a) shows the schematic construction of edge-specific projected indices and the resulting compressed pair matrix ¯E¯xm¯xn. Panels (b) and (c) illustrate typical reductions in local dimensions obtained in...</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The need for a reliable benchmarking framework for quantum annealers that evaluates algorithmic performance, sampling diversity, and thermodynamic efficiency beyond simple energy minimization.

**Main result.** Developed SpinGlassPEPS.jl, a topology-aware tensor network heuristic, and demonstrated that specific annealing pauses can improve solution quality while reducing thermodynamic cost.

**Method.** Combines tensor network heuristics (PEPS) for optimization, reinforcement learning for error-correcting post-processing, and a thermodynamic framework treating annealers as thermal machines.

**Summary.** This work presents a comprehensive framework for benchmarking D-Wave quantum annealers. It introduces a new tensor-network-based heuristic, SpinGlassPEPS.jl, to provide physically interpretable references for optimization. The research also treats quantum annealers as thermal machines to study the relationship between annealing schedules and thermodynamic cost. Additionally, it uses reinforcement learning to post-process samples and correct hardware errors, ultimately arguing for a multi-faceted approach to evaluating quantum hardware performance.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on D-Wave quantum annealers (Pegasus and Zephyr topologies) using the Ising/QUBO model and the Transverse Field Ising Model (TFIM) Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Success probability, energy accuracy, low-energy diversity, energy dissipation, entropy production, and effective temperature.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Annealing schedules (including pauses), inverse temperature, bond dimension in tensor networks, and hardware-specific parameters like qubit temperature and coupling strengths.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Tensor network accuracy is limited by approximate contractions in large-scale instances; simulations primarily focus on closed-system unitary dynamics despite annealers being open systems.

**Figures summary.** Includes symbolic representations of Ising spin glasses, diagrammatic notation for tensor contractions, visual comparisons of contraction sequences, and schematics of the reinforcement learning loop.

**Paper structure.** The dissertation introduces a benchmarking framework, presents a tensor-network-based heuristic (SpinGlassPEPS.jl), analyzes the thermodynamics of annealing schedules, implements RL-based error mitigation, and provides mathematical foundations for tensor networks and RL.

**Why it may be interesting.** It bridges the gap between many-body physics (tensor networks, spin glasses) and quantum hardware performance, offering insights into how environmental coupling and thermal dynamics affect computation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum annealing targets low-energy solutions of Ising/QUBO problems, but reliable assessment requires more than best-energy comparisons. This dissertation develops a benchmarking framework for D-Wave quantum annealers that combines strong classical baselines, sampling and diversity metrics, and thermodynamic cost.   Its first contribution, SpinGlassPEPS.jl, is a topology-aware tensor-network heuristic for optimization and sampling on Pegasus/Zephyr-like graphs. It maps Ising instances to local Potts clusters, represents the partition function with PEPS, and performs branch-and-bound search in probability space. Benchmarks show that it is a physically interpretable reference solver, though approximate contractions limit its competitiveness on the largest instances.   The second contribution treats quantum annealers as effective thermal machines, relating success probability and solution quality to dissipation, entropy production, and effective temperature. Carefully placed pauses can improve performance while reducing thermodynamic cost, although longitudinal fields may become harmful in paused schedules.   The thesis also introduces reinforcement-learning post-processing to improve returned samples and exact small-system simulations to probe annealing dynamics. Overall, it argues for quantum-annealing benchmarks that jointly measure algorithmic performance and physical expenditure.

</details>

### [Rethinking Nonlocality: Locality, Counterfactuals, and the EPR-Bell Argument](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26916v1)

**Authors:** Partha Ghose  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26916v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `quantum measurements` **3/5**

<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 6</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The paper re-examines whether violations of Bell inequalities necessarily imply nonlocal causation or if they can be interpreted as evidence of contextuality.

**Main result.** The author demonstrates that Bell violations do not logically mandate nonlocality, but rather signal the failure of global value assignment (contextuality) across incompatible measurement contexts.

**Method.** The study employs a logical deconstruction of the EPR-Bell argument, a historical re-analysis of Einstein's formulations, and modern sheaf-theoretic approaches to model measurement contexts.

**Summary.** This paper challenges the standard conclusion that Bell inequality violations prove the nonlocality of nature. The author argues that these violations actually demonstrate contextuality—the impossibility of assigning definite values to all possible measurements simultaneously. By focusing on the role of counterfactual reasoning, the work suggests that locality can be preserved if one accepts that physical quantities are context-dependent. This interpretation aligns with modern mathematical approaches to quantum contextuality.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The analysis focuses on entangled quantum systems and the framework of the EPR setup and CHSH-type experiments involving spatially separated, non-interacting systems.

**Key observables.** Non-commuting observables (A, A', B, B') and the existence of a joint probability distribution for all observables.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The paper challenges the assumption of global value assignment (counterfactual definiteness) and evaluates the tension between locality, measurement independence, and contextuality.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with a re-evaluation of the EPR argument, moves into a logical deconstruction of the assumptions in Bell's theorem, applies sheaf-theoretic frameworks to define contextuality, and concludes by contrasting Einstein's and Bohr's interpretations.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum information and foundations, as it provides a structural, sheaf-theoretic alternative to the standard interpretation of nonlocality, potentially reframing how we understand entanglement and contextuality.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The widespread claim that violations of Bell inequalities establish the nonlocality of nature is critically reexamined. It is argued that this conclusion is not logically compelled by either the Einstein--Podolsky--Rosen (EPR) argument or Bell's theorem. The analysis highlights the central role of counterfactual reasoning -- the assumption that outcomes of unperformed measurements possess definite values -- in deriving Bell inequalities. It is shown that these inequalities follow not from locality alone, but from the conjunction of locality with a global assignment of values across incompatible measurement contexts. Their experimental violation therefore signals the impossibility of such a global assignment, i.e.\ contextuality, rather than necessarily implying nonlocal causation. This interpretation aligns with Bohr's emphasis on the contextual character of physical quantities and is naturally formulated within modern sheaf-theoretic approaches to contextuality.

</details>

### [Amplitude Encoding of Slater-Type Orbitals via Matrix Product States: Efficient State Preparation and Integral Evaluation on Quantum Hardware](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26314v1)

**Authors:** Sorin Bolos  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26314v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `QC/QI experiment` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

**Main problem.** The challenge of efficiently encoding and preparing physically accurate Slater-Type Orbitals (STOs) on quantum hardware to enable exact evaluation of quantum chemistry integrals without the exponential cost of generic state preparation.

**Main result.** The study establishes that 3D Cartesian amplitude encoding of STOs has bounded complexity rather than exponential scaling, with the MPS bond dimension saturating at approximately 138. It also demonstrates successful 1D integral pipeline validation on IBM Heron processors.

**Method.** The authors use Matrix Product States (MPS) for amplitude encoding and derive analytical constructions for 1D orbitals. They employ SVD truncation to manage bond dimension and use Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE) for hardware error mitigation.

**Summary.** This paper presents a method for encoding Slater-Type Orbitals (STOs) on quantum computers using Matrix Product States (MPS). By using amplitude encoding, the authors show that the complexity of representing 3D orbitals does not grow exponentially but instead reaches a bounded bond dimension. They successfully demonstrate a 1D integral evaluation pipeline on IBM Heron hardware and prove that SVD truncation can significantly reduce circuit complexity without losing accuracy. This establishes a viable path for performing exact quantum chemistry calculations on near-term and fault-tolerant quantum hardware.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on hydrogen-like 1s and 2s orbitals in 1D, 3D spherical, and 3D Cartesian coordinate systems, validated on IBM Heron-generation quantum processors.

**Key observables.** Overlap integrals, kinetic energy integrals, and nuclear attraction integrals.

**Important parameters / regimes.** MPS bond dimension (chi), SVD truncation threshold, qubit count (n), and decay constant (zeta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The efficiency of Cartesian encoding may depend on orbital localization; current hardware noise levels currently limit the feasibility of large-scale 3D Cartesian and nuclear attraction circuits.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows discretization convergence for 1D integrals; Figure 2 shows 3D overlap convergence; Figure 3 plots bond dimension saturation and sensitivity to decay constants; Figure 4 illustrates the bond dimension profile across the MPS chain; Figure 5 and 6 show hardware validation and error scaling on IBM processors.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Gaussian vs. Slater trade-off, presents analytical 1D MPS constructions, extends the framework to 3D Cartesian and spherical coordinates, analyzes entanglement scaling and bond dimension saturation, validates results on IBM hardware, and discusses limitations regarding two-electron integrals and hardware noise.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant as it provides a scalable, tensor-network-based framework for mapping continuous molecular wavefunctions onto qubits, potentially enabling high-precision quantum chemistry simulations that bypass the systematic errors of Gaussian approximations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Slater-type orbitals (STOs) provide the physically correct description of atomic wavefunctions but have been largely replaced by Gaussian-type orbitals in computational chemistry due to the lack of closed-form multi-center integrals. We present a systematic study of amplitude encoding of STOs on quantum computers using matrix product states (MPS). For one-dimensional orbital functions of the form $p_d(x) e^{-ζx}$, we derive analytical MPS constructions with constant bond dimension $χ= d + 1$, requiring $O(n)$ classical and quantum resources for $n$-qubit registers with no grid sampling. We demonstrate a complete one-electron integral pipeline -- overlap, kinetic energy, and nuclear attraction -- in one dimension, validating the overlap and kinetic energy on IBM Heron processors at 5~qubits with 0.67\% hardware-induced error using Zero-Noise Extrapolation. In three dimensions, we compute multi-center overlap integrals between 1s and 2s orbitals in Cartesian coordinates with 0.02\% discretization error at 18~qubits. A systematic entanglement analysis reveals that the MPS bond dimension of three-dimensional STOs in Cartesian coordinates saturates with increasing grid resolution -- reaching $\sim$138 for the hydrogen 1s orbital at 12~qubits per coordinate -- establishing bounded encoding complexity rather than the exponential scaling initially expected. The SVD truncation threshold provides a practical resource parameter, reducing the bond dimension to 39 at threshold $10^{-6}$ with negligible accuracy loss. These results map the entanglement landscape for amplitude encoding of atomic orbitals and establish MPS-based state preparation as a viable path toward exact STO basis sets on quantum computers.

</details>
