**Main problem.** The study addresses the lack of a rigorous evaluation regarding the accuracy, generalization, and robustness of various Quantum Neural Network (QNN) architectures against both adversarial attacks and quantum noise in NISQ environments.

**Main result.** The researchers found a fundamental trade-off between accuracy and robustness: QViT achieves high accuracy but is highly vulnerable to adversarial attacks, whereas QRNN and QCNN demonstrate superior resilience to perturbations and quantum noise.

**Method.** A comparative analysis of three hybrid classical-quantum architectures (QCNN, QRNN, and QViT) using MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets, subjected to adversarial attacks (APGD, PGD, etc.) and various quantum noise models (depolarizing, amplitude damping, etc.).

**Summary.** This paper provides a comprehensive benchmark of three prominent quantum neural network architectures. It reveals that while transformer-based models (QViT) excel in accuracy on simple tasks, they are extremely fragile to noise and adversarial attacks. In contrast, recurrent and convolutional quantum models offer much better stability in noisy, intermediate-scale quantum environments. This work is crucial for guiding the selection of quantum machine learning models for practical, noisy hardware.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Hybrid Classical-Quantum Neural Networks (HCQNNs) including Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNN) based on MERA-like structures, Quantum Recurrent Neural and Quantum Vision Transformers (QViT) utilizing quantum self-attention cores.

**Key observables.** Classification accuracy, generalization error, Lipschitz constant (to measure sensitivity), average fidelity, and adversarial success rate (ASR).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Feature dimensionality (low vs. high), perturbation intensity (epsilon), noise types (bit-flip, phase-flip, amplitude damping, depolarizing), and number of training samples.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the use of classical optimizers (Adam) to mitigate the barren plateau problem and is limited to a 15-qubit simulation environment.

**Figures summary.** Schematics of the three architectures; comparison of adversarial success rates across different attack methods; plots of accuracy and generalization error versus training set size; and the impact of various quantum noise channels on model performance.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QML landscape, details the specific architectures (QCNN, QRNN, QViT), describes the experimental setup for noise and adversarial testing, presents comparative results on accuracy and robustness, and concludes with limitations and future directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum Machine Learning (QML) has recently emerged as a highly promising research frontier. Within this domain, Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs),characterized by Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs) at their core and featuring layers of quantum gates optimized by classical algorithms, have garnered significant attention. However, a rigorous and exhaustive evaluation of their practical performance remains largely incomplete. In this study, we conduct a comprehensive comparative analysis of three prominent hybrid classical-quantum architectures: Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNN), Quantum Recurrent Neural Networks (QRNN), and Quantum Vision Transformers (QViT), focusing on the critical dimensions of generalization, accuracy, and robustness. Our findings provide novel insights that address previous evaluative gaps. Notably, while these models exhibit exceptional performance on low-feature datasets such as MNIST, their learning efficacy degrades significantly when transitioned to high-feature datasets. Furthermore, convolutional-based models like QCNN appear less effective on high-dimensional data than other machine learning architectures. Additionally, while all models are susceptible to adversarial noise, traditional architectures, such as recurrent and convolutional networks, demonstrate superior resilience. Conversely, in the presence of quantum noise, the transformer-based architecture proves its strength by maintaining high robustness against measurement noise, channel noise, and finite-shot effects, whereas other architectures suffer marked performance declines. These results provide a granular perspective on the current state of the field and underscore the critical importance of tailoring model selection to the constraints of contemporary Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) environments.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26430"></a>
### [A Multi-Level Integrity Evaluation Framework for Quantum Circuits under Controlled Anomaly Injection](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26430v1)

**Authors:** Ejaz Ahmed, Boshuai Ye, Syed Hamza Shah, Muhammad Azeem Akbar, Arif Ali Khan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26430v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26430_figures/2604.26430_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1: Multi-layer quantum circuit integrity framework using SIS, IGS, and OIS. Black lines show the reference circuit; red lines indicate anomalies.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26430_figures/2604.26430_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26430_figures/2604.26430_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 6 compares the runtime of IGS and OIS across qubit counts for different severity levels. IGS maintains consistently low and stable runtime across all configurations, with only a modest increase as the number of qubits grows and minimal variance across severity levels. In contrast, OIS exhibits substantially higher runtime and significantly greater variability, with both mean runtime and variance increasing sharply beyond 10 qubits. Notably, OIS runtime becomes increasingly unstable at higher qubit counts, with large error bars indicating sensitivity to circuit structure and execution conditions. This highlights the computational advantage of IGS as a low-runtime pre- execution metric...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26430_figures/2604.26430_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3: IGS response across defined anomalies.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.26430_figures/2604.26430_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4: SIS remains high for structure-preserving anomalies, while IGS and OIS degrade with increasing severity, capturing interaction level and behavioral deviations.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 6</summary>

<img src="2604.26430_figures/2604.26430_fig6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 5: IGS and OIS exhibit weak correlation across severity levels, confirming that interaction-level similarity does not reliably reflect behavioral equivalence.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 7</summary>

<img src="2604.26430_figures/2604.26430_fig7.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 6: IGS achieves stable and low runtime across qubit counts, while OIS incurs significantly higher and more variable computational cost.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Existing quantum circuit validation methods are fragmented, failing to detect 'structural blind spots' where circuit transformations preserve global properties like gate count but alter computational behavior.

**Main result.** A multi-level framework (SIS, OIS, IGS) is necessary because structural similarity does not guarantee behavioral equivalence; specifically, OIS can detect 93.85% of anomalies that structural metrics miss.

**Method.** The authors propose a three-layer metric framework combining Structural Integrity Score (SIS), Operational Integrity Score (OIS) via Jensen-Shannon distance, and Interaction Graph Score (IGS) using graph-based representations, tested via controlled anomaly injection.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the challenge of ensuring quantum circuit integrity in the NISQ era, particularly against accidental or adversarial modifications. It identifies that structural analysis alone is insufficient because certain gate changes do not alter global circuit descriptors. By proposing a multi-layered approach that integrates structural, interaction-level, and behavioral metrics, the authors provide a more robust way to detect hidden circuit deviations. The framework offers a trade-off between the high accuracy of execution-based methods and the computational efficiency of graph-based pre-execution analysis.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study uses quantum circuits from the QASMBench suite, modeled as Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs), and simulated using the Qiskit Aer backend.

**Key observables.** Gate count, circuit depth, interaction topology, output probability distributions, and Jensen-Shannon distance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Anomaly severity levels (0.1, 0.3, 0.6) and circuit scales ranging from small to 40 qubits.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes an idealized environment without hardware noise or physical device variability and uses a predefined taxonomy of perturbations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the framework workflow; Figure 2 shows SIS and OIS performance across anomaly types; Figure 3 shows IGS degradation; Figure 4 shows the divergence between metrics; Figure 6 compares the computational runtime of IGS versus OIS.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of circuit integrity, proposes a three-layer metric framework (SIS, OIS, IGS), details the anomaly injection methodology, presents experimental results on detection performance and scalability, and concludes with limitations and future directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Ensuring the integrity of quantum circuits is a significant challenge in the Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) era, where circuits are subject to compilation transformations, hardware constraints, and potential adversarial modifications. Existing validation approaches typically rely on either structural analysis or behavioral evaluation, leading to incomplete assessment of circuit correctness.   In this work, we investigate the relationship between structural, interaction-level, and behavioral perspectives of circuit integrity, demonstrating that a single aspect of integrity is insufficient to guarantee circuit integrity; structural similarity alone does not ensure behavioral equivalence. To address this problem, we use a three-layer metric framework that combines the Structural Integrity Score (SIS), the Operational Integrity Score (OIS), and the Interaction Graph Semantic-Logical Score (IGS). SIS captures global structural properties, OIS quantifies behavioral divergence using Jensen-Shannon distance, and IGS models interaction patterns and dependencies in a pre-execution setting.   Through controlled anomaly injection on benchmark quantum circuits, we demonstrate that each metric captures a different aspect of circuit deviation. In particular, structural blind-spot cases (SIS >= 0.95) reveal a clear limitation of structural analysis, where OIS detects anomalies in 93.85% of instances, while IGS detects 72.58%. These results highlight that the metrics provide complementary insights and that a single metric is insufficient for reliable circuit validation.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26267"></a>
### [Algebraic quantum kinematics and SR-selection](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26267v1)

**Authors:** Leonardo A. Pachon  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26267v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26267_figures/2604.26267_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. The photon-sector construction. Classical Fourier–Maxwell theory supplies, before any quantum input, a complex Hilbert-space scaffold (top). A single canonical commutator with scale ¯h (middle) promotes the scaffold to a quantum theory in which photon indivisibility, the Planck relation Eγ = ¯hω, and the spin spectrum σ ¯h = ±¯h along propagation arise as theorems (bottom). The entire quantum step is governed by one scalar parameter, ¯h; the relativistic content (k2 = 0, ω = c|k|, helicity σ = ±1) already lives in the classical scaffold.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26267_figures/2604.26267_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. The complementary structural roles of c and ¯h. The constant c acts within each of the two Fourier-conjugate spaces, defining the metric structure (light cone in spacetime, null cone in dual space) and supporting the local-algebra microcausality of (HK2). The constant ¯h acts between the two spaces: it converts the kinematic phase rates (ω,k,σ), intrinsic to the dual-space mode equation, into the dynamical observables (E,p,Sz), realized as operators on the single-particle Hilbert space. The roles are non-interchangeable.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether the algebraic axioms of quantum field theory, specifically microcausality and positive energy, structurally necessitate special relativity over Galilean kinematics.

**Main result.** The author establishes an algebraic framework where the photon sector of QED is a realization of a single quantum postulate and proposes the SR-selection conjecture, suggesting Galilean kinematics are incompatible with a Haag-Kastler net supporting sharper-than-equal-time microcausality.

**Method.** The paper utilizes operator-algebraic methods, including Weyl C*-algebras, Tomita-Takesaki modular theory, and the GNS construction, to analyze the compatibility of kinematic groups with local algebras.

**Summary.** This paper presents the first in a series exploring the deep algebraic link between quantum mechanics and special relativity. It shows that fundamental properties like photon energy quantization and spin are theorems derived from a single algebraic postulate applied to classical electromagnetism. Crucially, it proposes that the requirements of local quantum field theory (specifically microcausality) effectively 'select' relativistic physics by making Galilean kinematics structurally inconsistent. This provides a potential foundation for understanding why the universe obeys relativistic rather than Newtonian laws at a fundamental algebraic level.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The primary model is the photon sector of free Quantum Electrodynamics (QED) in 3+1 dimensions, treated via a classical Fourier-Maxwell scaffold and quantized using canonical commutation relations.

**Key observables.** Energy (E), momentum (p), helicity (spin), and the number operator (N).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Planck's constant (h-bar) as the deformation scale, the speed of light (c) as the geometric scale, and the Newtonian/Galilean limit (c to infinity).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes the validity of Weyl C*-algebras, the existence of a positive-energy spectrum, and the requirement of sharper-than-equal-time microcausality.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 illustrates the complementary roles of c and h-bar, showing c defining the geometry of light and null cones while h-bar acts as the bridge between kinematic phase rates and dynamical observables.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces an algebraic framework for quantum kinematics, demonstrates its realization in the photon sector, establishes the structural roles of c and h-bar, and concludes by stating the SR-selection conjecture and a roadmap for a six-paper series.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop, as the first of a six-paper series, an operator-algebraic framework relating non-relativistic quantum mechanics and special relativity. Three structural facts organize the framework. (i)~The photon sector of free QED is a transparent realization: classical Fourier--Maxwell theory supplies a complex Hilbert-space scaffold (inner product, symplectic form, Schrödinger-form mode equation, polarization $\mathbb{C}^2$) with no quantum input, and a single canonical commutator with scale $\hbar$ on the mode amplitudes promotes it to single-photon QED, with photon indivisibility, the Planck relation $E=\hbarω$, and the spin spectrum $\pm\hbar$ as theorems. (ii)~The constants $c$ and $\hbar$ play non-interchangeable roles: $c$ is intrinsic to each Fourier-conjugate space, while $\hbar$ acts \emph{between} them, converting kinematic phase rates into dynamical observables. (iii)~Lifting the framework to a Haag--Kastler net with sharper-than-equal-time microcausality and positive-energy spectrum is structurally obstructed in the Galilean case; we state this as the SR-selection conjecture and identify three strands of evidence (Hegerfeldt spreading; absence of a known Galilean multi-particle resolution; Reeh--Schlieder failure on Galilean Haag--Kastler nets), the third established as a precise no-go theorem in the second paper of the series. The framework's modular content (Tomita--Takesaki, Bisognano--Wichmann, Unruh as KMS, type-$\mathrm{III}_1$ universality) is collected as the algebraic substrate on which the curved-background, dynamical-metric, and crossed-product extensions of the third through sixth papers operate.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

We note that for B ∥a, there are multiple metamagnetic tran- sitions, while in contrast, the other field directions are mostly featureless with minor curvature. The curves for temperatures above 1.5 K are constantly shifted upward (with a factor of 60%) for clarity to illustrate the multiple field-induced transitions as marked with different numbers ( with notation ‘u’: up sweep, and ‘d’: down sweep) symbols for 1.5 K curve.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 9</summary>

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating the anisotropic magnetism, field-induced metamagnetic transitions, and the interplay between localized 4f magnetism and itinerant electronic topology in the heavy rare-earth system TbAlGe.

**Main result.** The study identifies a rich, field-tuned magnetic phase diagram with five distinct metamagnetic transitions (MMT1-MMT5) for fields along the a-axis and demonstrates significant magnetotransport anomalies up to 41.5 T.

**Method.** Experimental characterization using high-temperature self-flux crystal growth, followed by magnetization, heat capacity, and electrical transport measurements in magnetic fields up to 41.5 T.

**Summary.** This paper provides a comprehensive study of the magnetic and transport properties of TbAlGe single crystals. The researchers discovered a complex series of magnetic phase transitions that can be tuned with high magnetic fields, particularly along the a-axis. By observing multiple metamagnetic transitions and large magnetoresistance, the study establishes TbAlGe as a key platform for studying the interaction between magnetism and topological electronic states in a tunable semimetal.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Single crystals of the orthorhombic TbAlGe (space group Cmcm), which features a distorted triangular lattice of Tb ions and exhibits complex magnetic interactions.

**Key observables.** Electrical resistivity, magnetization, magnetic susceptibility, heat capacity, magnetoresistance, and magnetic entropy change.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Neel temperatures (T_N1 = 40 K, T_N2 = 8 K), magnetic field strength (up to 41.5 T), and crystallographic axes (a, b, c).

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Structural comparison and XRD refinement; Fig 2: Temperature-dependent susceptibility showing Neel transitions; Fig 3: Isothermal magnetization showing metamagnetic hysteresis; Fig 4: Heat capacity data; Fig 6: Magnetoresistance and phase evolution.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with structural characterization, moves to zero-field magnetic and thermodynamic properties, details high-field magnetization and metamagnetic transitions, analyzes magnetotransport anomalies, and concludes with a discussion on the interplay between magnetism and topology.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report a comprehensive investigation of the anisotropic magnetism and magnetic field-induced transitions in single crystals of the orthorhombic system TbAlGe, a member of the topological RAlGe (R = rare-earth) family with the highest ordering temeprature in the RAlX (X = Si, Ge) series. With a single rare earth site with triangular coordination in its Cmcm orthorhombic unit cell, TbAlGe harbors complex magnetic interactions that yield two antiferromagnetic transitions at 40 K and 8 K in zero field, and a rich cascade of metamagnetic transitions that only appear for fields directed along the crystallographic a-axis. Combining electrical resistivity, magnetization and heat capacity measurements with magnetotransport experiments performed up to 41.5 T, we construct a magnetic phase diagram mapping the multiple magnetic phases of TbAlGe, and discuss the complex interplay between localized 4f magnetism and itinerant electronic topology, establishing TbAlGe as a compelling platform for exploring tunable magnetic semimetal physics.

</details>

**Main problem.** Achieving thermodynamic consistency in the constitutive modeling of Korteweg fluids, specifically ensuring compatibility between balance laws and the entropy balance law.

**Main result.** The derivation of a thermodynamically consistent model that includes a generalized Gibbs relation with capillary terms and demonstrates cross-coupling between mechanical and thermal effects via temperature-dependent coefficients.

**Method.** Liu's method of multipliers (Lagrange multipliers) applied to the entropy inequality to derive restrictions on constitutive functions.

**Summary.** This paper develops a mathematically rigorous framework for modeling Korteweg fluids using Liu's method of multipliers. It ensures that the resulting constitutive relations, such as the stress tensor and heat flux, are compatible with the second law of thermodynamics. A key finding is the derivation of a generalized Gibbs relation that incorporates density gradients and the discovery that temperature-dependent capillary coefficients lead to thermal-mechanical cross-coupling. This provides a consistent macroscopic description for fluids with diffuse interfaces.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Korteweg fluids, which are weakly non-local fluids characterized by mass density gradients and capillary/surface tension effects.

**Key observables.** Entropy production, entropy flux, Korteweg stresses, and the generalized Gibbs relation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mass density (rho), temperature (theta), capillary coefficient (alpha), and transport coefficients (viscosity, thermal conductivity).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The specific entropy is assumed to have a functional form allowing for both equilibrium and non-equilibrium contributions; the symmetric part of the velocity gradient tensor is used in constitutive functions.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with an algebraic lemma, applies the method to a simplified Euler fluid case to establish a baseline, and then extends the framework to the main subject of Korteweg fluids.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The paper studies constitutive modelling of Korteweg fluids. Thermodynamic consistency, i.e. compatibility with entropy balance law, is achieved using Liu's method of multipliers. Appropriate constitutive assumptions facilitated inclusion of the capillary effects in the specific entropy. Korteweg stresses are derived from the equilibrium conditions -- vanishing of the entropy production and its minimization in equilibrium. Material parameter in Korteweg stresses is allowed to depend on temperature, which turns out to be consistent with kinetic-theory results and leads to cross-coupling of mechanical and thermal effects. The generalized Gibbs' relation, which inherits the capillary effects, is derived as consequence, which is a peculiar feature of the Liu's method.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26599"></a>
### [Effective length scales, dispersion relations, and discrete densities of states for Laplacian eigenvectors on complex networks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26599v1)

**Authors:** Per Arne Rikvold  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26599v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to establish a method for constructing dispersion relations and identifying effective length scales for diffusion or oscillation processes on complex networks.

**Main result.** The authors successfully adapt a condensed-matter correlation length method to graph Laplacians, allowing for the identification of both distributed and localized eigenvectors across various network topologies.

**Method.** The method adapts the Debye-Anderson-Brumberger approach, calculating effective length scales as the ratio of twice the total number of edges to the number of edges connecting vertices with different eigenvector signs.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a way to study how waves or diffusion spread across complex, irregular networks. By adapting a technique used to study disordered materials, the author provides a way to measure the 'size' or length scale of network vibrations. The method is tested on a wide variety of real-world and artificial networks, revealing how some patterns stay localized while others spread across the whole system. This is important for understanding dynamics in any complex system, from biological neural networks to power grids.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study applies the method to nine different graph types, including tree graphs (with and without random shortcuts), a roundworm nervous system, a food web, a dolphin social network, an electrical power grid, and a model porous material.

**Key observables.** Effective length scales, dispersion relations, and discrete densities of states.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Eigenvalues of the graph Laplacian (representing inverse time scales) and the sign distribution of eigenvectors.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes that the ratio of volume to interface area can be meaningfully mapped to the ratio of total edges to sign-changing edges in a graph setting.

**Figures summary.** The figures show example eigenvectors, dispersion relations, and discrete densities of states in graphical format.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with a pedagogical description of the method and necessary concepts, followed by the application of the method to nine diverse network models, and concludes with numerical results presented in graphs and tables.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

To construct dispersion relations for diffusion or oscillation processes on random networks, it is necessary to obtain effective length scales for the eigenvectors of a graph Laplacian matrix, whose eigenvalues represent inverse time scales. For this purpose, we adapt a method originally introduced in condensed-matter physics to estimate correlation lengths for disordered materials as the ratio of volume to interface area [P. Debye, H.R. Anderson and H. Brumberger, J. Appl. Phys. 28, 679 (1957)]. In a graph setting of vertices connected by edges, we interpret this as the ratio of twice the total number of edges to the number of edges connecting vertices bearing values of different sign on the particular eigenvector. After describing the method and the necessary concepts in pedagogical detail, we apply it to nine different graphs representing natural and artificial networks, including two tree graphs without and with random shortcuts, the nervous system of a roundworm, a food web, a social network of dolphins, an electrical power grid, and a model porous material. The results identify both distributed and localized eigenvectors. They are given in graphical format showing example eigenvectors, dispersion relations, and discrete densities of states, as well as tables summarizing the main numerical results.

</details>

**Main problem.** Understanding the microscopic origins of the anomalous normal-state electronic structure, magnetism, and orbital-dependent correlations in the hybrid nickelate La5Ni3O11 (1212-LNO).

**Main result.** The study reveals an orbital-selective Mott insulating state in the single-layer Ni ions and strongly renormalized quasiparticle bands in the bilayer Ni ions, with magnetic correlations shifting from single-layer to bilayer-dominated due to correlation effects.

**Method.** Fully charge self-consistent DFT+DMFT using plane-wave pseudopotentials and the continuous-time hybridization expansion quantum Monte Carlo (CTQMC) method.

**Summary.** This paper uses advanced DFT+DMFT calculations to study the hybrid nickelate La5Ni3O11. It identifies a unique orbital-selective Mott insulating state in the single-layer Ni ions while the bilayer ions remain metallic but heavily renormalized. The research also shows how electronic correlations reconstruct the magnetic spectrum and how high pressure can drive a transition from an insulator to a metal. These findings are crucial for understanding the interplay between structural complexity and strong electron correlations in new superconducting materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is the alternating single-layer/bilayer Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate La5Ni3O11 (1212-LNO), consisting of structurally distinct NiO6 slabs.

**Key observables.** Quasiparticle renormalization (m*/m), spectral functions, magnetic susceptibility (spin/charge density waves), and local magnetic moments.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hubbard interaction U = 6 eV, Hund's exchange J = 0.95 eV, and high-pressure regimes (>20 GPa).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Use of the particle-hole bubble approximation for momentum-resolved static magnetic susceptibility, neglecting higher-order vertex corrections.

**Paper structure.** The paper investigates the electronic and magnetic properties of 1212-LNO, detailing the orbital-dependent correlations in single-layer vs. bilayer Ni ions, the effects of pressure on the Mott transition, and the reconstruction of magnetic instabilities.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Using DFT+DMFT we study the normal-state electronic structure and magnetic correlations of the recently discovered alternating single-layer bilayer Raddlesden-Popper nickelate La$_5$Ni$_3$O$_{11}$ (1212-LNO). Our results exhibit qualitative differences for the structurally distinct single-layer and bilayer Ni ions, implying the importance of confinement and orbital-dependent correlations. The Ni $e_g$ electronic states originating from the bilayer Ni ions form strongly renormalized quasiparticle bands with a large enhancement factor $m^*/m \sim 3.5$ and 4.2 for the Ni $x^2-y^2$ and $3z^2-r^2$ orbitals, respectively. Moreover, the $e_g$ states of the single-layer Ni ions exhibit an orbital-selective Mott insulating state, with a narrow energy gap for the Ni $3z^2-r^2$ states and metallic, strongly incoherent (non-Fermi-liquid) Ni $x^2-y^2$ ones. Our analysis of magnetic correlations suggests the formation of intertwined spin and charge density wave stripes in the bilayer NiO$_6$ slab, in close similarity to the double-layer material. We refine two major instabilities, a leading one is associated with a wave vector ${\bf Q}=(\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3})$ (``up-down-0" spin pattern), competing with the bicollinear $(\frac{1}{4},\frac{1}{4})$ (``up-up-down-down") stripe state. The single-layer Ni $3d$ electrons exhibit instability towards the Néel-type magnetic state. Under pressure, 1212-LNO undergoes an orbital-selective Mott insulator-to-metal phase transition, associated with metallization of the single-layer Ni $e_g$ states. As a result, the single-layer Ni $e_g$ bands exhibit non-Fermi-liquid (bad metal) behavior with strongly incoherent spectral weights near $E_F$. We note that correlation effects result in a reconstruction of magnetic correlations as compared to that obtained within DFT. In fact, we observe a crossover from single-layer to double-layer dominated magnetic correlations.

</details>

### [Emergence of $π$ from Equatorial Quantum Localization](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26638v1)

**Authors:** Bin Ye, Ruitao Chen, Lei Yin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26638v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26638_figures/2604.26638_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Exact polar density Pm(θ) = sin2m+1 θ/I2m+1 for several values of m. The distribution</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether the mathematical constant pi emerges from non-radial, angular geometric mechanisms during the classicalization of quantum states.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that pi emerges from equatorial localization on a sphere, where the quantum rigidity index is exactly related to the Wallis partial product.

**Method.** The study uses analytical derivations of expectation values for spherical harmonics, asymptotic analysis of Gamma functions, and the application of the correspondence principle.

**Summary.** This paper shows that the mathematical constant pi can be derived from the way quantum probability clouds localize around the equator of a sphere. By studying the highest-weight spherical harmonics, the authors find that a specific geometric measure of localization, called the rigidity index, is exactly linked to the Wallis product. As the quantum number increases, the system approaches classical behavior, and the Wallis formula for pi is recovered. This reveals that certain mathematical structures in quantum mechanics are signatures of geometric localization rather than specific radial dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The paper analyzes the highest-weight branch of spherical harmonics within the frameworks of a rigid rotor and a thin spherical shell.

**Key observables.** Equatorial Rigidity Index (Rm), Wallis partial product (Wm), and angular width (delta theta).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Large-quantum-number limit (m to infinity), highest-weight branch (m = l), and the regime of radial freezing.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation is specific to the highest-weight branch of spherical harmonics and assumes a fixed spherical radius (radial freezing).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of pi emergence, defines the geometric rigidity index, applies the derivation to the rigid rotor and thin shell models, and concludes with the asymptotic recovery of the Wallis formula in the semiclassical limit.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a genuinely non-radial quantum-mechanical route by which $π$ emerges from equatorial localization on the sphere. For the highest-weight branch of spherical harmonics, this localization is captured by a natural geometric rigidity index, whose exact finite-quantum-number value is a Wallis partial product. The mechanism is realized in two settings: the standard rigid rotor and the surface sector of a thin spherical shell, where radial freezing reduces the dynamics to the same angular problem. In the large-quantum-number limit, the probability cloud collapses toward the equator, the rigidity index approaches its classical value, and the Wallis formula is recovered through the correspondence principle. The result shows that Wallis-type structures in quantum mechanics can arise as exact signatures of semiclassical localization encoded by a simple geometric observable.

</details>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the computational power of quantum witnesses under perfect completeness, specifically seeking oracle separations between the complexity classes QMA1 and QCMA.

**Main result.** The authors construct classical oracles that separate QMA1 from QCMA under bounded adaptivity and derive an in-place permutation oracle separation. They also establish lower bounds on the circuit complexity required to prepare approximate ground states of certain sparse Hamiltonians.

**Method.** The study employs oracle constructions, derandomization of permutation-based models, and the Feynman-Kitaev history state construction. They use techniques like the parallel-query hybrid method, quantum phase estimation, and transcript analysis to prove lower bounds.

**Summary.** This paper addresses fundamental questions in quantum complexity theory regarding whether quantum witnesses provide more power than classical ones when perfect completeness is required. By constructing specific oracle models, the authors demonstrate a separation between QMA1 and QCMA. Furthermore, they bridge complexity theory and many-body physics by showing that these separations imply high circuit complexity for preparing the ground states of certain sparse, frustration-free Hamiltonians.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The work focuses on complexity-theoretic models including sparse Hamiltonians (O(1)-sparse), permutation oracles (in-place and forward), and the Hamiltonian query model (Orc_H).

**Key observables.** Completeness and soundness gaps, ground-state energy, and circuit gate complexity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of adaptive rounds (R), number of parallel queries (q), Hamiltonian sparsity (d), and the precision of the energy gap (exponentially small vs. fixed).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The separation results are sensitive to the number of adaptive rounds and the specific oracle model used; some results assume a specific universal gate set.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by introducing the QMA1 vs. QCMA problem, followed by the construction of classical oracles for bounded adaptivity. It then moves to the derandomization of permutation oracles to achieve in-place separation, analyzes the impact of small energy gaps, and concludes by applying these complexity results to derive lower bounds on Hamiltonian ground-state preparation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the power of quantum witnesses under perfect completeness. We construct a classical oracle relative to which a language lies in $\mathsf{QMA}_1$ but not in $\mathsf{QCMA}$ when the $\mathsf{QCMA}$ verifier is only allowed polynomially many adaptive rounds and exponentially many parallel queries per round. Additionally, we derandomize the permutation-oracle separation of Fefferman and Kimmel, obtaining an in-place oracle separation between $\mathsf{QMA}_1$ and $\mathsf{QCMA}$. Furthermore, we focus on $\mathsf{QCMA}$ and $\mathsf{QMA}$ with an exponentially small gap, where we show a separation assuming the gap is fixed, but not when it may be arbitrarily small. Finally, we derive consequences for approximate ground-state preparation from sparse Hamiltonian oracle access, including a bounded-adaptivity frustration-free variant.

</details>

### [Fault-Tolerant Resource Comparison of Qudit and Qubit Encodings for Diagonal Quadratic Operators](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26792v1)

**Authors:** Samuel Godwood, Doğa Murat Kürkçüoğlu, Gabriel N. Perdue, Marina Maneyro, Alessandro Roggero  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26792v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The dashed curve marks the same-precision qubit-Rz effective prefactor aLCU Rz (d, 3000, 10−6) from Eq. 121. Values of aLCU max above this reference prefactor indicate that the fully qudit implementa- tion can match or outperform the qubit baseline in total non- Clifford cost without requiring an embedded two-level qudit synthesis prefactor smaller than the corresponding qubit-Rz effective prefactor. Only prime local dimensions d are in- cluded in the fixed-encoding analysis.</sub>

</details>

Panel (a) shows the ratio Rtot(d) = T qb tot(d)/T qd tot(d) as a function of the local dimension d. Panel (b) shows the corresponding total T-count estimates T qb tot(d) and T qd tot(d).</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

Panel (a) shows the ratio Rtot(d) = T qb tot(d)/T qd tot(d) as a function of the local dimension d. Panel (b) shows the corresponding total T-count estimates T qb tot(d) and T qd tot(d). The piecewise-constant structure and repeated crossover windows arise from the binary embedding.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Comparing the fault-tolerant resource costs, specifically non-Clifford gate counts, between qudit and qubit encodings for implementing diagonal quadratic operators.

**Main result.** While qubit encodings are asymptotically superior, qudits can provide meaningful constant-factor savings in specific finite-dimensional windows, particularly in the LCU framework and time-dominated regimes.

**Method.** The authors analyze two simulation paradigms (product-formula and LCU/block-encoding) using a resource metric of non-Clifford gate counts after synthesis into a discrete gate set.

**Summary.** This paper investigates whether using higher-dimensional qudits can reduce the computational overhead of simulating scalar field theories compared to standard qubit encodings. By analyzing non-Clifford gate costs in both Trotter-based and LCU-based simulation regimes, the authors find that while qubits win asymptotically, qudits offer significant resource savings for small, finite dimensions. The work provides explicit thresholds for quantum compilers to determine when qudit encodings are advantageous.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the implementation of diagonal quadratic operators, such as the mass term $e^{-it\phi_x^2}$ in a discretized real scalar field theory using a uniform grid.

**Key observables.** Non-Clifford gate counts (T-count), rotation counts, and the ratio of resource costs between qudit and qubit encodings.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Local dimension $d$, number of qubits $n_b$, synthesis prefactor $a$, target precision $\epsilon$, and simulation time $t$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes an idealized, negligible-overhead code-switching model between qubits and qudits and focuses on the synthesis cost of embedded $SU(2)$ rotations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the break-even synthesis prefactor versus dimension; Figure 2 compares non-Clifford resources in precision-dominated and time-dominated regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of qudit vs. qubit encoding, analyzes the product-formula regime, evaluates the LCU framework, performs a secondary analysis using a code-switching model, and concludes with synthesis targets for compilers.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides critical guidance for designing hardware-efficient quantum algorithms for lattice field theories by identifying specific dimensions where qudit-based architectures outperform standard qubits.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Finite local Hilbert-space truncations arise naturally in quantum simulations of lattice field theories and motivate qudit encodings, but their fault-tolerant advantage over qubit encodings remains unclear. We compare the non-Clifford cost of implementing quadratic diagonal evolutions, exemplified by $U=e^{-itφ_x^2}$ in a uniform field-amplitude discretization of a real scalar field, using either one logical $d$-level qudit or $n_b=\lceil \log_2 d\rceil$ logical qubits. We analyze two standard settings: product-formula simulation and LCU/block encoding, taking the resource metric to be the number of non-Clifford gates after synthesis into a discrete logical gate set. Because tight synthesis bounds for general single-qudit rotations are not known, we express the qudit constructions in terms of embedded two-level $SU(2)$ rotations and derive explicit finite-$d$ break-even conditions for their synthesis cost; these serve as compiler targets for when qudit encodings can outperform the qubit baseline. Within the constructive models studied here, product-formula implementations would require an exponentially stronger per-primitive synthesis advantage for qudits to win asymptotically, while in the LCU setting the qubit encoding is asymptotically cheaper in $d$. Nevertheless, the finite-$d$ threshold analysis identifies low dimensional regions in which qudits can yield meaningful constant-factor savings, particularly for LCU-based implementations. As a secondary analysis of the LCU construction, we use an idealized negligible-overhead qubit-qudit code-switching model to give an absolute $T$-count comparison, and reinterpret the savings as an allowable per-switch overhead budget.

</details>

The results suggest that this quantity peaks at intermediate temperatures when ρ = 0.6 or ρ = 1.0. The compressibility at half filling decreases rapidly at this temperature upon increasing U/t from 5 to 10, a finite-temperature signature of the Mott metal- insulator transition in the ground state.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the development of ferromagnetic correlations and the metal-insulator transition in the Kagome lattice Hubbard model across varying densities and interaction strengths.

**Main result.** The authors find that repulsive interactions enhance ferromagnetic correlations at high densities and that increasing interaction strength extends these correlations toward half-filling, connecting flat-band ferromagnetism to Nagaoka ferromagnetism. They also provide an accurate estimate for the critical interaction strength of the Mott transition at $U_c/t = 6.41(5)$.

**Method.** The study utilizes two exact finite-temperature numerical methods: Numerical Linked-Cluster Expansion (NLCE) and Determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC).

**Summary.** This paper explores how magnetism evolves in the Kagome lattice Hubbard model. By using exact numerical methods, the authors show how ferromagnetic correlations transition from being driven by flat-band physics to being driven by Mott physics (Nagaoka ferromagnetism) as interaction strength increases. They also precisely locate the metal-insulator transition at half-filling. These findings are significant for understanding strongly correlated electrons in frustrated geometries and provide a roadmap for experiments using ultracold atoms in optical lattices.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The Kagome lattice Fermi-Hubbard model, characterized by geometric frustration, a non-interacting flat energy band, and van Hove singularities.

**Key observables.** Spin correlations, magnetic susceptibility, charge compressibility, density of states, charge gap, and Curie-Weiss temperature.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interaction strength ($U/t$), temperature ($T/t$), and electron density ($ho$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The NLCE convergence is limited by the size of the largest clusters at low temperatures, and DQMC is subject to the fermion sign problem.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the equation of state, magnetic susceptibility across different densities and temperatures, Curie-Weiss temperature dependence, and compressibility/double occupancy as functions of interaction strength and temperature.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Kagome Hubbard model, describes the NLCE and DQMC methodologies, presents results on magnetic correlations and susceptibility, analyzes the Mott metal-insulator transition via compressibility, and concludes with implications for future studies.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Kagome lattice Fermi-Hubbard model is one of the most physically rich, and at the same time most challenging, models to study in strongly-correlated physics. Among its special features are geometric frustration and a flat energy band that create conditions favorable to ferromagnetism near the band insulating limit. Here, we utilize two exact finite-temperature methods, the numerical linked-cluster expansion and the determinant quantum Monte Carlo, to study the extent as well as doping and interaction dependence of ferromagnetic correlations and other thermodynamic properties of the model. We find that repulsive interactions enhance ferromagnetic correlations at high electron densities and that increasing the interaction strength, extends the region with strong ferromagnetic correlations towards half filling, smoothly connecting it to Nagaoka ferromagnetism near the Mott insulating region. We also use the charge compressibility to obtain an accurate estimate for the critical interaction strength for the metal-insulator transition at half filling. These results improve our understanding of the magnetic tendencies of the model away from half filling and pave the way for further studies, including with ultracold atoms in optical lattices.

</details>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the feasibility of forming and controlling the doubly charged cerium monofluoride (CeF2+) ion as a stable, isoelectronic surrogate for the radioactive protactinium monofluoride (PaF3+) to search for physics beyond the Standard Model.

**Main result.** The researchers successfully demonstrated the experimental formation and high-resolution mass identification of CeF2+ using the TITAN facility and provided quantum chemical predictions for its electronic structure and sensitivity to P,T-odd properties.

**Method.** Experimental methods include ECR ionization, molecule synthesis via SF6 addition in a RFQ cooler-buncher, and MR-TOF mass spectrometry; theoretical methods involve UCCSD(T) and 2c-ZORA-cGHF ab initio calculations.

**Summary.** This paper presents the experimental production and identification of the doubly charged cerium monofluoride ion (CeF2+) as a surrogate for the radioactive protactinium monofluoride (PaF3+). By using the TITAN mass spectrometer at TRIUMF, the authors demonstrate that CeF2+ can be synthesized in the gas phase using SF6. Theoretical calculations are provided to show that CeF2+ shares an electronic structure with PaF3+, making it a valuable platform for studying parity and time-reversal symmetry violations. The study establishes a foundation for future searches for new physics using controlled molecular ions.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of gaseous, multiply charged molecular ions (CeF2+, CeF+, and potentially PaF3+) produced and manipulated within the TITAN ion-trap system at the TRIUMF laboratory.

**Key observables.** Mass-to-charge ratio (m/q), ionization energies, dissociation energies, transition dipole moments, and sensitivity to P,T-odd properties like the nuclear Schiff moment.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mass resolving power (R > 10^5), ion flux (up to 5 x 10^8 pps), and gas pressures (He buffer gas at ~10^-1 mbar, SF6 at ~10^-3 mbar).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of CeF2+ as a valid surrogate assumes the electronic structure of the isoelectronic Ce2+ [Xe]4f2 and Pa3+ [Rn]5f2 configurations are sufficiently similar for sensitivity estimates.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the experimental workflow from ion production to mass determination; Figure 2 displays charge state distributions; Figure 4 and 5 show high and low resolution TOF mass spectra; Figure 13 illustrates reaction energy landscapes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the motivation (new physics searches), describes the experimental setup at TRIUMF, details the molecular synthesis and mass identification, presents quantum chemical calculations of electronic structure, and concludes with prospects for quantum control.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to AMO physics as it demonstrates advanced techniques in molecular ion synthesis, mass spectrometry, and the use of molecular spectroscopy to probe fundamental symmetries and precision physics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Tricationic protactinium monofluoride ($^{229}$PaF$^{3+}$) has been proposed as a candidate for probing physics beyond the Standard Model of particle physics. Since studies with $^{229}$PaF$^{3+}$ require significant experimental advances, we exploit the stable, valence-isoelectronic dicationic cerium monofluoride (CeF$^{2+}$) as a surrogate. Gas-phase fluorinated-cerium molecular ions are formed and identified using the Off-Line Ion Source and TITAN mass measurement facilities at TRIUMF. Quantum chemical calculations are performed on the electronic structure of CeF$^{2+}$, revealing a parallel to that of $^{229}$PaF$^{3+}$. Moreover, these calculations provide estimates on the sensitivity of CeF$^{2+}$ itself to various $\mathcal{P,T}$-odd properties. A brief discourse on the specifics of the quantum control of CeF$^{2+}$ is presented which anticipates future searches for symmetry violations.

</details>

### [Functional Dimensional Regularization for O(N) Models](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26207v1)

**Authors:** P. Beretta, A. Codello  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26207v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26207_figures/2604.26207_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Estimate of the critical exponent ω as a function of N in d = 3 at FDR-DE2 (blue) nu = 11, NPRG-DE2 (red-square final result vs red-dot raw data), NPRG-DE4 (green-square final result vs green-dot raw data), large-N (magenta) and CB stars (black).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26207_figures/2604.26207_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Estimate of the critical exponent ν as a function of N in d = 3 FDR-DE2 (blue) with nu = 11, NPRG-DE2 (red- square final result vs red-dot raw data), NPRG-DE4 (green- square final result vs green-dot raw data) and at large-N (ma- genta) and CB stars (black).</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to extend the Functional Dimensional Regularization (FDR) scheme to the O(N) universality class and evaluate its efficiency and accuracy in calculating critical exponents.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that FDR provides highly efficient and accurate results for critical exponents in the O(N) model, with precision competitive with state-of-the-art methods like the Conformal Bootstrap and higher-order Non-Perturbative Renormalization Group.

**Method.** The study utilizes Functional Dimensional Regularization (FDR) at the second-order derivative expansion (DE2) level, involving a polynomial expansion of the potential and wave-function renormalizations.

**Summary.** This paper investigates the Functional Dimensional Regularization (FDR) scheme applied to the O(N) universality class. The authors show that FDR is a lightweight and efficient method that can produce critical exponents comparable to much more computationally intensive non-perturbative renormalization group and conformal bootstrap methods. By explicitly deriving the flow equations, they prove that the scheme's success is not coincidental. The results demonstrate rapid convergence and high accuracy across a wide range of N values.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on O(N) symmetric models/universality classes in three dimensions, including specific cases such as the Ising model (N=1), XY model (N=2), Heisenberg model (N=3), and self-avoiding random walks (N=0).

**Key observables.** Critical exponents: anomalous dimension (eta), correlation length exponent (nu), and correction-to-scaling exponent (omega).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimension d=3, number of field components N (ranging from -2 to 1000), and mass scale mu.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is performed using a derivative expansion truncated at the second order (DE2) and a polynomial expansion of the potential and renormalization functions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows eta vs N; Figure 2 shows nu vs N; Figure 3 shows omega vs N, comparing FDR results against NPRG, Conformal Bootstrap, and large-N limits.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the FDR scheme, derives the flow equations for the O(N) model, presents numerical results for various N values, and compares these results against established benchmarks like NPRG and Conformal Bootstrap.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The novel functional dimensional regularization (FDR) scheme has proven capable of yielding results that are competitive with the state-of-the-art in the computation of critical exponents in $d=3$, while also reproducing those from the $\varepsilon$-expansion for the Ising and other universality classes. In this work, we show that this is not a mere coincidence: by applying the scheme to the $O(N)$ universality class, we explicitly derive the flow equations and obtain critical exponents that are comparable to those obtained with higher-order non-perturbative approaches. In this case, FDR retains the features already highlighted in previous works -- namely, its efficiency and rapid convergence.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26271"></a>
### [Galilean Reeh--Schlieder Obstruction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26271v1)

**Authors:** Leonardo A. Pachon  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26271v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether the Reeh-Schlieder property, a fundamental feature of relativistic algebraic quantum field theory (AQFT), can be maintained within a Galilean-invariant framework.

**Main result.** The author proves that the standard Galilean Haag-Kastler axioms, when combined with Bargmann mass superselection, are mathematically inconsistent with the Reeh-Schlieder property. This identifies the Reeh-Schlieder property as the defining structural difference between relativistic and Galilean AQFT.

**Method.** The proof utilizes algebraic quantum field theory (AQFT) frameworks, specifically examining the interaction between Galilean Schrödinger fields, Bargmann mass superselection rules, and the properties of the Fock vacuum.

**Summary.** This paper establishes a fundamental mathematical distinction between relativistic and Galilean quantum field theories. It proves that the Reeh-Schlieder property—which allows for the preparation of local states from the vacuum—cannot exist in a Galilean-invariant theory with mass superselection. The work shows that the absence of this property in Galilean systems leads to the unavailability of Tomita-Takesaki modular flow, marking a deep structural divergence from relativistic physics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on Galilean algebraic quantum field theory (AQFT) nets, specifically those characterized by Schrödinger fields and Bargmann mass superselection charges.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Bargmann mass charges, mass spectrum, and the presence/absence of the Tomita-Takesaki modular flow.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The proof assumes the standard Galilean Haag-Kastler axioms and the presence of Bargmann mass superselection; it also assumes canonical fields carry definite mass charges and admit time-zero restrictions on a stable common dense domain.

**Paper structure.** The paper presents a formal proof of inconsistency, demonstrates how the mass spectrum and vacuum properties are derived consequences of the algebraic structure, and concludes by establishing the Reeh-Schlieder property as the boundary between relativistic and Galilean field theories.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We prove that the standard Galilean Haag--Kastler axioms, augmented by Bargmann mass superselection, are inconsistent with the Reeh--Schlieder property: no such net admits a vacuum that is cyclic and separating for every local field algebra. Two ingredients combine: Galilean Schrödinger fields annihilate the Fock vacuum, and Bargmann mass superselection forbids the Hermitian-combination evasion that keeps relativistic axioms consistent. The result extends beyond the Fock-representation hypothesis: any Galilean Haag--Kastler net whose canonical fields carry definite Bargmann mass charges and admit time-zero restrictions on a field-algebra-stable common dense domain is incompatible with Reeh--Schlieder. The bounded-below mass spectrum and the vacuum-at-spectral-minimum, usually imposed as separate axioms, are derived consequences -- of positive-energy boost positivity and a Bose-CCR algebraic descent, respectively. The Tomita--Takesaki modular flow is consequently unavailable on Galilean local field algebras. We identify the Reeh--Schlieder property as the precise structural ingredient distinguishing relativistic from Galilean algebraic quantum field theory: relativistic AQFT has it as a theorem, Galilean AQFT cannot.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [HyPulse: A Pulse Synthesis Framework for Hybrid Qubit-Oscillator Gates on Trapped-Ion Platform](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26804v1)

**Authors:** Masoud Hakimi Heris, Yuan Liu, Frank Mueller  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26804v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26804_figures/2604.26804_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. Full hybrid qubit-qumode quantum computing stack. HyPulse fills the hybrid qubit-qumode pulse synthesis slot between the compiler/decomposer and the hardware export layer, alongside existing DV-only synthesis tools. The content-addressed pulse library is shared between the offline synthesis phase and the online assembly phase.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26804_figures/2604.26804_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Phase-space illustration of the three HyPulse gate primitives. Each column shows one gate. Rows show the motional Wigner function conditioned on the two qubit branches, with dashed contours showing the initial state. (a) CD(√π): opposite displacements along x conditioned on |±⟩. (b) CR(θ = 0.38): opposite phase-space rotations of a squeezed vacuum conditioned on |0⟩, |1⟩. (c) CS(r = 0.5): opposite squeezings along orthogonal quadratures conditioned on |±⟩.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26804_figures/2604.26804_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. HyPulse two-phase architecture. The offline phase runs the optimizer once per (gate, device, model) combination and populates a content-addressed library. The online phase assembles circuits from cached pulses, triggering on- demand synthesis only for parameter combinations not yet in the library.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26804_figures/2604.26804_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 5. Wigner function evolution through the squeezed cat preparation circuit on Sydney hardware parameters. (a) After CS(r = 0.5): squeezed vacuum. (b) After CS + CD(√π): spin-motion entangled state with two separated lobes. (c) After post-selection on | ↑⟩: squeezed cat state with Wigner negativity neg = −0.278, confirming non-classical coherence consistent with Kienzler et al. [36].</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.26804_figures/2604.26804_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. CD gate characterization on the hardware parameters from Table III. (a) Optimized pulse waveforms Ωx(t) (blue) and Ωp(t) (orange) for T = 100 µs, showing both channels bounded within the hardware amplitude limit. (b) Wigner function of the motional mode after CD(√π), confirming a coherent displacement. (c) Infidelity 1−F vs. gate duration T, demonstrating the pre-characterization sweep capability of the framework.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 6</summary>

<img src="2604.26804_figures/2604.26804_fig6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 5 shows the Wigner function at each circuit stage. After CS (panel a), the vacuum Gaussian is elongated along P, con- firming squeezing. After CD (panel b), the state is entangled: Two separated squeezed lobes are visible, corresponding to the two spin-conditioned displacement branches. After post- selection on | ↑⟩(panel c), the motional mode is projected onto the squeezed cat state with peak Wigner negativity neg = −0.278 and post-selection probability p(↑) = 0.514 ≈0.5, consistent with the expected even-parity cat state. The clear Wigner negativity in panel (c) is the definitive signature of non-classical coherence, confirming that the two HyPulse-compiled primitives compose...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 7</summary>

<img src="2604.26804_figures/2604.26804_fig7.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 6. Noise characterization under the motional dephasing model reported in Matsos et al. [7] (γ = 18 Hz, 500 trajectories). (a) Closed-system squeezed cat Wigner function, neg = −0.278. (b) Noisy Wigner function averaged over 500 trajectories, neg = −0.273: negativity preserved. (c) Per-gate noisy fidelity Fnoisy for CD, CR, and CS. CD and CR are less affected by noise; CS is limited by its 1300 µs gate duration, a hardware constraint addressable by improved sideband Rabi rates.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** There is a missing compilation layer in the quantum software stack that connects high-level hybrid qubit-oscillator gate primitives to hardware-level pulse waveforms for trapped-ion platforms, specifically addressing the difficulty of optimizing continuous parametric gates.

**Main result.** The authors present HyPulse, a two-phase pulse synthesis framework that uses a content-addressed cache to efficiently reuse optimized pulses, achieving high gate fidelities (F > 0.99) and successful numerical preparation of a squeezed cat state.

**Method.** A two-phase architecture consisting of an offline optimization engine (using gradient-based GRAPE) to populate a hashed pulse library and an online assembler to stitch waveforms into hardware-executable programs for DAX/ARTIQ and JaqalPaw.

**Summary.** HyPulse is a new software framework designed to automate the synthesis and assembly of complex pulses for hybrid qubit-oscillator gates in trapped-ion systems. It solves the bottleneck of manually optimizing every unique parameter set by using a smart caching system that recognizes when a pulse can be reused. The framework is compatible with major hardware backends like ARTIQ and QSCOUT. By allowing researchers to pre-characterize gates numerically, it helps identify hardware constraints before physical experiments are even performed.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A hybrid qubit-oscillator system on a trapped-ion platform, utilizing laser-driven sideband interactions to couple a spin qubit to a bosonic motional mode.

**Key observables.** Gate fidelities (closed and noisy systems), Wigner function negativity, and phase-space displacements/rotations/squeezing.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lamb-Dicke parameter (eta), motional frequency (omega_m), qubit transition frequency (omega_q), sideband detuning (Delta), and squeezing/displacement amplitudes (alpha, zeta, theta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The validation is purely numerical/simulated; the noise model is restricted to motional dephasing; and effects like AC Stark shifts or cross-mode coupling are not considered.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the hybrid quantum computing stack; Figure 2 illustrates phase-space transformations for gate primitives; Figure 3 diagrams the HyPulse architecture; Figure 4 shows optimized waveforms and infidelity sweeps; Figure 5 shows the Wigner function evolution for a squeezed cat state.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the compilation gap, describes the HyPulse four-layer abstraction architecture (Device, Gate, Compilation, Export), details the two-phase synthesis/assembly process, validates the framework via numerical gate fidelity and state preparation simulations, and concludes with future directions.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a systematic way to bridge the gap between continuous-variable and discrete-variable quantum control, offering a scalable software approach to managing the complex, parametric pulse landscapes required for hybrid bosonic-qubit architectures.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

As hybrid qubit-oscillator algorithm development and trapped-ion hardware demonstrations advance in parallel, there is a lack of a compilation layer connecting the two at the pulse level in the vertical software stack. While qubit gate control and pulse synthesis are well-established, the translation of hybrid qubit-oscillator primitives to the pulse level has not been systematically addressed. This gap is further compounded by the inherently continuous parametric nature of such gates. Each distinct parameter value defines a physically unique operation requiring independent pulse optimization, making static pre-compilation strategies inapplicable. To fill this gap, we present HyPulse, a hardware-aware pulse synthesis and generation framework, which contributes a two-phase architecture decoupling pulse discovery from circuit assembly. An offline optimization engine populates a content-addressed cache of high-fidelity primitives: If a pulse for a given gate, parameter, and device specification already exists in the library, it is retrieved instantly; otherwise the optimizer synthesizes, hashes, and caches it automatically. An online assembler then constructs circuit-specific pulse programs ready to drive trapped-ion hardware control systems via DAX/ARTIQ (Duke) and JaqalPaw/QSCOUT (Sandia), trapped-ion pulse execution backends.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26047"></a>
### [Iterative warm-start optimization with quantum imaginary time evolution](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26047v1)

**Authors:** Phillip C. Lotshaw, Titus Morris, Stuart Hadfield, Ryan Bennink  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26047v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26047_figures/2604.26047_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. The iterative quantum optimization algorithm uses a conventional computer (left) to compute a circuit that attempts to decrease the energy of the best-known solution, then sends the circuit to a quantum computer (right) which executes the circuit to update the best-known solution.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26047_figures/2604.26047_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Performance of the protocol for MaxCut on collections random 3-regular graphs. (top) Using 100 total measurements the approach yields median normalized cost values that are close to optimal for N ≤30, outperforming random search and a ”classical” version of the protocol that omits the quantum imaginary time evolution. Error bars show quartiles of the distributions. (bottom) The algorithm obtains significant optimal solution probabilities out to N = 30, while the classical and random searches do not.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26047_figures/2604.26047_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. Performance at N = 22 as the number of iterations Nit and the number of shots per iteration Nshots/it are varied, with a fixed shot budget Nshots = 100.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26047_figures/2604.26047_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. Performance at N = 22 as the number of shots Nshots is varied.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Efficiently approximating NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems, specifically Max-Cut, using quantum algorithms with limited shot budgets and circuit depth.

**Main result.** The iterative warm-start QITE algorithm achieves median solutions within 95% of the global optimum and finds exact optimal solutions in 11% of cases for 3-regular graphs with up to 30 vertices, significantly outperforming random and classical search methods.

**Method.** A non-variational, iterative algorithm that uses Quantum Imaginary Time Evolution (QITE) to evolve a state initialized near the best-known solution. The circuit parameters are determined classically using analytic equations to avoid quantum device queries during the derivation phase.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new non-variational quantum algorithm for solving combinatorial optimization problems like Max-Cut. Instead of using a fixed ansatz, the algorithm uses an iterative 'warm-start' approach where each step begins with a state centered around the best solution found so far. By applying Quantum Imaginary Time Evolution, the algorithm drives the state toward lower energy levels. Simulations show that this method is highly efficient, finding near-optimal or exact solutions even with a very small number of quantum measurements.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the unweighted Max-Cut problem on 3-regular graphs, modeled by a Hamiltonian consisting of Pauli Z-Z interaction terms.

**Key observables.** Normalized cost (relative to the global optimum) and the probability of finding the exact optimal solution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Shot budget (tested at 100 total shots), number of iterations (N_it = 10), imaginary time step (delta_tau = 0.1), and the initial state superposition parameter (phi) which varies from pi/4 to 0.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The algorithm assumes that the circuit parameters can be computed analytically for product states and that the problem can be effectively represented by a pairwise ansatz.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a schematic of the classical-quantum hybrid loop; Figure 2 shows the median normalized cost and the fraction of optimal solutions found compared to other search methods.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of combinatorial optimization, describes the proposed iterative warm-start QITE algorithm and its classical-quantum workflow, presents numerical simulation results on 3-regular graphs, and concludes with discussions on limitations and future research directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Approximate combinatorial optimization is a promising use case for quantum computers. The quantum optimization algorithms often employ a fixed ansatz that evolves an unbiased initial state towards states with better values of the optimand, then samples the states to determine an approximately optimal solution. However, promising alternative approaches have considered ``warm-start" and sampling-based methods that instead begin from the best known solution, which can be directly optimized with the quantum computer and updated as new information becomes available, potentially outperforming the fixed ansätze. Here we use these ideas to design a nonvariational quantum algorithm for combinatorial optimization. At each step the algorithm begins with a state superposed around the best known solution, then drives it to lower energy using quantum imaginary time evolution. These nonvariational, initial-state-dependent circuits are determined using analytic equations that are evaluated using only a conventional computer. After implementing the circuits, the state is sampled, potentially obtaining a new best-known solution to use as the initial state at the next iteration. Using simulations of the algorithm solving MaxCut on 3-regular graphs with 30 or fewer vertices and a shot budget of 100 total shots, the approach obtains median solutions within 95\% of the global optimum and finds optimal solutions in 11\% or more of cases, significantly outperforming random and simplified classical search procedures. We discuss several future directions.

</details>
