<a id="paper-2604.26028"></a>
### [Magnetononlinear Hall effect from multigap topology in metal-organic frameworks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26028v1)

**Authors:** Chun Wang Chau, Wojciech J. Jankowski, Bo Peng, Robert-Jan Slager  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26028v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26028_figures/2604.26028_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Multigap topology in metal-organic frameworks. (a) Crystal structure of two-dimensional metal-organic NHC- Au kagome lattice framework, top view (above) and front view (below). The partial charge density of the kagome bands near the Fermi level is shown by the light cyan isosurface, indicating delocalized features across the kagome lattice, against the localized orbitals around metallic atoms. The hybridized states of the gold atoms and NHC ligands constitute the kagome basis orbitals A, B, C. (b) Comparison of band structure sections of kagome NHC-X (X = Au, Ag, Cu, AuH) metal-organic frameworks across hexagonal BZ, with quadratic band touching at the Γ point (BZ center), and...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26028_figures/2604.26028_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Magnetononlinear Hall effect from non-Abelian Euler band topology. (a) Orbital-projected kagome bands with e2 = 1 in NHC-Au metal-organic framework and nonlinear magnetoelectric Hall response at different dopings (µ). (b) Temperature dependence of the magnetononlinear Hall effect around the quadratic node in NHC-Au metal-organic frame- work. (c) Scaling of the magnetononlinear Hall response at temperature T = 1 meV in the NHC-X (X = Au, Ag, Cu) metal- organic frameworks, against the power-law dependence, χorb xy,z ∝|e2|/µ, arising from the multiband topology via non-Abelian Berry connection vorticity.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The study aims to identify and detect non-Abelian multigap band topology, specifically characterized by non-trivial Euler class invariants, using measurable transport phenomena.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that non-Abelian multigap topology induces an observable magnetononlinear Hall effect in 2D kagome metal-organic frameworks, providing a 'smoking-gun' probe for these topological states.

**Method.** The researchers combined first-principles DFT calculations with Wannier-based transport simulations (WannierBerri, WannierTools) and analytical continuum k.p models.

**Summary.** This paper explores the discovery of non-Abelian multigap topology in 2D metal-organic frameworks. By studying kagome-structured NHC-X materials, the authors show that the Euler class topology leads to a measurable magnetononlinear Hall effect. They demonstrate that this effect is highly tunable via chemical substitution, temperature, and voltage. This work establishes a new way to experimentally detect complex, non-Abelian topological invariants in organic-based materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical platform is a class of 2D kagome N-heterocyclic carbene (NHC) metal-organic frameworks (NHC-X, where X = Au, Ag, or Cu) featuring a three-band subspace near the Fermi level.

**Key observables.** Magnetononlinear Hall response (specifically the $\chi_{xy,z}^{orb}$ component), multi-gap edge states, and the Euler class invariant ($e_2$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Chemical potential ($\mu$), temperature ($T$), external voltage, and chemical substitution (Au, Ag, Cu); regimes include the flat band limit and zero-temperature limit.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analytical derivation relies on a low-energy physics approximation via a $k \cdot p$ continuum model and focuses primarily on the zero-temperature limit.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the crystal structure, band structures, and edge states of the NHC-X kagome lattice. Fig 2 displays the nonlinear Hall response as a function of doping and temperature, including scaling behavior.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the material platform and topological features, presents DFT-based electronic structure and edge state analysis, derives the analytical magnetononlinear Hall response using a continuum model, and validates the results through numerical transport simulations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We unveil that non-Abelian multigap band topology characterized by nontrivial Euler class invariants induces observable magnetononlinear Hall transport phenomena. We demonstrate these effects in a highly-tunable class of recently synthesized two-dimensional kagome N-heterocyclic carbene (NHC) metal-organic frameworks. We showcase the controllability of the nonlinear effect upon applying external voltage, changing temperature, and chemical substitutions that preserve the bulk topology and associated edge states. Our findings therefore reveal an uncharted presence of Euler class topology in metal-organic materials that can be experimentally deduced through measurable magnetotransport.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26477"></a>
### [Multi-Objective Optimization by Quantum-Annealing-Inspired Algorithms](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26477v1)

**Authors:** Xian-Zhe Tao, Pavel Mosharev, Man-Hong Yung  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26477v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26477_figures/2604.26477_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

The y-axis shows the Hypervolume difference (HVmax −HVt + 1) in logarithmic scale, and the x-axis represents the sampling time in seconds. Data for all algorithms, except for SB, is taken from References [19, 22]. See details and hardware information in Table 1.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26477_figures/2604.26477_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Comparison of sampling time with full Pareto front reconstruction time. The y-axis shows the Hypervol- ume difference (HVmax −HVt + 1) in logarithmic scale, and the x-axis represents the end-to-end time in seconds. DCM and DPA-a are classical baseline algorithms. End-to-end time for NI-dSB and NI-bSB includes both model construction and filtering of Pareto optimal solutions from the sampled candidate set.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26477_figures/2604.26477_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: a) Convergence to optimal hypervolume for original and noisy SB. b) Distribution of numbers of samples needed by NI-SB to discover whole Pareto set of the 3-objective problem. Values for Quantum Annealing and QAOA according to [22] and [19]. c) Illustration of the Pareto front for two-objective problem with correlation coefficient between the two objectives equal approximately to −0.92.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26477_figures/2604.26477_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Left: Das-Dennis simplex lattice points compared to random Dirichlet distribution for 3-objective problem. Middle: Hypervolume difference for solutions found by exact solver at each number of fixed weight vectors. Right: Number of Pareto-optimal solutions found by exact solver at each number of fixed weight vectors.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.26477_figures/2604.26477_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: Total sample size required to reach optimal hypervolume, depending on different amplitude of noise injected in the algorithm. Left: NI-dSB. Right: NI-bSB. Values for QA and QAOA from references [22, 19].</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 6</summary>

<img src="2604.26477_figures/2604.26477_fig6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 6: Comparison of SimCIM and NI-SB. The y-axis shows the Hypervolume difference (HVmax −HVt + 1) in logarithmic scale, and the x-axis represents the end-to-end run time in seconds.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of efficiently solving Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO) problems, specifically the Multi-Objective MaxCut (MO-MaxCut) problem, by comparing quantum-inspired algorithms against quantum hardware and classical heuristics.

**Main result.** GPU-accelerated Quantum-Annealing-Inspired Algorithms (QAIAs), specifically a noise-injected version of Simulated Bifurcation, outperform both quantum annealers and gate-based quantum circuits in end-to-end runtime and sampling speed, while also surpassing industry-leading classical heuristics.

**Method.** The authors utilize GPU-accelerated Simulated Bifurcation (SB) algorithms, specifically introducing a Gaussian-noise variant (NI-bSB and NI-dSB) to increase sample diversity and coverage of the Pareto front.

**Summary.** This paper re-evaluates the practical utility of quantum processors for multi-objective optimization by accounting for the full end-to-end computational overhead. It demonstrates that GPU-based quantum-inspired algorithms, such as noise-injected Simulated Bifurcation, are significantly faster and more efficient than current quantum annealers and gate-based circuits. By providing a superior classical baseline, the study highlights the high bar that near-term quantum hardware must overcome to demonstrate practical advantage in combinatorial optimization.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The problem is modeled using an Ising Hamiltonian framework on an undirected graph, where multiple conflicting objectives are addressed via a scalarized Hamiltonian using the weighted sum method and a Das-Dennis simplex-lattice design for weight generation.

**Key observables.** Hypervolume (HV) indicator, end-to-end runtime, sampling speed (velocity), sample diversity, and Pareto dominance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Noise amplitude (alpha), node count (n), edge density (d), batch size, and the number of objectives (K).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The evaluation assumes the effectiveness of the weighted sum method for scalarization and relies on the selection of a reference point for the Hypervolume calculation.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare the runtime ratio of NI-SB against classical baselines, show the convergence of hypervolume for noisy vs. original SB, illustrate the distribution of samples needed to recover the Pareto set, and demonstrate the growth of the hypervolume difference relative to weight vector count.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the MO-MaxCut problem, defines the Ising Hamiltonian and scalarization method, describes the implementation of the noise-injected Simulated Bifurcation algorithm, presents a comparative benchmark against quantum and classical solvers, and concludes with performance analysis across different scales.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Combinatorial optimization is widely regarded as a primary application for near-term quantum processors, although a definitive demonstration of the practical quantum advantage remains elusive. Recent studies have reported that both gate-based quantum circuits and quantum annealers can outperform state-of-the-art classical heuristics on multi-objective optimization (MO-MaxCut) problems. However, these studies did not fully account for the substantial pre- and post-processing overheads intrinsic to quantum solvers, leading to incomplete comparisons between quantum and classical approaches. In this work, we re-examine the same benchmark suite using GPU-based quantum-annealing-inspired algorithms (QAIAs), which, analogously to quantum processors, generate probabilistic samples and thus serve as formidable classical contenders. Our results show that QAIAs can sample candidate solutions approximately two orders of magnitude faster than previously studied quantum processors. In terms of end-to-end runtime, QAIAs also surpass industry-leading classical solvers, thereby establishing themselves as the superior performers among the quantum and classical solvers evaluated thus far for the MO-MaxCut instances.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Parameterized Quantum Circuits as Feature Maps: Representation Quality and Readout Effects in Multispectral Land-Cover Classification](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26675v1)

**Authors:** Ralntion Komini, Aikaterini Mandilara, Georgios Maragkopoulos, Dimitris Syvridis  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26675v1>

<sub>Figure 6 shows the average VQC test accuracy as the number of qubits is increased from</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 7</summary>

<img src="2604.26675_figures/2604.26675_fig7.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 6. Average VQC test accuracy versus qubit count on PCA-32 inputs, averaged over all 45</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating the effectiveness of Variational Quantum Classifiers (VQCs) as nonlinear feature maps for multispectral land-cover classification and determining how the choice of readout mechanism affects performance.

**Main result.** While VQCs with linear readout do not outperform classical RBF-SVMs, the trained quantum feature map significantly improves performance when used within a kernel-based decision framework (SVM-QK).

**Method.** The authors perform a systematic one-vs-one evaluation on the EuroSAT-MS dataset, comparing VQCs and Quantum Kernel SVMs against classical baselines like Logistic Regression, SVMs, and Neural Networks using noiseless state-vector simulations.

**Summary.** This paper explores using parameterized quantum circuits as learned nonlinear feature maps for satellite imagery classification. It demonstrates that while standard quantum classifiers struggle to beat classical models, the learned quantum representation is highly effective when integrated into a classical kernel-based SVM. The study also identifies a saturation effect in performance as the number of qubits increases. Ultimately, it suggests that the synergy between quantum representations and classical decision mechanisms is key to potential quantum utility.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A parameterized quantum circuit (PQC) utilizing a data re-uploading architecture with 1 to 7 qubits, featuring an initialization block, data-dependent rotations, and alternating entangling layers.

**Key observables.** Pauli-Z expectation values used to construct classical feature vectors.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of qubits (1-7), number of trainable parameters, and PCA-reduced input dimensions.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes noiseless state-vector simulation, omitting hardware noise, finite-shot effects, and full spatial image structures.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the VQC architecture; Figure 2 details the 4-qubit PQC structure; Figure 3 compares per-class accuracy of VQC, NN, and LogReg; Figure 4 compares different SVM kernels; Figure 5 compares VQC linear head vs. SVM-QK; Figure 6 shows accuracy scaling with qubit count.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the VQC architecture and problem, describes the PQC model and data preprocessing, presents a comparative analysis of different readout strategies and classical baselines, evaluates scalability via a qubit-count sweep, and concludes with limitations and future directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate variational quantum classifiers (VQCs) for land-cover classification from multispectral satellite imagery, adopting a feature-map perspective in which the quantum circuit defines a nonlinear data embedding while the readout determines how this representation is exploited. Using the EuroSAT-MS dataset, we perform a systematic one-vs-one evaluation across all class pairs under a controlled experimental protocol, comparing classical baselines (logistic regression, SVMs, neural networks) with VQCs employing both linear readout and quantum-kernel SVM strategies. Our results show that, while VQCs with linear readout do not outperform strong classical baselines such as RBF-SVM, the same trained quantum feature map can significantly improve performance when reused within a kernel-based decision framework. A qubit-count sweep further reveals saturation effects consistent with the mismatch between exponential Hilbert space dimension and linear parameter scaling. Overall, our findings highlight that the effectiveness of quantum models depends critically on the interplay between representation and readout, and that meaningful gains may arise from combining learned quantum feature maps with classical decision mechanisms rather than seeking direct replacement of classical models.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26609"></a>
### [Probabilistic Condition, Decision and Path Coverage of Circuit-based Quantum Programs](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26609v1)

**Authors:** Daniel Fortunato, José Campos, Rui Abreu  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26609v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26609_figures/2604.26609_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Original, transpiled, and instrumented circuit of the Swap test written in Qiskit in Figure 1.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26609_figures/2604.26609_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Transpilation and instrumentation of the Qiskit code in Figure 1.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Traditional classical software testing metrics like decision and path coverage are ineffective for quantum circuits due to the lack of classical control flow and the probabilistic nature of quantum states.

**Main result.** The authors proposed six quantum-tailored coverage criteria and a tool called QaCoCo, finding that while condition and decision coverage are often high, path coverage is significantly lower due to path explosion in multi-controlled gates.

**Method.** The researchers developed the QaCoCo tool to instrument OpenQASM circuits via Qiskit, using gate decomposition and non-destructive simulator instructions to track control qubit states and compute structural and probabilistic coverage.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the challenge of verifying quantum software by introducing new coverage metrics tailored for circuit-based quantum programs. By treating controlled gates as quantum branches, the authors propose ways to measure condition, decision, and path coverage, even accounting for the probabilistic nature of quantum states. They introduce the QaCoCo tool to automate this process and demonstrate through large-scale benchmarking that multi-controlled gates cause significant path explosion. Ultimately, the work provides a framework for improving the reliability and testing adequacy of quantum algorithms.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on circuit-based quantum programs implemented via Qiskit and OpenQASM, specifically analyzing the behavior of controlled gates (e.g., CX, CCX, CSWAP) as quantum analogues to classical branches.

**Key observables.** Condition, decision, and path coverage; probabilistic variants using Jain's fairness index; expectation values of qubits; and mutation scores for fault detection.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Circuit depth, number of qubits, number of controlled gates, and the probability of specific measurement outcomes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The current instrumentation method relies on Qiskit Aer simulator instructions (save_expectation_value), which requires access to the full statevector and is not yet directly applicable to real hardware without modification.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a Swap test implementation as a motivational example; Figure 2 illustrates the transformation pipeline from original to instrumented circuits; Figure 4 demonstrates the retrieval of expectation values and probabilities from the simulator.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of quantum testing adequacy, proposes new structural and probabilistic coverage metrics, describes the QaCoCo tool implementation, presents an empirical evaluation using MQT Bench, and performs mutation testing to assess the correlation between coverage and fault detection.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Coverage criteria play a central role in assessing test adequacy in classical software, yet their effectiveness for quantum programs remains poorly understood and largely unexplored. In this paper, we propose six quantum-tailored criteria - condition, decision, and path coverage, and their probabilistic variants - adapted from their classical counterparts. We present QaCoCo, a tool that computes these criteria for circuit-based quantum programs. We empirically evaluate these criteria on a large and diverse set of 540 circuits and analyze the coverage achieved. Our results show that while circuits frequently achieve high condition and decision coverage (97.56% and 97.63%, on average), path coverage remains limited (71.84%), particularly in the presence of multi-controlled gates, which induce extreme path explosion and coverage imbalance. Moreover, to account for the probabilistic nature of quantum circuits, we introduce probabilistic coverage, which augments structural coverage with a confidence measure (88.87%, 88.65%, and 37.18% for condition, decision, and path coverage, respectively, on average). Finally, through mutation testing, we find weak or no correlation between fault detection and structural coverage, consistent with observations in classical computing.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26040"></a>
### [QAOA Parameter Transfer for Hypergraphs](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26040v1)

**Authors:** Lucas T. Braydwood, Phillip C. Lotshaw  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26040v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26040_figures/2604.26040_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2: The average approximation ratio across all our hy- pergraph instances as a function of QAOA circuit depth, p. We plot the results for the best found QAOA angles, as well as reweighting of reference angles from the literature [10] using the γ reweighting from Eq. (20) with and without the β reweighting from Eq. (18)</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26040_figures/2604.26040_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3: The average approximation ratio across just the graph instances, including only terms with locality 2 or less, as a function of QAOA circuit depth, p. We plot the results for the best found QAOA angles, as well as reweighting of reference angles from the literature [10] using the γ reweighting from Eq. (20) with and without the β reweighting from Eq. (18)</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of the variational optimization loop in QAOA and the lack of parameter transfer/reweighting rules for higher-locality hypergraph structures.

**Main result.** The authors analytically derive new parameter reweighting rules for the mixer Hamiltonian (beta parameters) to transfer QAOA parameters between different hypergraph localities, significantly improving the approximation ratio.

**Method.** Analytical derivation using a small-gamma expansion and a Berge-acyclic hypergraph assumption, supplemented by numerical simulations using the L-BFGS algorithm and bootstrapping.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the computational bottleneck of the variational loop in QAOA by proposing a method to transfer optimized parameters from one problem instance to another. While previous work focused on reweighting cost Hamiltonian parameters, this work introduces a new analytical rule for reweighting the mixer Hamiltonian parameters when moving to hypergraphs. The proposed method significantly improves the approximation ratio for high-locality hypergraphs. The results remain robust even when the underlying assumption of acyclic structures is violated.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) applied to Max-k-XORSAT problems on hypergraphs, using a cost Hamiltonian with hyperedge weights and a standard transverse field mixer Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Approximation ratio (achieved energy divided by true ground state energy) and the expectation value of the cost function.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Variational parameters gamma and beta, circuit depth p, hyperedge locality k, and hyperedge weights w.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analytical derivation assumes Berge-acyclic hypergraphs (cycle-free), low circuit depth, and a small-gamma expansion.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates Berge cycles in hypergraphs; Figure 2 compares the approximation ratio across different QAOA methods showing improvement with beta reweighting; Figure 3 shows the performance for low-locality (k <= 2) hypergraphs.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QAOA parameter transfer problem, defines the hypergraph model and Hamiltonian, provides the analytical derivation for beta reweighting, presents numerical validation across various hypergraph localities, and discusses limitations and future directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Variational Quantum Algorithms, including the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA), have shown promise in solving optimization problems but rely on costly variational loops that can themselves be hard optimization problems. Many methods have been proposed to mitigate this variational cost, with one of the most common being parameter transfer and concentration where variational parameters for one problem instance or for an average over problem instances can be used as a good set of parameters for another instance. Methods exist for reweighting these parameters based off graph degree and edge weights, but there has been little work on how to do this reweighting to handle higher locality problems where the graph structure turns into a hypergraph structure. In this paper, we analytically derive parameter reweighting rules to transfer parameters between different locality hypergraphs, resulting in a reweighting for the mixing terms in the Hamiltonian which have previously not been considered. These analytics rely on three cycle-free and low-circuit-depth assumptions, but numerics indicate that the results can be used even when these assumptions are not satisfied. The numerics obtain high quality results across a diverse set of hypergraphs with locality less than or equal to five, improving on previous relations that do not reweight the mixing terms.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26018"></a>
### [QERNEL: a Scalable Large Electron Model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26018v1)

**Authors:** Khachatur Nazaryan, Liang Fu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26018v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26018_figures/2604.26018_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Architecture of QERNEL. (a) Overall conditional wavefunction pipeline: electron features are embedded into electronic streams, processed by stacked equivariant blocks, projected to generalized orbitals, and combined into deter- minants. The Hamiltonian context is threaded alongside the electron streams and repeatedly injected throughout the depth of the network. (b) Mixture-of-experts block with a router and an shared expert. (c) Grouped-query attention, where multiple query heads share key and value heads for improved efficiency. (d) FiLM conditioning, which injects the context into the electronic streams through channel-wise shifts and rescalings.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26018_figures/2604.26018_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Inferred real space densities for a 150-electron system obtained with QERNEL in a fixed-context, non-foundational setting. (a) Fermi-liquid regime, λ = 0.7. (b) Generalized Wigner-crystal regime, λ = 1.5. The isolated minor defects in the density profile are a computational artifact and are not interpreted as a physical excitation.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26018_figures/2604.26018_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Benchmarking the efficiency of QERNEL. (a) final variational energy per electron versus parameter count for 18- and 24-electron systems. QERNEL reaches the same accu- racy regime as the PsiFormer baseline [13] with roughly 6–7× fewer parameters. For sake of presentation, the 24e data was shifted down by 0.08meV (b),(c) Training curves of QERNEL vs baseline. (b) at comparable parameter count, QERNEL converges about 6× faster and with improved stability than the baseline. (c) a 240K-parameter QERNEL converges to a slightly lower energy as a 1.1M-parameter baseline.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26018_figures/2604.26018_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Inference of the foundational-model for capturing the phase transition. (a) Interaction energy per electron as a function of moir´e strength λ, inferred from a single trained conditional QERNEL ansatz for 54-electron system. The data separate into two approximately linear branches whose extrapolated mismatch near λ ≈1.05 indicates a sharp reorganization of the ground state. (b) Total energy per electron across the same parameter range. In contrast to the interaction energy, the total energy remains smooth and continuous through the transition region, as expected for a finite-size system. (c),(d) Inferred real-space densities on the two sides of the transition for 96-electron system....</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Transitioning from single-instance neural wavefunction solvers that require retraining for every new Hamiltonian to 'foundation models' that can generalize across entire families of Hamiltonians efficiently.

**Main result.** The QERNEL model achieves comparable accuracy to much larger baselines with 6-7x fewer parameters and 6x faster convergence, successfully capturing the phase transition between Fermi liquid and Wigner crystal states in a single model.

**Method.** A variational Monte Carlo approach using a neural wavefunction architecture inspired by LLMs, incorporating Grouped-Query Attention, Mixture-of-Experts, and FiLM-based parameter conditioning.

**Summary.** This paper introduces QERNEL, a 'Large Electron Model' designed to act as a foundation model for many-electron systems. By using modern transformer-inspired techniques like Mixture-of-Experts and Grouped-Query Attention, the model can solve entire families of Hamiltonians without retraining. It demonstrates the ability to predict phase transitions in moiré materials, such as the transition from a Fermi liquid to a Wigner crystal, with much higher computational efficiency than previous neural wavefunction methods.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of interacting electrons in semiconductor moiré heterobilayers, described by a Hamiltonian including kinetic energy, a moiré potential, and Coulomb interaction.

**Key observables.** Interaction energy per electron, total energy per electron, and real-space charge density profiles.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Moiré potential strength (lambda), effective mass (m* = 0.35me), moiré lattice constant (8.031 nm), and filling factor (nu = 2/3).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes the ability to represent continuous Fermi wavefunctions via a linear combination of Slater determinants.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the QERNEL architecture; Fig 2 compares energy accuracy, parameter count, and convergence speed against the PsiFormer baseline; Fig 3 displays real-space density profiles for the liquid and crystal phases.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QERNEL architecture, details the implementation of MoE and GQA, describes the application to moiré systems, presents benchmarking results against existing neural wavefunctions, and demonstrates the capture of phase transitions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce QERNEL, a foundational neural wavefunction that variationally solves families of parameterized many-electron Hamiltonians and captures their ground states throughout parameter space within a single model. QERNEL combines FiLM-based parameter conditioning with scale-efficient architectural elements -- mixture of experts and grouped-query attention, substantially improving expressivity at low computational cost. We apply QERNEL to interacting electrons in semiconductor moiré heterobilayers, training a single weight-shared model for systems of up to 150 electrons. By solving the many-electron Schrödinger equation conditioned on moiré potential depth, QERNEL captures both quantum liquid and crystal states and discovers the sharp phase transition between them, marked by abrupt changes in interaction energy and charge density. Our work establishes a foundation model for moiré quantum materials and a scalable architecture toward a Large Electron Model for solids.

</details>

**Main problem.** Achieving a quadratic speedup in the query complexity of discrete simulation optimization within a fixed-confidence (epsilon, delta-PAC) framework.

**Main result.** The developed SOGAS algorithm achieves a quadratic speedup in query complexity relative to the number of candidate solutions, reducing complexity from O(|X|) to O(sqrt(|X|/epsilon)).

**Method.** The algorithm uses a binary-search-based framework (OptimalRegion) to narrow the performance interval, a quantum flagging algorithm (FlagOracle) to label near-optimal solutions, and amplitude amplification to extract the solution.

**Summary.** This paper introduces SOGAS, a quantum algorithm designed to optimize stochastic, discrete simulations. By leveraging Grover-based techniques and quantum mean estimation, the authors demonstrate a quadratic speedup in finding near-optimal solutions compared to classical Monte Carlo methods. The work provides both theoretical guarantees for error control and numerical evidence of quantum advantage across various noise distributions.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A quantum simulation oracle that prepares a coherent superposition over candidate solutions and encodes random noise and simulation outputs into a three-register quantum state (solution, noise, and output registers).

**Key observables.** The optimal mean performance y(x*) and the proportion of solutions meeting a specific performance threshold.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Optimality gap (epsilon), failure probability (delta), and the size of the discrete solution set (|X|).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes the existence of a quantum simulation oracle capable of coherent superposition and that performance values are bounded within [0, 1].

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows Grover iteration mechanics; Figure 2 illustrates performance interval partitioning; Figure 3 depicts the binary search process; Figures 4-6 show empirical query complexity scaling and performance across different probability distributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of discrete simulation optimization, defines a new quantum simulation oracle, presents the SOGAS algorithm (comprising subroutines for region identification, flagging, and amplification), provides a rigorous theoretical proof of complexity and correctness, and concludes with numerical validation using Qiskit.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum computing has advanced rapidly in recent years and has shown advantages in a variety of domains. In this paper, we investigate its potential for discrete simulation optimization in the fixed-confidence setting, a fundamental problem in the simulation literature. We first introduce a quantum simulation oracle that prepares a coherent superposition over all candidate solutions and provides the foundation for quantum algorithm design. Based on this oracle, we develop the first Grover-search-based quantum algorithm for discrete simulation optimization, called SOGAS. In particular, SOGAS uses a binary-search framework to progressively eliminate suboptimal solutions while carefully controlling the error probability, and eventually identifies a set of near-optimal solutions. We prove that SOGAS returns a near-optimal solution with probability at least the prescribed confidence level and achieves a quadratic speedup in the dependence of query complexity on the number of candidate solutions. Numerical experiments further show that SOGAS substantially outperforms classical benchmarks and provide empirical evidence for quantum advantage in discrete simulation optimization.

</details>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates how coupling a 2D Quantum Hall liquid to 3+1 dimensional dynamical electromagnetism affects fundamental properties like Hall resistivity, Hall conductivity, and quasiparticle statistics.

**Main result.** The Hall resistivity remains robustly quantized, while the longitudinal resistivity becomes non-zero and the Hall conductivity, fractional charge, and statistical angle are all renormalized by a common factor related to the fine-structure constant.

**Method.** The authors use a Ginzburg-Landau-Chern-Simons (GLCS) hydrodynamic theory and a composite boson representation, integrating out the 3D electromagnetic vector potential to derive an effective 2+1D action.

**Summary.** This paper explores the impact of 3D electromagnetic fluctuations on 2D Quantum Hall liquids. It demonstrates that while the Hall resistance remains quantized, the coupling to 3D photons introduces longitudinal resistance and renormalizes the Hall conductivity and quasiparticle statistics. The study provides a theoretical basis for why Hall resistance quantization can persist even when the system is coupled to a dissipative environment.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A 2D electron fluid supporting a Quantum Hall state, embedded in an infinite 3D space filled with a weakly conducting medium governed by Maxwell action.

**Key observables.** Hall resistivity (rho_H), longitudinal resistivity (rho_L), Hall conductivity (sigma_H), fractional charge (e*), and anyonic statistical angle (theta_s).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Filling fraction (nu), fine-structure constant (alpha), Klitzing constant (R_K), 3D conductivity (sigma_tilde), and the vacuum impedance (Z_0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes a low-frequency/DC limit, neglects density fluctuations and quasiparticle effects, and assumes a local, momentum-independent dielectric response.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of electromagnetic coupling, defines the hydrodynamic and Maxwell actions, performs the integration of the 3D gauge field, derives the effective conductivity tensor, and discusses the renormalization of quasiparticle properties and experimental implications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the effect on a Quantum Hall (QH) liquid of its coupling to 3+1 dimensional dynamical electromagnetism, which renders the system gapless. We calculate both the Hall and longitudinal resistances, $ρ_H$ and $ρ_L$, in the context of a minimal model of the electromagnetic environment, with a small three dimensional conductivity ${\tildeσ}$, that allows for a counter-flow current. In the thermodynamic limit, we show that $ρ_H$ is quantized, while $ρ_L$ approaches a non-zero limit, $ρ_L \sim α\, R_K$, where $α$ and $R_K=2π/e^2$ are the fine structure and the Klitzing constant. In contrast, the QH conductance, $σ_H$, is smaller than the expected quantized value by a correction $\sim α^2/R_K$. The electromagnetic interaction also generates corrections of order $α^2$ to the quasiparticle charges and statistics, in a way that is consistent with general arguments based on gauge invariance. In addition, we present an intuitive argument that relates the flux attachment associated with the composite boson representation of the electron liquid to the empirically observed %persistence of approximate quantization of $ρ_H$, even in circumstances in which $ρ_L$, and the deviation of $σ_H$ from its quantized value, are substantial.

</details>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 7</summary>

<img src="2604.26213_figures/2604.26213_fig7.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 7: A Vine Structured Circuit for 3 &amp; 4 Dimensional Distributions. In the case that d = 3, we have a 3k qubit memory with precisely 2 trees in the vine. In the case that d = 4, we have a 4k qubit memory with precisely 3 trees in the vine. (B) shows the D-vine we used in experiments, the vine is also depicted in Fig. 1.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 8</summary>

<img src="2604.26213_figures/2604.26213_fig8.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 8: Progressive TVD of the trained QNN projected onto the X axis for the uncorrelated 3D Gaussian. Each of the 3 dimensions is discretized over 3 qubits, with marginal loading as step 0 and the next 3 steps for each BEB of the 3 edges in the vine. Ti,j corresponds to the j-th edge of the i-th tree.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 9</summary>

<img src="2604.26213_figures/2604.26213_fig9.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 9: Projections of the best tested number of layers to achieve convergence on a 3D stock price return distribution. The top row shows the trained pdf and the bottom row shows the original pdf. Fig. 12B depicts throughout the progressive vine training.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 10</summary>

<img src="2604.26213_figures/2604.26213_fig10.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 10: TVD of the progressive trained QNN projected onto the X, Y axis for the 4D Gaussian.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The challenge of efficiently loading high-dimensional continuous probability distributions into quantum states without suffering from the curse of dimensionality or vanishing gradients.

**Main result.** The Qvine ansatz achieves high-quality distribution loading for Gaussian and empirical financial data, with circuit depth scaling linearly for D-vines and quadratically for R-vines.

**Method.** A hierarchical quantum circuit architecture based on vine copula decompositions, utilizing Bivariate Entangling Blocks (BEBs) and a progressive training methodology.

**Summary.** This paper introduces Qvine, a new quantum circuit architecture designed to load high-dimensional probability distributions into quantum computers. By mimicking the hierarchical structure of vine copula decompositions, the method avoids the exponential complexity typically associated with unstructured circuits. The authors demonstrate that the circuit depth scales efficiently (linearly or quadratically) with the number of dimensions. They successfully validate the approach using both synthetic Gaussian distributions and real-world financial stock return data.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A quantum circuit architecture (Qvine) that represents a d-dimensional random vector by discretizing each dimension into k-bit registers, totaling dk qubits, and uses the Special Orthogonal Group SO(2^n) to ensure real-valued wavefunctions.

**Key observables.** Total Variation Distance (TVD), Infidelity (1 - Fidelity), and axis projections of the learned probability density function.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimension (d), resolution (k), number of ansatz layers, and vine structure type (R-vine, C-vine, or D-vine).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes the continuous density can be accurately represented via discretization into 2^k bins and that resolution k grows only logarithmically with dimension for a fixed error tolerance.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the vine structure (D-vine), the mapping between copula decomposition and circuit architecture, the discretization process, the construction of SORB and BEB blocks, and performance metrics like TVD reduction during progressive training and PDF comparisons for stock data.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of high-dimensional data loading, presents the Qvine ansatz based on vine copula theory, provides mathematical proofs for the circuit's expressibility and scalability using Lie algebra, describes the progressive training algorithm, and validates the method through numerical simulations on Gaussian and financial datasets.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Loading high dimensional distributions is an important task for utilizing quantum computers on applications ranging from machine learning to finance. The high dimensionality leads to a curse of dimensionality, representing a d-dimensional distribution with k resolution requires dk qubits and an unstructured parameterized circuit would express a unitary in an exponential operator space in the number of qubits, leading to vanishing gradients and poor convergence guarantees even at high depth.   Vine copula decompositions are widely used to represent high dimensional distributions classically, showing high quality approximation in many important applications, such as financial modeling. We present Qvine, a vine structured ansatz for quantum circuits, that mirrors the vine decomposition to construct scalable quantum circuits with efficient trainability while achieving similarly high quality approximation for amplitude encoding distributions. For regular vines (R-vines), we show that the circuit depth scales at most quadratic in the dimension of the distribution, while for D-vines, as well as many practical R-vines, the circuit depth scales linear in the dimension. For 3-dimensional and 4-dimensional Gaussians and empirical joint stock price return distributions for selected stocks, our experiments show Qvines achieve high quality loading.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26589"></a>
### [Rare but Resilient: Dispersal diversity buffers species vulnerability](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26589v1)

**Authors:** Davide Bernardi, Giorgio Nicoletti, Prajwal Padmanabha, Samir Suweis, Sandro Azaele, Simon A. Levin, Andrea Rinaldo, Amos Maritan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26589v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Main problem.** Predicting species persistence and vulnerability within ecological communities without needing to estimate difficult-to-measure mechanistic parameters or species traits.

**Main result.** The development of a 'competitive balance' metric shows that greater heterogeneity in dispersal strategies reduces species vulnerability for a given abundance.

**Method.** A general framework grounded in the statistical physics of complex systems that integrates dispersal diversity and interspecific interaction structures into a single measurable quantity.

**Summary.** The paper introduces a new framework based on the statistical physics of complex systems to assess species vulnerability in ecosystems. By defining a 'competitive balance' metric, the authors show that the diversity of dispersal strategies acts as a buffer against extinction. This metric is particularly useful because it can be calculated directly from spatial abundance data, bypassing the need for complex parameter estimation. The findings were validated using real-world data from tropical and temperate forests.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** An ecological community model incorporating spatial abundance data, dispersal strategies, and interspecific interaction structures.

**Key observables.** Competitive balance, species abundance, and dispersal strategy diversity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dispersal strategies, interspecific interaction structure, and spatial abundance.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes that species vulnerability can be inferred from spatial abundance data and that the competitive balance captures the essential dynamics of survival.

**Paper structure.** Introduction to the challenge of species persistence, presentation of the statistical physics-based framework, introduction of the competitive balance metric, validation using tropical and temperate forest data, and discussion of broader ecosystem applications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Predicting species persistence within ecological communities is a fundamental challenge for both empirical and theoretical ecology. Existing methods span from mechanistic models, whose parameters are difficult to estimate from data, to statistical tools whose context-specific parameters are less interpretable. Here, we present a general framework, grounded in the statistical physics of complex systems, that integrates the key processes governing species survival into a single measurable quantity: the competitive balance. This metric quantifies a focal species' vulnerability beyond its abundance by incorporating the diversity of dispersal strategies and the structure of interspecific interactions within the community. Crucially, it can be inferred from spatial abundance data, thus circumventing the need to estimate species traits or dispersal parameters. Our results reveal that greater heterogeneity in dispersal strategies reduces vulnerability for a given abundance. Although we validate the framework using tropical and temperate forest data, it can be applied to a range of different ecosystems, providing a systemic and interpretable tool for assessing a context-dependent species vulnerability that accounts for its interactions with the entire community.

</details>

**Main problem.** The challenge of automating the discovery of optimal variational quantum circuit (VQC) structures to improve the performance of Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) while managing the exponential growth of the search space.

**Main result.** The paper provides a comprehensive review of advancements in QAS, demonstrating that automated search methods can find high-performing, hardware-efficient circuits that outperform manually designed ansätze.

**Method.** A survey of various search paradigms including Evolutionary Algorithms, Bayesian Optimization, Reinforcement Learning, Monte Carlo Tree Search, and Differentiable QAS.

**Summary.** This paper reviews the recent progress in Quantum Architecture Search (QAS), a field dedicated to automating the design of quantum circuits for near-term quantum computers. It explores various machine learning-driven approaches, such as reinforcement learning and Bayesian optimization, to find circuits that are both effective and hardware-efficient. The review also highlights strategies to reduce the massive computational cost of evaluating new architectures. Ultimately, it provides a roadmap for future research into scalable and hardware-native quantum circuit design.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The research focuses on Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) such as VQE and QAOA, operating on NISQ-era hardware with specific constraints like qubit connectivity and native gate sets.

**Key observables.** Circuit expressibility, trainability (gradient statistics), entanglement, circuit complexity (depth, gate count), and fidelity/energy error.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Variational parameters (theta), discrete circuit structure (A), and hardware-specific constraints like connectivity graphs and gate sets.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Many current QAS methods are limited to small-scale systems (a few to a dozen qubits) and assume that performance can be estimated via proxies or latent representations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QAS problem, categorizes search space strategies and paradigms (discrete, differentiable, and latent-space), discusses efficiency enhancement techniques (weight sharing, predictors, proxies), and concludes with future research directions and challenges.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Variational quantum algorithms (VQAs) constitute a prominent framework for exploring the capabilities of near-term quantum computers. As the effectiveness of VQAs depends heavily on the design of variational quantum circuits, Quantum Architecture Search (QAS) has emerged as a critical research area to automate the discovery of high-performing circuit structures. This paper reviews key advancements in current research on QAS, including its core concepts, representative methodologies, and applications. The content is structured to ensure broad accessibility for a diverse audience of researchers while preserving the core principles of complex methodologies. In addition, we discuss remaining challenges and suggest potential research directions to offer perspectives on future exploration in this rapidly evolving field.

</details>

**Authors:** Caslav Brukner  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26325v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<img src="2604.26325_figures/2604.26325_page7.jpg" width="500"><br>

**Main problem.** The conceptual tension between the intuitive 'wave picture' of the Schrodinger equation and its actual ontological status, specifically the risk of treating the wave function as a literal physical wave in configuration space.

**Main result.** The author argues that while the wave picture is a powerful tool for discovery, it should be demoted ontologically to avoid the misconception that the wave function or quantum fields are classical-like physical entities.

**Method.** A historical and conceptual analysis of Schrodinger's work, framed by the intellectual influences of Mach and Boltzmann, and supplemented by a critique of modern interpretations using Bell-type no-go theorems.

**Summary.** This essay reflects on the 100th anniversary of the Schrodinger equation, exploring how its visual 'wave' language has both aided and hindered quantum theory. The author argues that the success of this imagery has led to a persistent, misleading attempt to treat the wave function as a real physical wave. By examining the transition from single-particle to many-body systems and QFT, the paper warns against 'wave-function realism.' It concludes that while visual models are essential for calculation, they should not be mistaken for the underlying ontology of the universe.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The paper discusses the Schrodinger equation, N-particle systems in high-dimensional configuration space, and the Schrödinger picture of Quantum Field Theory (QFT) involving wave functionals.

**Key observables.** Probability densities and the distinguishability of quantum states via orthogonality.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The dimensionality of configuration space (3N vs 3D) and the distinction between representational and ontological interpretations.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the validity of Bell-type no-go theorems as a basis for critiquing wave-function realism.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with a historical context of Schrodinger's influences, moves to the mathematical tension between 3D space and configuration space, critiques modern wave-function realism in QFT, and concludes with a philosophical recommendation for physicists.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Schroedinger's equation gave early quantum theory a visual language that looked like physics again: a wave evolving by a linear differential equation. This essay argues that the same success also seeded a recurring impulse to keep quantum theory "classical-looking" by treating the wave function as a physical wave. Schroedinger quickly realized that, for many-particle systems, the wave function is naturally defined on configuration space rather than ordinary physical space, blocking any straightforward reading of it as a literal classical wave. Read through Mach and Boltzmann, who shaped his intellectual outlook most deeply, his achievement appears double-edged: it provided an extraordinarily powerful picture for calculation and discovery, while also warning against taking that picture too literally. I argue that this tension never fully disappeared. It still reappears in modern physics whenever the wave function, or in quantum field theory the field itself, is treated as ontology rather than as part of a representation tied to measurement and observational context, a point sharpened by Bell-type no-go theorems. The centenary moral is: use pictures boldly, but demote them ontologically.

</details>

### [Schwinger-Boson Mean-Field Study of the Anisotropic Kagome Antiferromagnet](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26907v1)

**Authors:** Sankha Subhra Bakshi, Brandon B. Le, Seung-Hun Lee, Gia-Wei Chern  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26907v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

</details>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<sub>FIG. 4. Zero-temperature dynamical structure factor S(k, ω) along Γ–M–K–Γ for representative anisotropies. (a)–(c) Results for the q = 0 ansatz at δ = 0.00, 0.14, 0.26. (d)–(f) Results for the √</sub>

<sub>FIG. 5. Soft-spinon modes and reconstructed spin textures for the anisotropic kagome antiferromagnet. (a–c) Results for the q = 0 ansatz at δ = 0.26: lowest spinon band ω1(k) and condensate textures at Γ and M. (d–f) Results for the √</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how spatial exchange anisotropy affects the stability of quantum spin liquid states and drives the transition to magnetic order in the spin-1/2 kagome antiferromagnet.

**Main result.** Anisotropy leads to a softening of the spinon branch and a downward shift of the two-spinon continuum, eventually causing the spinon gap to close and triggering an instability toward anisotropic magnetic order.

**Method.** The authors use Schwinger-boson mean-field theory (SBMFT) combined with Projective Symmetry Group (PSG) analysis to classify ansätze and calculate spinon dispersions and dynamical structure factors.

**Summary.** This paper explores how breaking the rotational symmetry of the kagome lattice via exchange anisotropy impacts quantum spin liquids. Using Schwinger-boson mean-field theory, the authors show that increasing anisotropy softens spinon excitations and eventually leads to the condensation of spinons into a magnetically ordered state. The study provides a microscopic framework for understanding the transition from spin liquids to magnetic order, which is particularly relevant for interpreting experiments on titanium-based kagome materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A spin-1/2 Heisenberg antiferromagnet on a kagome lattice with spatial anisotropy, where one set of nearest-neighbor bonds (J) is strengthened relative to the others (J').

**Key observables.** Spinon gap, spinon dispersion, dynamical structure factor S(k, omega), and real-space spin textures.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Anisotropy parameter delta = 1 - J'/J, and the gauge flux (0 or pi) through the lattice triangles.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes a Z2 invariant gauge group and focuses on nearest-neighbor ansätze; the spin value is fixed at S = 0.2 to stabilize the gapped spin-liquid saddle point.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the anisotropic kagome model; Figure 2 tracks the evolution of mean-field parameters and the spinon gap; Figure 3 displays spinon dispersions; Figure 5 illustrates the lowest spinon band and reconstructed magnetic textures.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the introduction of the anisotropic model, followed by a PSG analysis to classify symmetry-allowed ansätze, then presents self-consistent SBMFT results regarding spinon dispersions and the dynamical structure factor, and concludes with the reconstruction of magnetic textures at the onset of order.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the effect of spatial exchange anisotropy on the spin-$1/2$ kagome antiferromagnet using Schwinger-boson mean-field theory. The anisotropy is introduced by strengthening the Heisenberg exchange along one set of nearest-neighbor bonds relative to the other two, and is controlled by a parameter $δ$ that measures the deviation from the isotropic limit. Incorporating the reduced lattice symmetry, we construct the corresponding projective-symmetry-group ansätze and focus on representative $0$- and $π$-flux states connected to the conventional $q=0$ and $\sqrt{3}\times\sqrt{3}$ kagome states. We find that anisotropy predominantly reconstructs the low-energy spinon sector, leading to a strong softening of the lowest spinon branch and a downward shift of the two-spinon continuum. At sufficiently large $δ$, the spinon gap closes at ansatz-dependent values, signaling an instability toward spinon condensation and the onset of magnetic order. From the soft Bogoliubov eigenmodes, we reconstruct the associated incipient spin textures and show that the resulting magnetic orders are intrinsically anisotropic, with suppressed moments on strongly coupled bonds and enhanced moments on more weakly connected sites. These results provide a microscopic picture of how exchange anisotropy drives the transition from kagome spin-liquid states to magnetic order, and offer a framework for interpreting recent experiments on anisotropic kagome materials, particularly titanium-based spin-$1/2$ compounds.

</details>

### [Solve Crude Oil Scheduling Problems by Using Quantum-Classical Hybrid Algorithms](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26459v1)

**Authors:** Jian Yang, Bohang Wang, Lina Wang, Jiacheng Chen, Gaoxiang Tang, Zihan Deng, Wending Zhao, Xianfeng Cai  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26459v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26459_figures/2604.26459_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: A schematic diagram of petroleum flow direction in the process of solving crude oil scheduling using benders</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26459_figures/2604.26459_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Target cost results of various algorithms</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26459_figures/2604.26459_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Calculation time of various algorithms</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.26459_figures/2604.26459_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: Traditional algorithms are prone to getting stuck in local optima in special situations</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Optimizing large-scale, NP-hard crude oil scheduling in refineries, which involves coupling discrete logistics events with continuous material flows.

**Main result.** The proposed hybrid quantum-classical framework achieves a 73-80% reduction in operating costs and maintains computational speeds comparable to the Gurobi commercial solver, significantly outperforming traditional metaheuristics.

**Method.** A Benders Decomposition framework that decouples the problem into a discrete Master Problem reformulated as a QUBO and a continuous Subproblem, solved via a hybrid quantum solver with classical refinement.

**Summary.** This paper presents a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm designed to solve complex industrial scheduling problems in oil refineries. By using Benders Decomposition, the authors split a difficult mixed-integer problem into a discrete part solvable via QUBO on quantum hardware and a continuous part handled classically. The method demonstrates significant cost savings and efficiency compared to standard heuristic approaches like Genetic Algorithms. It provides a practical roadmap for applying NISQ-era quantum computing to large-scale logistics and supply chain optimization.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A multi-stage refinery network consisting of vessels, storage tanks, blending tanks, and Crude Distillation Units (CDUs) modeled using a Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) framework.

**Key observables.** Total operating costs (including demurrage, switching, and inventory costs) and computational execution time.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 15 multi-scale test instances; complexity up to 5,350 discrete variables, 16,550 continuous variables, and 51,931 constraints.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes the refinery's physical limits can be captured by the defined constraints and uses 'constraint softening' via slack variables to encode complex industrial constraints into a QUBO format.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the physical refinery network; Figure 2 shows petroleum flow direction; Figures 3-5 illustrate cost/time performance comparisons and the failure of heuristics to avoid local optima.

**Paper structure.** The paper defines the industrial scheduling problem, introduces the Benders Decomposition and QUBO reformulation, details the hybrid quantum-classical algorithm, presents experimental results on multi-scale instances, and compares performance against classical solvers and metaheuristics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The optimization of front-end crude oil scheduling is a critical determinant of refinery profitability and operational stability. However, the coupling of discrete logistics events (e.g., vessel berthing) with continuous material flows (e.g., pipeline transfers) renders this problem an NP-hard Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) challenge, often intractable for classical solvers at industrial scales. This study proposes a novel hybrid quantum-classical framework to address these computational bottlenecks. We employ Benders Decomposition to decouple the monolithic model into a discrete Master Problem (MP) and a continuous Subproblem (SP). To exploit the search capabilities of quantum computing, the MP is reformulated as a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) model and solved via a hybrid quantum solver, while the SP enforces mass balance and quality constraints through iterative optimality and feasibility cuts. Extensive experiments on 15 multi-scale instances demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly outperforms traditional metaheuristics (e.g., Genetic Algorithms, Tabu Search), reducing total operating costs by approximately 73--80% and achieving computational speeds comparable to state-of-the-art commercial solvers (Gurobi). By effectively leveraging global optimality cuts, the method overcomes the tendency of heuristic approaches to trap in local optima, providing a robust and scalable solution for complex refinery logistics.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26125"></a>
### [Topological transitions in spin-ice induced by geometrical constraints](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26125v1)

**Authors:** R. A. Borzi, E. S. Loscar, S. A. Grigera  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26125v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26125_figures/2604.26125_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. a- A conventional, cubic unit cell for the pyrochlore lattice, made of one set of tetrahedra pointing down (marked in gray) and another one pointing up. The two-in/two-out spin configuration is compatible with a saturated state both for B ∥[111] or B ∥[110]. For B ∥[111] the lattice can be thought as made of spins on triangular planes (dashed arrows) and spins on Kagome planes (dark triangles). For B ∥[110], it can be thought as composed of α−chains (lines of black spins, with a component in the direction of [110]) and β−chains (lines of red spins perpendicular to the field). The cell constants a, b, and c are indicated in magenta. They allow for periodic boundary conditions...</sub>

</details>

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating how geometrical constraints and finite transverse dimensions can stabilize and induce discrete topological phase transitions in spin ice, which would otherwise be smooth crossovers in the thermodynamic limit.

**Main result.** The study shows that constraining the transverse dimensions of a spin-ice system quantizes the number of system-spanning string excitations, leading to a cascade of discrete magnetization steps and sharp peaks in specific heat and susceptibility.

**Method.** The authors use Monte Carlo simulations to characterize the sequence of transitions and scaling behavior, complemented by an analytical treatment based on an entropy-energy balance in reduced dimensions.

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the shape of a sample can fundamentally change the nature of phase transitions in frustrated magnets. By restricting the transverse dimensions of a spin-ice system, the authors show that continuous crossovers are replaced by a cascade of discrete, topological transitions. These transitions are driven by the quantized entry of string-like excitations into the system. The study provides both numerical evidence via Monte Carlo simulations and an analytical framework to predict these transitions based on entropy-energy competition.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A nearest-neighbor spin-ice model on a pyrochlore lattice subjected to magnetic fields applied along the [111] and [110] directions.

**Key observables.** Magnetization (M), specific heat (Cv), magnetic susceptibility (chi), monopole density, and the number of system-spanning strings.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field strength (h), temperature (T), longitudinal dimension (L_parallel), and transverse dimension (L_perp).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analytical prediction for critical fields assumes a semi-infinite system and may break down for very small system sizes due to the influence of closed-loop excitations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the pyrochlore lattice and string excitations; Figure 2 shows magnetization steps and specific heat peaks for different geometries; Figure 6 compares smooth vs. jump-like behavior in different field directions; Figure 7 validates analytical predictions for critical fields.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of geometric stabilization of transitions, describes the spin-ice model and geometry, presents Monte Carlo results for magnetization and heat capacity, provides an analytical derivation for critical fields, and discusses scaling and the thermodynamic limit.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the nearest-neighbor spin-ice model subjected to a magnetic field applied along the global [111] and [110] directions, focusing on the role of sample geometry in stabilizing topological phase transitions. While no Kasteleyn transition is expected for this field orientations in the thermodynamic limit, we show that constraining the transverse dimensions of the system qualitatively changes the behavior. For samples elongated along the field direction with finite transverse area, the divergence-free constraint quantizes the number of string excitations that can span the system. As a result, the magnetization evolves through a cascade of discrete transitions corresponding to the successive entry of individual strings. Using Monte Carlo simulations, we demonstrate that each transition is marked by sharp magnetization steps and peaks in the specific heat and susceptibility, whose amplitudes scale linearly with the system length. We complement the numerical results with an analytical treatment based on the entropy - energy balance on a system with reduced dimensionality, deriving the critical fields associated with each topological sector. In the isotropic limit these transitions merge into a smooth crossover, but for anisotropic samples they remain sharply resolved, illustrating an unconventional mechanism by which finite geometry stabilizes topological phase transitions in frustrated magnets.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26617"></a>
### [Torsion Induced Asymmetric Luttinger Liquids](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26617v1)

**Authors:** Arseny Pantsialei, Nicholas Sedlmayr  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26617v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26617_figures/2604.26617_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Microscopic extraction of the tilt from the exact projected lower band. In panel (a) the exact lower-band dis- persion ϵ−(k) for τB &gt; 0, τB &lt; 0, and τ = 0, are shown. For the representative asymmetric case, the tangents at the two Fermi points k−and k+ are shown explicitly, demonstrating unequal chiral slopes v−̸= v+. Reversing the sign of τB re- verses the asymmetry. In panel (b) the velocity asymmetry or tilt, δv = (v−−v+)/2, extracted numerically from the exact Fermi-point slopes is plotted versus τB. The odd dependence and near-linear behavior close to the origin confirm the mi- croscopic scaling δv ∝τB.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26617_figures/2604.26617_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Schematics of the g′ 2 and g′ 1 backscattering processes, as labeled on the figures. Pictured are those non-oscillating processes which lead to the massive term Eq. (35).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26617_figures/2604.26617_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. The spectral functions for q &gt; 0, top panel, and q &lt; 0, bottom panel. Relatively large values of the asymmetric velocity vac are used for illustrative purposes. For plotting the spectral function has been convoluted with a Gaussian resolution function of width ω/vc|q| = 0.02.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26617_figures/2604.26617_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. The odd spectral function, see Eq. (71). A non-zero odd spectral function is a demonstration of the tilt. For plot- ting the spectral function has been convoluted with a Gaus- sian resolution function of width ω/vc|q| = 0.02. See teh caption of Fig. 3 for more details.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how the breaking of parity (P) and time-reversal (T) symmetries, while preserving composite PT symmetry, affects the properties of a spinful Luttinger liquid, specifically focusing on the emergence of asymmetric left- and right-moving velocities.

**Main result.** The authors show that in a general spinful model, the spin degree of freedom generically becomes gapped out, resulting in an effective spinless Luttinger liquid with asymmetric velocities, and identify a non-zero odd component in the spectral function as a definitive experimental signature of this tilt.

**Method.** The researchers use bosonization, renormalization group (RG) analysis, and the calculation of Green's functions to derive the effective low-energy theory and spectral properties.

**Summary.** This paper explores a novel class of 'asymmetric' Luttinger liquids where left- and right-moving particles travel at different speeds due to torsion and magnetic fields. By generalizing previous spinless models to a spinful framework, the authors demonstrate that the spin sector becomes gapped, leaving an effective spinless liquid. They provide a concrete way to detect this effect experimentally by looking for an asymmetric signature in the spectral function. This work is significant for understanding transport in complex 1D geometries like twisted nanowires.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The model describes a 1D spinful electronic system, such as a helical nanowire, subject to torsion and a Zeeman field, which induces a velocity asymmetry between chiral modes.

**Key observables.** The spectral function A(k, omega), specifically the even and odd combinations (Ae and Ao) of the spectral function.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Torsion (tau), Zeeman field (B), velocity asymmetry (delta v), and Luttinger parameters (Kc, Ks).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes a smooth/adiabatic limit where curvature and torsion vary slowly relative to the Fermi wavelength, and relies on a first-order RG analysis.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the projected band dispersion and the scaling of velocity asymmetry with torsion and Zeeman field; Figure 2 provides a schematic of the model; Figures 3 and 4 illustrate the asymmetry in the spectral function and the non-zero odd component signature.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with a microscopic derivation of the asymmetric Hamiltonian from a helical wire, proceeds to a bosonized description and RG analysis to show the gapping of the spin sector, and concludes with the calculation of the spectral function to identify experimental signatures.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider a general model of a Luttinger liquid with broken parity and time reversal symmetry, but with their composite symmetry intact. Such a scenario can be due to a combination of torsion and a Zeeman field in nanowires, or a result of bringing different helical Luttinger liquids into proximity. The broken symmetries result in a band structure with no axis of symmetry, and therefore with asymmetric velocities between left and right moving contributions. By taking a general spin-full model with all possible scattering and interaction terms in the bosonic model we show that generically the spin degree of freedom becomes gapped out, resulting in an effective spinless Luttinger liquid with asymmetric velocities. Our work generalizes and extends previous studies which focused on a minimal model of a spinless Luttinger liquid. We further demonstrate that a possible experimental signature of the asymmetry of such asymmetric models can be seen in the spectral function.

</details>

### [Towards Quantum Optimised Malware Containment](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26692v1)

**Authors:** Matthew Sutcliffe, Ravindra Mutyamsetty  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26692v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
