<sub>Figure 3 shows the results of experiments measuring the number of steps taken to find the minimum of an unsorted list of pre-computed influences for each candidate edge, using both a classical linear search and a Grover search. We used pre-computed influences here to clearly demarcate the QAE experiments from the Grover experiments and, in this case, focus on the latter. Moreover, note that the ‘number of steps’ refers to the number of oracle calls in the case of the Grover results and simply the number of candidate edges |EC| in the case of the classical linear search. These Grover measurements were computed on real quan- tum hardware (namely IBM’s ‘Fez’) and were hence limited to small...</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The computational bottleneck in malware containment, which requires minimizing the expected spread of infection in a network while balancing the operational cost of disabling connections.

**Main result.** The proposed hybrid quantum approach provides quadratic improvements in both the estimation of influence via QAE and the optimization of edge removals via GMF, with experimental results supporting the theoretical scaling.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum workflow combining Quantum Amplitude Estimation (QAE) to replace Monte Carlo simulations and Grover Minimum Finding (GMF) to accelerate the search for optimal edge removals.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the high computational cost of optimizing network security against malware spread. By replacing classical Monte Carlo simulations with Quantum Amplitude Estimation and using Grover's algorithm for optimization, the authors demonstrate a theoretical quadratic speedup. Experimental tests on small-scale quantum hardware and classical simulations show that the quantum approach can achieve much higher accuracy with significantly fewer iterations than classical methods. This work highlights a promising long-term application for quantum algorithms in stochastic network optimization.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A network model represented by a graph G=(V,E) using the Independent Cascade (IC) model for stochastic diffusion, where nodes are infected with specific probabilities.

**Key observables.** Expected influence (number of infected nodes), operational impact of edge removal, and the objective function balancing security benefit vs. disruption.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Edge activation probability (p), edge operational importance (i), target additive error (epsilon), candidate edge set size (|Ec|), and the weighting coefficient (lambda).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The complexity analysis assumes standard oracle assumptions and requires fault-tolerant quantum devices for large-scale implementation; the advantage depends on the cost of the quantum oracle relative to classical simulation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a graph illustrating seed nodes and edge parameters; Figure 2 compares the accuracy of influence estimation between Monte Carlo and QAE; Figure 3 compares the number of steps required for linear search versus Grover search.

**Paper structure.** The paper defines the malware containment problem, proposes a hybrid quantum algorithm (QAE + GMF), provides a theoretical complexity analysis, and presents experimental validation using classical simulations and real IBM quantum hardware.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The containment of malware in computing networks may be naturally formulated as a network influence minimisation problem, in which one seeks to limit the expected spread of an infection while balancing the operational cost of disabling network connections. Classical approaches often rely on Monte Carlo simulation of stochastic diffusion processes and greedy optimisation over candidate edge removals, resulting in significant computational overhead due to repeated influence evaluations. In this work, we propose a hybrid quantum approach which combines Quantum Amplitude Estimation (QAE) and Grover Minimum Finding (GMF) to provide quadratic improvements in both the estimation and optimisation components of the problem. Specifically, QAE replaces classical Monte Carlo simulation, reducing the sampling complexity of influence estimation from $O(1/\varepsilon^2)$ to $O(1/\varepsilon)$ for a target additive error $\varepsilon \ll 1$, while GMF reduces the number of candidate evaluations required to identify optimal edge removals from $O(|E_C|)$ to $O(\sqrt{|E_C|})$. We present a formal problem definition, describe the construction of the corresponding quantum oracles, and analyse the resulting complexity improvements under standard oracle assumptions. Preliminary experiments, including classical simulation of QAE and small-scale execution of Grover search on real quantum hardware, support the expected theoretical scaling. While practical implementation at scale requires fault-tolerant quantum devices, our results demonstrate that quantum algorithms offer a promising long-term direction for accelerating stochastic network optimisation problems such as malware containment.

</details>

### [Voltage-Regulated Photoluminescence Modulation in a 0D-2D Mixed Dimensional Heterostructure](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26358v1)

**Authors:** S. V. U. Vedhanth, Amit Bhunia, Mohit Kumar Singh, Yuvraj Chaudhry, Mohamed Henini, Shouvik Datta

**Main problem.** The study investigates the origin of voltage-regulated oscillations in photoluminescence, photocurrent, and photo-capacitance within a mixed-dimensional 0D-2D heterostructure.

**Main result.** The researchers observed that PL intensity oscillates 180 degrees out-of-phase with the DC photocurrent, driven by a competition between coherent and incoherent electron tunneling processes regulated by bias voltage.

**Method.** The study uses low-temperature (10 K) photoluminescence spectroscopy, DC/AC electrical measurements (impedance and conductance), and power-law/Arrhenius/Varshni analysis of spectral features.

**Summary.** This paper explores how applying a voltage can modulate the light emission (photoluminescence) in a structure containing both quantum dots and a 2D electron gas. The researchers found that the light intensity oscillates in opposition to the electrical current, a phenomenon caused by the periodic switching between coherent and incoherent electron tunneling. This discovery is significant because it demonstrates a way to control exciton populations using electrical bias, opening new possibilities for developing advanced quantum optoelectronic devices.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A mixed-dimensional 0D-2D heterostructure consisting of InAs quantum dots (0D) embedded in a GaAs/AlAs-based Resonant Tunneling Diode (RTD) structure, which hosts a photogenerated 2D electron gas (2DEG).

**Key observables.** Integrated PL intensity, PL peak position, PL linewidth (FWHM), DC photocurrent (IDC), AC conductance (GAC), and photo-capacitance (CPh).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature of 10 K, reverse bias voltage, excitation intensity, Fermi wavelength (~35 nm), and exciton densities (~10^11/cm^2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes oscillations arise from periodic energy level changes rather than sequential tunneling and assumes the system acts as a 2D array of parallel resonant tunneling channels.

**Figures summary.** Figures include PL spectra vs. excitation intensity, temperature-dependent PL analysis (Arrhenius/Varshines fits), and the correlation between PL, photocurrent, and capacitance oscillations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the 0D-2D heterostructure, details the experimental setup and spectroscopy methods, presents the observed oscillatory behavior in optical and electrical signals, provides a mechanistic explanation based on tunneling competition, and concludes with implications for optoelectronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper explores the interplay between coherent and incoherent tunneling and the periodic ordering of excitonic populations, which is relevant to the study of many-body dynamics and light-matter interaction in semiconductor nanostructures.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Bias dependent oscillations in excitonic photoluminescence are observed in a mixed dimensional 0D 2D heterostructure. These oscillations arise from modulation by oscillatory DC photocurrent, which exhibits periodic negative differential resistance, indicating recurring charge accumulation within the heterostructure. The persistence of these oscillations across a macroscopic area of diameter around 200 microns suggests the presence of periodically correlated quantum phenomena over large length scales. Furthermore, bias dependent oscillations in the photo capacitance are observed, reflecting a periodic ordering and disordering of excitonic populations. Together, these observations point to a direct competition between coherent and incoherent electron tunnelling processes. The coupled oscillatory behaviour of photoluminescence, photocurrent, and photo capacitance highlights new opportunities for exciton-based quantum optoelectronic devices.

</details>

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26717"></a>
### [Non-Equilibrium Orbital Transport in Terahertz Optorbitronics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26717v1)

**Authors:** Sobhan Subhra Mishra, Ranjan Singh  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26717v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **3/5**

**Main problem.** Understanding how orbital angular momentum (OAM) currents are generated, transported, and converted into electrical signals on ultrafast timescales.

**Main result.** The review introduces 'terahertz optorbitronics' as a way to track orbital currents in real time and highlights the debate between ballistic-like transport versus rapid decay within atomic layers.

**Method.** The use of terahertz optorbitronics, which employs ultrafast femtosecond laser pulses and terahertz radiation to observe non-equilibrium orbital transport.

**Summary.** This review introduces terahertz optorbitronics, a new approach to studying the ultrafast transport of orbital angular momentum in materials. It addresses the fundamental uncertainty regarding whether orbital currents travel long distances or decay rapidly. By using femtosecond pulses and THz radiation, the technique can distinguish orbital motion from spin transport. The authors highlight potential materials like graphene and altermagnets that could enable more efficient, post-spintronic technologies.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Nanoscale thin-film materials, including engineered graphene and altermagnets, subject to light, electrical gating, and strain.

**Key observables.** Orbital currents, orbital angular momentum (OAM) transport, and conversion into electrical signals.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Ultrafast timescales (quadrillionth of a second), transport distances (nanometre scale), and non-equilibrium dynamics.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the concept of orbital-based information technology, defines the terahertz optorbitronics technique, discusses the current scientific debate regarding transport scales, explores specific material platforms like graphene and altermagnets, and reviews methods for active control of orbital transport.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper discusses non-equilibrium dynamics and the use of ultrafast light-matter interaction to drive and observe transient currents, which is highly relevant to the study of driven many-body systems and quantum optics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Modern information technologies rely on controlling the flow of electrons through their charge and spin. A rapidly emerging alternative is to use the orbital motion of electrons, the way they circulate around atomic sites as a new carrier of information. This orbital angular momentum (OAM) could enable more energy-efficient devices and reduce reliance on scarce heavy elements, but how orbital currents are generated and transported, especially on ultrafast timescales, remains largely unknown. In this review, we introduce terahertz optorbitronics, an approach that uses ultrafast femtosecond laser pulses and terahertz radiation to observe orbital transport in real time. On timescales of quadrillionth of a second, this technique allows us to track how orbital currents are launched, propagate, and convert into electrical signals in nanoscale thin-film materials. Surprisingly, recent experiments have revealed conflicting pictures such as orbital currents may travel over tens of nanometres like ballistic waves or instead decay within just a few atomic layers, highlighting a fundamental unresolved question in the field. We explain how these ultrafast measurements can disentangle orbital motion from conventional spin transport, and we highlight new materials from engineered graphene to altermagnets, that could act as tunable sources of orbital currents. We also discuss how light, electrical gating, strain, and interface design can be used to actively control orbital transport and improve its conversion into usable electronic signals. By revealing orbital transport as a dynamic, non-equilibrium process, terahertz optorbitronics opens a new direction for nanoscale science, the one that could lead to faster, more efficient technologies operating beyond the limits of conventional spin-based electronics.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

(a,b) Fermi energy (ϵF ) dependence of the longitu- dinal charge conductivities derived from (a) ordinary and (b) anomalous off-diagonal density matrix for different values of impurity density ni. Panel (c) and (d) plot the corresponding temperature T dependence. Other parameters are from the experimental results Ref. [28]: M = 5meV, Aπ/a = 1.1eV and B(π/a)2 = 7.0eV, U/a2 = 23eV and a = 0.65nm.</sub>

</details>

(c-d) The corresponding longitudinal charge conductivities for several values of M at ϵF = 0. Other parameters are the same as Fig. 2.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether longitudinal transport can occur in Berry-curvature-dominated systems, such as topological insulators, even when the density of states at the Fermi energy vanishes.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that impurity-induced quantum decoherence creates a longitudinal transport channel that persists even in the absence of carriers at the Fermi level, exhibiting a scaling linear in impurity density and a 1/T temperature dependence.

**Method.** A microscopic theory using a quantum master equation framework to analyze the decay of interband coherence (off-diagonal density matrix elements) due to impurity scattering.

**Summary.** This paper challenges the traditional distinction between metals and insulators by showing that longitudinal transport can exist even when there are no charge carriers at the Fermi surface. By focusing on Berry-curvature-dominated systems like topological insulators, the authors show that impurity scattering destroys interband coherence, creating a new conduction channel. This mechanism leads to unconventional scaling, such as conductivity being linear in impurity density and inversely proportional to temperature. These findings suggest that quantum decoherence can be a primary driver of electrical response, potentially linking topological insulators to the physics of strange metals.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on bulk topological insulators, specifically utilizing the Bernevig-Hughes-Zhang (BHZ) model for 2D systems, including a Hamiltonian with electronic structure, external electric field coupling, and short-range impurity potential.

**Key observables.** Longitudinal conductivity, longitudinal resistance, and interband coherence.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Impurity density (ni), impurity strength (U), Fermi energy (epsilon_F), temperature (T), and the mass parameter (M).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theory assumes short-range impurity potential and models decoherence via ordinary and anomalous-type scattering rates.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows conductivity dependence on Fermi energy and temperature; Figure 3 illustrates scaling with impurity density and mass parameter; Figure 4 compares temperature and impurity density dependence of total conductivity.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the breakdown of the Drude paradigm, develops a microscopic theory of decoherence-induced transport, demonstrates the mechanism in both topological and trivial regimes, and compares the results to known scaling laws like strange-metal behavior.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper explores how quantum decoherence acts as a fundamental mechanism for transport, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and the emergence of classical-like transport from quantum dynamics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In modern condensed matter theory, phases of electronic matter--such as metals and insulators-are fundamentally distinguished by the presence or absence of charge-carrying quasiparticles or excitations near the Fermi surface at low temperatures. Here, we show that this criterion breaks down in Berry-curvature-dominated systems, where transport is governed by interband coherence across the entire Fermi sea. We develop a microscopic theory of quantum transport in bulk topological insulators with a vanishing density of states at the Fermi energy, for which the conventional Drude contribution is absent. We demonstrate that impurity-scattering-induced coherence decay generates a distinct longitudinal transport channel even in the topologically trivial regime, with edge contributions rigorously excluded. This mechanism yields a finite longitudinal conductivity even in the absence of carriers at the Fermi level and exhibits an unconventional scaling linear in impurity density in the dilute limit, in stark contrast to Drude behaviour. Importantly, this decoherence-induced conductance is inversely proportional to temperature, reminiscent of strange-metal behaviour, most prominently observed in cuprate superconductors above their critical temperature. Our findings reveal quantum decoherence as a fundamental origin of longitudinal transport beyond the Drude paradigm, challenging the traditional distinction between metals and insulators.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26825"></a>
### [Programmable Persistent Random Walks in Active Brownian Particles Govern Emergent Dynamics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26825v1)

**Authors:** Tarun Sunkesula Raghavendra, Yogesh Shelke, Stijn van der Ham, Anpuj Nair S, Hanumantha Rao Vutukuri  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26825v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5**

**Main problem.** Most synthetic active matter systems are limited to a single mode of motion, making it difficult to study how different types of individual particle dynamics affect collective behavior.

**Main result.** The authors developed an experimental strategy to program various persistent random walks (Levy, run-and-tumble, self-avoiding, and Gaussian) in active Brownian particles using light and magnetic fields, demonstrating that these motion modes directly influence clustering and emergent organization.

**Method.** The researchers combined light-modulated propulsion strength with magnetic control of propulsion direction to encode specific trajectories and motion modes in synthetic active particles.

**Summary.** This paper presents a new experimental method to program the motion of active Brownian particles using light and magnetism. By controlling these forces, the researchers can switch between different types of random walks, such as Levy walks and run-and-tumble dynamics, within a single experiment. They also demonstrate the ability to steer particles along complex geometric paths. Finally, they show that the specific type of individual particle motion significantly affects how the particles cluster together, providing a tool to study emergent organization in active matter.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of active Brownian particles (ABPs) that can be manipulated via light and magnetic fields to emulate different types of self-propelled motion, including chiral active particles.

**Key observables.** Step-length distributions, particle trajectories (e.g., Fibonacci spirals, nested polygons), and clustering dynamics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Propulsion strength, propulsion direction, and the specific type of random walk (Levy, run-and-tumble, etc.).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the limitation of current ABP systems, presents the experimental strategy for programmable motion, details the implementation of various random walk types and complex trajectories, and concludes by analyzing the impact of these modes on collective clustering dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is relevant to researchers in non-equilibrium statistical mechanics and open quantum systems as it provides a platform to study how programmable microscopic stochasticity and broken detailed balance drive emergent many-body organization and transport.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Self-propelled particles serve as minimal models for emulating the dynamic self-organization of microorganisms, yet most synthetic systems remain limited to a single mode of motion, namely active Brownian particles (ABPs). Here, we present an experimental strategy to encode various persistent random walks in ABPs by combining light-modulated propulsion strength with magnetic control of propulsion direction. Our system enables programmable Levy walks with tunable step-length distributions, run-and-tumble dynamics, self-avoiding random walks, and Gaussian walks, with on-demand switching between motion modes within a single experiment. In addition, particles are steered along complex trajectories such as Fibonacci spirals and nested polygons. Beyond single-particle behavior, we show that propulsion modes influence clustering dynamics by comparing ABPs with chiral active particles undergoing circular motion. These results establish a versatile platform for investigating how encoded motion at the level of individual particles governs transport, search strategies, and emergent organization in active matter systems.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26751"></a>
### [Sub-50 Picosecond exceptionally Bright Perovskite Scintillation by Unlocking Giant Oscillator Strength](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26751v1)

**Authors:** Chuanwei Dai, Yunbiao Zhao, Xiao Ouyang, Huaqing Huang, Yulan Liang, Jiaqi Bai, Yingjie Song, Jianhan Sun, Yiqun Duan, Wenjun Ma, Senlin Huang, Shufeng Wang, Jianming Xue, Xiaoping Ouyang  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26751v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
**Topic relevance:** `quantum optics experiment` **3/5** · `free-space correlated emission` **2/5**

**Main problem.** Conventional scintillators are limited by a trade-off between emission rate and light yield, preventing them from breaking the 50-picosecond timing limit.

**Main result.** The researchers achieved a sub-50 ps scintillator with a 13.11 ps dominant lifetime and high light yield (21,851 ph/MeV) by unlocking giant oscillator strength in perovskite nanocrystals.

**Method.** The study uses weakly confined CsPbCl3 perovskite nanocrystals at mild cryogenic temperatures to suppress exciton-phonon scattering and harness coherent radiative acceleration.

**Summary.** This paper presents a breakthrough in scintillator technology using CsPbCl3 perovskite nanocrystals. By suppressing exciton-phonon scattering at cryogenic temperatures, the researchers unlocked a giant oscillator strength that enables ultra-fast photon bursts. This allows for a scintillator lifetime of just 13.11 ps with a very high light yield. The results enable unprecedented timing resolution for high-energy physics and medical imaging applications.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The experimental platform consists of weakly confined CsPbCl3 perovskite nanocrystals operating at mild cryogenic temperatures.

**Key observables.** Dominant lifetime, light yield (ph/MeV), prompt photon emission rate, coincidence time resolution (CTR), and resolution of electron/gamma-ray pulses.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 13.11 ps lifetime, 21,851 ph/MeV light yield, 30.8 ps coincidence time resolution, and 13.5-16.6 ps pulse resolution.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The effect relies on suppressing exciton-phonon scattering via mild cryogenic temperatures.

**Paper structure.** Introduction to scintillator limitations, demonstration of the strategy using CsPbCl3 nanocrystals, characterization of lifetime and light yield, and validation in realistic detection scenarios.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper demonstrates the manipulation of giant oscillator strength and coherent radiative acceleration in nanocrystals, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and the control of light-matter interactions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Ultrafast scintillators are indispensable for precise timing in high-energy physics and medical diagnostics. Fundamentally constrained by the trade-off between emission rate and light yield, conventional scintillators remain kinetically trapped in the sub-nanosecond regime, failing to break 50-picosecond limit. Here, we demonstrate a strategy to bypass this limitation by harnessing the coherent radiative acceleration in weakly confined CsPbCl3 perovskite nanocrystals to generate an ultrafast photon burst. This effect originates from the giant oscillator strength, which we unlock by suppressing exciton-phonon scattering at mild cryogenic temperatures. Consequently, our scintillator achieves an unprecedented dominant lifetime of 13.11 ps alongside a high light yield of 21,851 ph/MeV. The resulting prompt photon emission rate more than 100 times higher than that of state-of-the-art ultrafast scintillators. We validate this breakthrough in realistic detection scenarios, achieving a coincidence time resolution of 30.8 ps and accurately resolving 13.5 ps electron bunches and 16.6 ps single-shot gamma-ray pulses. Our findings establish a robust coherent framework for next-generation ultrafast scintillators, pushing extreme radiation diagnostics into the picosecond frontier.

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating how periodic hopping modulation in an off-diagonal Aubry-Andre-Harper chain creates internal boundaries that influence the transport signatures of edge, band-edge, and in-band bulk states.

**Main result.** The study identifies distinct transport signatures for different state classes, showing that in-band bulk states exhibit strong size-dependent oscillations and dephasing sensitivity, while edge and band-edge states are more robust.

**Method.** Non-equilibrium Green's function (NEGF) method for transport, Landauer formula for current, Participation Ratio (PR) for localization, and the Buttiker probe approach for modeling dephasing.

**Summary.** This paper examines quantum transport in an off-diagonal Aubry-Andre-Harper chain, focusing on how hopping modulation creates internal boundaries. It distinguishes between the transport behaviors of edge, band-edge, and in-band bulk states. The researchers show that while edge states are robust against system size and dephasing, in-band bulk states are highly sensitive to interference and decoherence. The results demonstrate that electrode configuration and coupling strength can drive a crossover from tunneling to ballistic transport. These findings are applicable to photonic arrays, ultracold atoms, and semiconductor nanowires.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A one-dimensional, commensurate off-diagonal Aubry-Andre-Harper (AAH) chain with periodic hopping modulation $t_i = t [1 + v \cos(2\pi b i + \phi)]$ connected to two semi-infinite 1D electrodes.

**Key observables.** Transmission probability $T(E)$, output current $I(V)$, energy spectrum, Participation Ratio (PR), and dephasing-induced peak broadening.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Modulation strength $v$, AAH phase $\phi$, system size $N$, electrode coupling strength, and dephasing strength.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Non-interacting electron approximation, zero temperature, and a linear voltage drop across the chain for dephasing probes.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Energy spectrum and PR vs system size; Fig 3: Transmission spectra showing smooth vs oscillating peaks; Fig 5: Current vs system size for different state classes; Fig 6: PR vs modulation strength; Fig 8: Transmission spectra under varying dephasing strengths.

**Paper structure.** Introduction to the AAH model and transport problem; Model description and methodology; Analysis of energy spectrum and localization; Investigation of transport signatures and electrode positioning; Study of system size and coupling effects; Analysis of dephasing and decoherence; Conclusion.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper explores the interplay between quasiperiodic modulation, localization, and environmental decoherence, which is highly relevant to understanding transport in open quantum systems and the stability of localized states in many-body dynamics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate quantum transport in an off-diagonal Aubry--André--Harper chain. The periodic hopping modulation generates effective internal boundaries that strongly influence the transmission characteristics. We show that edge, in-band bulk, and band-edge bulk states can be clearly distinguished through their transport signatures. In particular, bulk states near the band edges exhibit behavior similar to edge states, with weak dependence on system size, whereas in-band bulk states display pronounced size-dependent oscillations. We further demonstrate that the chain--electrode coupling strength controls the broadening of transmission resonances and drives a crossover from tunneling-dominated to nearly ballistic transport. In addition, dephasing introduces distinct sensitivity across different state classes, depending on their degree of spatial localization. These results highlight the key role of internal boundaries and quantum coherence in governing transport in modulated one-dimensional systems.

</details>

**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26631v1>

**Main problem.** Traditional superconducting diodes are static circuit elements because their asymmetry is fixed by crystal geometry or structure. The goal is to create a programmable superconducting diode where the polarity and strength can be tuned.

**Main result.** The researchers demonstrated a programmable superconducting diode in FeSe where the diode effect is controlled by manipulating nematic twin domain patterns via rapid thermal quenching. They achieved high diode efficiencies of up to 75% and showed that the device state can be 'written' using current pulses.

**Method.** The team used cryogenic Focused Ion Beam (FIB) milling to create symmetric FeSe microstructures and applied intense microsecond current pulses to quench nematic order at rates exceeding 10^7 K/s. Transport measurements, including angle-dependent magnetotransport and pulsed I-V curves, were used to characterize the diode efficiency and domain states.

**Summary.** This paper presents a method to create a programmable superconducting diode using the electronic nematicity of FeSe. By using rapid current pulses to quench the material, the researchers can manipulate the pattern of nematic domain walls, effectively 'writing' the diode's polarity and strength. This moves superconducting diode technology from static, fixed-geometry components to reconfigurable, programmable circuit elements. The effect is highly efficient and remains robust even in very high magnetic fields.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical platform is FeSe, an unconventional, layered, electronic-nematic superconductor. The system relies on the interaction between superconducting vortices and nematic twin boundaries to break inversion symmetry.

**Key observables.** Superconducting diode efficiency (eta), critical currents (Ic+ and Ic-), resistivity, and nematic transition temperature (Ts).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Quench rates (up to 12 MK/s), magnetic field strength (up to 12-16 T), temperature (near Tc and Ts), and current pulse width (microsecond scale).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the observed asymmetry is intrinsic to the domain wall interactions rather than structural defects introduced by the FIB process, which they verify via XPS/EDX.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 illustrates the quenching process and transport signatures; Fig 3 demonstrates the programming protocol and the ability to cycle through different diode states; Fig 4 shows the dependence of the SDE on the magnetic field angle and the underlying symmetry mechanism.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of static diodes, describes the fabrication of FeSe microstructures, details the thermal quenching method for programming, presents the results of the high-efficiency diode effect, and concludes with the physical mechanism involving vortex-domain wall interactions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The superconducting diode effect (SDE) allows polarity-dependent critical currents when time-reversal and current-inverting spatial symmetries are broken. Superconducting diodes show promise for applications, but inversion asymmetry is usually encoded in sample geometry or non-centrosymmetric crystals, rendering them static circuit elements. Here we demonstrate a programmable superconducting diode whose functionality is encoded in correlated electronic domains. We use the nematic superconductor FeSe as a platform and report a large intrinsic SDE with efficiencies up to $η\sim 75\%$ due to vortices interacting with nematic twin boundaries. The domain wall configuration thus encodes the SDE of the device. Through intense microsecond current pulses to quench the nematic order at rates exceeding $10^7$~K/s, we modify the domain pattern and control the polarity and strength of the SDE. These results establish a new paradigm in which superconducting circuit elements can be programmed through patterns imprinted into correlated electronic states.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26887"></a>
### [Resolving growth-induced off-stoichiometry in AgCrSe$_2$ single crystals](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26887v1)

**Authors:** Felix Eder, Zeno Maesen, Yurii Skourski, Enrico Giannini, Oksana Zaharko, Fabian O. von Rohr  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26887v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
**Topic relevance:** `Kondo & dissipative impurity` **2/5**

**Main problem.** Investigating whether the anomalous Hall behavior and Kondo physics reported in AgCrSe2 single crystals are intrinsic properties or artifacts of off-stoichiometry caused by the chemical vapor transport growth method.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that crystals grown via chemical vapor transport are systematically off-stoichiometric (Ag1-xCr(Se2-yCly)), leading to a suppressed Neel temperature; however, they successfully grew high-quality stoichiometric crystals using an Ag/Se self-flux method.

**Method.** The study employs elemental analysis, single-crystal X-ray diffraction, and magnetization measurements to characterize composition and magnetic properties, comparing chemical vapor transport-grown crystals to self-flux grown crystals and polycrystalline samples.

**Summary.** This paper addresses a critical quality issue in the study of AgCrSe2 single crystals. It reveals that previous crystals grown by chemical vapor transport contain significant impurities from the transport agent, which alters their magnetic properties. By developing a new Ag/Se self-flux growth method, the researchers produced stoichiometric crystals that match the properties of bulk powder. This work provides a reliable material platform for future studies into the intrinsic anomalous Hall effect and Kondo physics in this system.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is AgCrSe2, a layered delafossite-like antiferromagnet that acts as a superionic conductor at high temperatures and exhibits complex low-temperature transport phenomena.

**Key observables.** Néel temperature (Tc), saturation field, and chemical composition (stoichiometry).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Néel temperature (46 K for off-stoichiometric vs 58 K for stoichiometric), and the off-stoichiometry levels (x approx y approx 0.08).

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the issue of off-stoichiometry in existing crystals, quantifies the chemical deviations using diffraction and elemental analysis, compares magnetic properties of different growth methods, and presents the successful recovery of stoichiometric properties via the self-flux method.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The layered delafossite-like antiferromagnet AgCrSe$_2$ is a superionic conductor at high temperatures and has been reported to exhibit anomalous Hall behavior and Kondo physics at low temperatures. These extraordinary transport properties have been established almost exclusively on single crystals grown by chemical vapor transport, raising questions about the role of growth-induced off-stoichiometry. Using elemental analysis, single-crystal X-ray diffraction, and magnetization measurements, we show that such crystals are indeed systematically off-stoichiometric, with a general composition of Ag$_{1-x}$Cr(Se$_{2-y}$Cl$_y$) ($x \approx y \approx 0.08$) arising from the use of CrCl$_3$ as a transport agent. This off-stoichiometry manifests in altered magnetic properties, most notably a suppressed Néel temperature of 46\,K compared to 58\,K in stoichiometric polycrystalline samples prepared by solid-state synthesis. By optimizing an Ag/Se self-flux growth method, we obtained large single crystals of AgCrSe$_2$ that recover the magnetic transition temperature and saturation field of stoichiometric powder samples. These results establish self-flux growth as a route to high-quality stoichiometric AgCrSe$_2$ single crystals and provide a reliable platform for reassessing whether the reported anomalous transport phenomena are intrinsic or arise from off-stoichiometry.

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether Turing patterns (TPs) on non-fluctuating (fixed) surfaces respond to external mechanical stresses in the same way as TPs on fluctuating membranes.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that fixed-lattice models can accurately capture stress relaxation, entropy, and pattern anisotropy, showing that 3D rigid plates exhibit mechanical anisotropy analogous to 2D soft surfaces.

**Method.** A hybrid numerical technique combining Monte Carlo simulations for surface variables and discrete Reaction-Diffusion (FitzHugh-Nagumo) equations for pattern dynamics.

**Summary.** This paper explores how Turing patterns form and align on rigid, non-fluctuating surfaces under mechanical strain. By using Finsler geometry, the authors model how mechanical stress influences the direction of pattern formation. They show that even without vertex fluctuations, the model successfully captures thermodynamic properties like entropy and stress relaxation. The results suggest that the behavior of patterns on rigid 3D structures is fundamentally similar to those on flexible 2D membranes.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A discrete Finsler geometry model on 2D (square, triangular) and 3D (cubic) lattices, utilizing a Gaussian bond potential and an internal degree of freedom representing the direction of mechanical stress.

**Key observables.** Tensile stress, surface tension, entropy density, free energy density, and order parameters for pattern direction correlation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fluctuation range (epsilon_R), deformation ratio (R), internal degree of freedom vector (tau), and diffusion/reaction coefficients.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes non-fluctuating vertices (fixed positions) and treats the fixed lattice as the limit of a fluctuating lattice as epsilon_R approaches zero.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows biological pattern examples; Figure 2 illustrates lattice fluctuation ranges and partition function limits; Figure 5 shows TP snapshots on 2D/3D lattices; Figure 6 and 7 plot direction-dependent coefficients and energies; Figure 9 displays 3D plate stress, entropy, and free energy data; Figure 10 shows the discretization of the Finsler metric.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the biological motivation, defines the mathematical framework for the fixed-lattice model and Finsler geometry, details the hybrid simulation method, presents results for 2D and 3D lattice anisotropy, and concludes with thermodynamic analysis of stress relaxation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper presents a numerical investigation of Turing patterns (TPs) utilizing the reaction-diffusion equation for the activator $u$ and the inhibitor $v$ on two- and three-dimensional lattices, discarding vertex fluctuations. The absence of vertex fluctuations means the absence of positional movement of $u$ and $v$. Consequently, $u$ and $v$ have values at spatially discrete points, such as the pigment cells in zebrafish and sea shells. Furthermore, the mechanical property is implemented through the Finsler geometry modeling technique. This technique incorporates the internal degree of freedom $\vecτ$, corresponding to the direction of mechanical stress. Additionally, a stress formula based on Gaussian bond potential is shown to be well-defined on the non-fluctuating lattices, and therefore, it enables the calculation of entropy for capturing the stress relaxation phenomenon in a manner analogous to that on fluctuating surfaces. The results of the study indicate that these biological patterns also exhibit responses to external mechanical forces similar to TPs on fluctuating membranes.

</details>

**Main problem.** The lack of a formal, mathematical framework to translate hierarchical biological design logic (biomimicry) into engineered, programmable stimulus-response systems while preserving physical consistency across scales.

**Main result.** The development of a category-theoretic pipeline that successfully generates new actuator classes (e.g., thermal-twisting) through the composition of validated modules and demonstrates high agreement between predicted and experimental 4D-printed deformations.

**Method.** A category-theoretic framework using functors (Implementation, Projection, Compilation) and morphisms (Assembly, Reduction) to map biological mechanics to 3D-printing instructions, implemented via a modular Grasshopper workflow.

**Summary.** This paper presents a rigorous mathematical framework using category theory to bridge the gap between biological design and engineering. By treating biological mechanisms as a hierarchy of dynamical systems, the authors create a 'functorial' translation that allows researchers to map natural logic onto 3D-printing parameters. The framework is generative, meaning it can discover new actuator behaviors by recombining existing modules. Experimental validation using 4D-printed polymers confirms that the predicted mechanical responses match physical observations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A multiscale hierarchy modeled on the hygromorphic (moisture-responsive) structure of a pinecone, translated into 4D-printed bilayer composites using Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF).

**Key observables.** Moisture content, principal strains, strain mismatch, intrinsic curvature, and opening angles.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Swelling coefficients, relaxation times, fiber orientation angles, geometric factors (thickness/stiffness ratios), and fabrication parameters (layer height, infill density, print speed).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes smooth manifolds and smooth maps for dynamics, and that complex systems can be understood through the local verification of interacting parts.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the pinecone hierarchy; Figures 2-6 detail the mathematical morphisms at different scales (fiber to organ); Figure 8 shows the computational workflow; Figure 10 compares predicted vs. observed actuator geometries.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of non-formal biomimicry, defines a category-theoretic framework (Dyn, Nat, Art, Spec, Comp), instantiates this on a pinecone hierarchy, demonstrates a generative computational pipeline in Grasshopper, and validates the results through 4D-printed experiments.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Natural materials achieve adaptive behavior through hierarchical organization and coupled mechanisms across scales. Their translation into engineering, however, remains largely heuristic. What is missing is a formal translation framework that carries biological design logic into engineered realization while preserving physical consistency across levels of abstraction. Here we present a category theoretic compositional framework for verified nature-derived design. The framework defines a category of stimulus response dynamical systems with natural and artificial subcategories. It introduces a structure preserving implementation functor from biological mechanics to engineered systems. It also formalizes a machine agnostic specification layer that links behavioral intent to executable fabrication programs. We instantiate the framework on the hygromorphic pinecone hierarchy as a representative biological case. We implement the full pipeline in Grasshopper, where formal specifications are translated into modular parametric scripts that preserve the compositional structure of the model. The resulting designs are fabricated by fused filament fabrication, evaluated experimentally, and tested against model predictions derived from the pipeline. The current implementation generates four actuator classes spanning two stimulus types and two kinematic responses. One actuator arises purely through composition from previously validated components, without additional manual derivation. The results show that compositionality can function not just as a descriptive language, but as a generative and system level verifiable method for mechanical material design. More broadly, the work provides a concrete route for embedding formal multiscale reasoning within increasingly computational, generative, and physics-driven design workflows.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26703"></a>
### [A self-evolving agent for explainable diagnosis of DFT-experiment band-gap mismatch](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26703v1)

**Authors:** Yue Li, Bijun Tang  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26703v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26703_figures/2604.26703_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. Architecture of XDFT. Closed loop of four core modules — Sherlock (hypothesis selection), Manipulator (atom-edit operators), Simulation (VASP execution) and Comparator (verdict on electronic character) — connected by clockwise actions (pick → apply → result → judge). External Library and Tools (top) are exchanged bidirectionally with the loop. Input (left): a metal/semiconductor mismatch material. Output (right): a corrected computational protocol with mechanistic attribution and a recommended next experiment.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26703_figures/2604.26703_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Hypothesis-selection strategy ladder on 90 mismatch materials (10-round budget; 41-hypothesis library). Five strategies share the same library: uniform-random sampling, an LLM zero-shot library ordering, a naïve condensed-matter expert ordering, XDFT's per-material adaptive ordering, and a static rule distilled from XDFT's outcomes that branches on element class only (sage green, right of the dashed line). Numbers in parentheses are (resolved / total); italicised numbers inside each bar are average rounds- to-solution among resolved materials.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26703_figures/2604.26703_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

Spearman ρ (filled circles, solid line) and Kendall τ (open squares, dashed line) between Sherlock's Beta-mean posterior over the top-15 most-tried hypotheses and the same hypotheses' final raw success rates at material 100, evaluated at every snapshot. Shaded grey band: differentiation moment (materials 30–40) where ρ crosses zero. Dashed vertical line at material 46: first time the polymorph hypothesis class was tested.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26703_figures/2604.26703_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4. Cohort learning curve on the d-block subset (n = 50). First 25 d-block materials seen by the agent (early half, light) versus last 25 (late half, dark). One-shot rate (round = 1 win, left group) and three-shot rate (round ≤ 3, right group) among resolved materials, with Wilson 95% intervals. Numbers above each bar are (resolved / cohort).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.26703_figures/2604.26703_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

**Main problem.** Standard DFT calculations frequently misclassify semiconductors as metals due to unaddressed physical non-idealities, and identifying the specific cause of these band-gap mismatches is currently a manual, labor-intensive process.

**Main result.** The XDFT agent achieved a 78% resolution rate in identifying the correct physical mechanism for band-gap mismatches, significantly outperforming random and static LLM-based baselines.

**Method.** A closed-loop, self-evolving Bayesian agent (XDFT) that selects physical hypotheses (e.g., magnetism, Hubbard U, defects), executes VASP simulations, and updates a global posterior based on the success of each test.

**Summary.** This paper introduces XDFT, an autonomous AI agent designed to automatically diagnose why standard DFT calculations fail to predict experimental band gaps. By using a Bayesian closed-loop system, the agent tests various physical corrections like magnetism, Hubbard U, and structural defects to find the correct model. It successfully resolves 78% of mismatch cases in a benchmark set, effectively learning a simplified rule-based approach for material classification. This automation significantly reduces the computational and manual effort required to study complex, correlated materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on inorganic semiconductors, including strongly correlated oxides and intermediate-valence rare-earth compounds, using VASP-based first-principles calculations with GGA-PBE, Hubbard U, and SOC.

**Key observables.** Electronic band gap, resolution rate, rounds-to-solution, and Spearman/Kendall correlation coefficients for agent calibration.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Plane-wave cutoff of 520 eV, k-point density of 1000/atom, electronic convergence of 10^-5 eV, and a budget of 10 simulation rounds per material.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The selection process assumes a prior distribution over physical hypothesis families and relies on the accuracy of the experimental band-gap data used for comparison.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the XDFT architecture; Figure 2 compares different selection strategies; Figure 3 illustrates the agent's posterior calibration over time; Figure 4 shows learning curves for d-block materials; Figure 5 provides a taxonomy heatmap of resolved cases.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the DFT mismatch problem, describes the XDFT agent architecture (Sherlock, Manipulator, Simulation, Comparator), presents benchmark results and efficiency gains, analyzes the agent's learning dynamics and emergent rules, and concludes with failure analysis and future directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Standard density functional theory (DFT) routinely misclassifies the electronic ground state of correlated and structurally complex compounds, predicting metallic behaviour for materials that experiments report as semiconductors. Each such mismatch encodes a specific non-ideality -- magnetic ordering, electron correlation, an alternative polymorph, or a defect -- that the calculation excluded, but extracting that signal at scale has remained a manual exercise. Here we introduce XDFT, a closed-loop agent that diagnoses the mismatch automatically: it draws candidate hypotheses from a curated catalogue, executes the corresponding first-principles tests, and updates a global Bayesian posterior over hypothesis usefulness from each verdict. On a verified benchmark of 124 materials, XDFT identifies a resolving mechanism for 70 of 90 mismatch cases (78\%), an order of magnitude above a uniform-random baseline (19\%) and a static LLM ordering (20\%). The internal posterior aligns with empirical performance over the benchmark timeline, and resolved cases collapse into a tri-partite element-class taxonomy that we distil into a four-line static rule. Each diagnosed material is returned with a corrected protocol and a mechanistic attribution; failed cases are flagged as evidence-backed targets for experimental re-examination.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

**Main problem.** Investigating the thermal and photochemical mechanisms of ethylbenzene dehydrogenation to styrene on rutile TiO2(110) surfaces, specifically focusing on the role of surface oxidation and photon energy.

**Main result.** The study finds that thermal pathways are dominated by proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET), while higher energy photons (257 nm) enable a more efficient pathway by accessing higher excited states to bypass ground-state barriers. Additionally, surface oxidation introduces a more efficient hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mechanism via oxygen radicals.

**Method.** A dual-methodological quantum chemical approach using Density Functional Theory (DFT-P0BE-D3) for geometry optimization and high-level multi-reference methods (SA-CASSCF/NEVPT2) to describe excited states and radical intermediates.

**Summary.** This thesis explores how light and surface chemistry can be used to create a more sustainable, low-temperature alternative to the energy-intensive industrial production of styrene. By using advanced quantum chemical simulations, the author demonstrates that specific UV wavelengths and surface oxidation can bypass high energy barriers through excited-state mechanisms. The research highlights the importance of multi-reference methods in accurately modeling complex surface-catalyzed reactions involving radical intermediates.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** An embedded cluster model representing the rutile TiO2(110) surface, including both stoichiometric and oxidized (with pre-adsorbed oxygen radicals) configurations, with ethylbenzene as the adsorbate.

**Key observables.** Activation energies (Ea), adsorption energies, electronic roots (S0, S1, T2, etc.), spin density, and reaction coordinates (bond lengths).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Wavelengths of 343 nm and 257 nm; temperature regimes (thermal vs. photocatalytic); surface oxidation state.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study uses a cluster model to represent the infinite surface and acknowledges that a complete mapping of the multidimensional potential energy surface was not possible due to computational complexity.

**Figures summary.** Figures include the industrial styrene synthesis equation, structural models of the TiO2 surface and clusters, mechanistic schematics for photocatalysis, and energy profiles comparing thermal and photochemical pathways across different electronic states.

**Paper structure.** The work begins with industrial context, followed by a description of the computational models and methods, detailed analysis of adsorption and thermal mechanisms, investigation of photochemical pathways and wavelength dependence, an exploration of the effects of surface oxidation, and a concluding discussion on future research directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This master's thesis investigates the thermal and photochemical dehydrogenation of ethylbenzene (EB) to styrene on the rutile TiO$_{2}$(110) surface. A dual-methodological quantum chemical approach is used for this investigation. While industrial styrene production is energy-intensive, photocatalysis on semiconductor materials offers a promising alternative under significantly milder conditions. To elucidate the underlying mechanisms, this study employs density functional theory (DFT-PBE-D3) for geometry optimization and high-level multi-reference methods (SA-CASSCF) to accurately describe the electronic complexity of excited states and radical intermediates.   The investigation reveals that, on the stoichiometric surface, both thermal and photochemical pathways are dominated by proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET). The wavelength dependence observed in the literature is explained by how the system navigates electronic manifolds. 343 nm irradiation leads to rapid relaxation into the ground state, where high kinetic barriers persist. In contrast, 257 nm excitation enables the system to persist in higher excited states (S1/T2). This allows the reaction to bypass the rate-determining ground-state barrier.   Furthermore, the study demonstrates that surface oxidation causes a fundamental mechanistic shift. On oxidized surfaces, pre-adsorbed oxygen radicals (O$_{Ti}$) enable direct hydrogen atom transfer (HAT), which is more efficient than PCET on reduced surfaces. This "hydrogen scavenger" effect explains the significant increase in styrene yield. This work underscores the necessity of multi-reference treatments for complex surface reactions and provides a fundamental understanding of how surface stoichiometry and photon energy govern photocatalytic efficiency.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26302"></a>
### [A Thermodynamic Analysis of Enhanced Metastability in Isochoric Supercooled Liquids](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26302v1)

**Authors:** Boris Rubinsky  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26302v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26302_figures/2604.26302_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1. Thermodynamic basis of isochoric freezing. (A) Pressure–temperature phase diagram showing the isochoric process path along the water–ice Ih liquidus to −21.9°C, 209.9 MPa at the triple point. The negative Clapeyron slope</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26302_figures/2604.26302_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2. Helmholtz free energy T–V phase diagram of water. (A) Free energy surfaces of liquid water and ice Ih as functions of temperature and specific volume; the common tangent construction (Lines of Dissipated Energy) gives the equilibrium pressure. (B) Projected T–V phase diagram with broad two-phase equilibrium region traversed by pressure isoclines. Nucleation in an isochoric system traces a vertical path; the equilibrium phase fraction is determined by the Lever rule. The arrow shows a slow equilibrium decompression path. (Modified from Powell-Palm, M.J., Rubinsky, B. &amp; Sun, W.[20] Freezing water at constant volume and under confinement. Commun Phys 3, 39 (2020)....</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** To provide a general thermodynamic explanation for why isochoric (constant-volume) supercooling is more stable against nucleation than isobaric (constant-pressure) conditions.

**Main result.** The paper proves that for substances where the solid is less dense than the liquid, the Helmholtz driving force for solidification is strictly smaller than the Gibbs driving force, leading to an exponential suppression of nucleation rates.

**Method.** The author uses Helmholtz equilibrium thermodynamics combined with a first-order perturbation methodology, expanding thermodynamic variables in terms of a small solid mass fraction.

**Summary.** This paper provides a rigorous thermodynamic proof that constant-volume conditions enhance the stability of supercooled liquids compared to constant-pressure conditions. By showing that the expansion of a less-dense solid generates a self-limiting pressure rise, the author demonstrates a reduction in the thermodynamic driving force for nucleation. This reduction leads to an exponential suppression of nucleation rates, providing a universal, geometry-independent basis for the observed stability. The work introduces a new dimensionless parameter, the isochoric stability number, to compare this effect across different materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on supercooled liquids undergoing phase transformation to a less dense solid (such as water, silicon, or gallium) under isochoric confinement.

**Key observables.** The Helmholtz driving force, the Gibbs driving force, and the dimensionless isochoric stability number (Pi_0).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The isochoric stability number (Pi_0), the Clapeyron slope, liquid isothermal compressibility, and the nascent solid mass fraction (xi).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation assumes a negative Clapeyron slope (v_s > v_l) and utilizes a first-order approximation in the solid fraction, neglecting higher-order terms.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the P-T phase diagram and the prohibition of complete solidification under isochoric conditions; Figure 2 illustrates the Helmholtz free energy T-V phase diagram and the common tangent construction for equilibrium pressure.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of isochoric stability, develops a thermodynamic framework using Helmholtz free energy, derives a pressure-induced melting point depression, establishes a thermodynamic inequality for driving forces, and introduces a dimensionless stability criterion.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Experiments show that isochoric (constant-volume) conditions enhance supercooling stability relative to isobaric (constant-pressure) conditions. Here, combining Helmholtz equilibrium thermodynamics with a first-order perturbation methodology, we derive an inequality governing nucleation stability under volumetric constraint. The derivation provides a general thermodynamic proof that for any substance undergoing phase transformation in which the solid is less dense than the liquid, the Helmholtz driving force for solidification in isochoric systems is smaller than the Gibbs driving force in isobaric systems. Since nucleation rates depend exponentially on the inverse square of the driving force, this provides a thermodynamic basis for the observed suppression of nucleation rates. While a full stochastic treatment is beyond the scope of this work, the reduction in driving force implies a weakening of the bias toward growth of pre-critical fluctuations, increasing their probability of thermal dissolution. The analysis yields a dimensionless isochoric stability number. This number is computable from bulk thermodynamic data alone and provides a geometry-independent criterion for comparing metastable liquid stability across materials and conditions.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26264"></a>
### [Accelerated Prediction of Surface Stability and Particle Morphology in Ionic Crystals via Electrostatic Screening](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26264v1)

**Authors:** Sourav Baiju, Payam Kaghazchi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26264v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Main problem.** Predicting surface stability and equilibrium crystal morphology in ionic materials is computationally expensive when using conventional Density Functional Theory (DFT).

**Main result.** An electrostatic-based screening approach provides a fast, scalable way to predict surface stability and morphology, capturing dominant trends across 3D and 2D materials without the cost of DFT.

**Method.** The method uses electrostatic energy evaluation of stoichiometric slab terminations, surface dipole moment calculations, and replica-exchange Monte Carlo simulations for surface reconstruction.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a computationally efficient method for predicting the surface stability and particle shape of ionic crystals. By using electrostatic analysis instead of expensive DFT calculations, the researchers can rapidly screen large numbers of surface configurations. The method successfully identifies stable facets and handles polar surfaces through Monte Carlo simulations. It is particularly useful for studying large-scale systems and high-index surfaces that are otherwise too complex for standard quantum mechanical simulations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on ionic crystals, including simple and complex 3D materials as well as 2D layered oxides.

**Key observables.** Surface stability, equilibrium crystal morphology, surface dipole moment, and electrostatic energies.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Surface dipole moment (used as a descriptor), electrostatic screening, and high-index surface configurations.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The approach assumes that electrostatic interactions capture the dominant trends in relative surface stability, bypassing the need for full electronic structure calculations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces a new electrostatic-based screening method, describes the implementation for identifying polar surfaces and reconstruction, validates the method against DFT and experimental data for 3D and 2D materials, and discusses the implications for high-throughput materials discovery.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This work presents a fast and scalable approach for predicting surface stability and equilibrium crystal morphology in ionic materials using electrostatic analysis. The method constructs stoichiometric slab terminations and evaluates their electrostatic energies, enabling high-throughput screening of surface configurations at a fraction of the cost of conventional approaches. Polar surfaces are identified through surface dipole moment calculations and stabilized via electrostatics-based reconstruction using replica-exchange Monte Carlo simulations. The surface dipole moment further emerges as an effective descriptor to distinguish the behavior of different classes of materials. By bypassing expensive Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations, the approach extends naturally to large systems and high-index surfaces that are typically inaccessible to DFT. Electrostatic interactions are shown to capture the dominant trends in relative surface stability across diverse material systems. The method is validated on simple and complex 3D materials as well as 2D layered oxides, where the predicted dominant facets are consistent with reported density functional theory and experimental observations. Importantly, the framework also reveals cases where high-index surfaces play a non-negligible role in the equilibrium morphology. These results establish electrostatics as a fast and reliable route for high-throughput prediction of surface stability and particle morphology, opening a pathway for accelerated materials discovery and providing a robust starting point for more detailed calculations in complex energy materials.

</details>

**Authors:** Kartick Ramakrishnan, Phani Motamarri  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26037v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The second row shows the off-diagonal multiplication of the local dofs with the rotation matrix, with diagonal entries set to zero (Qii = 0). **Main problem.** The challenge of performing accurate, large-scale Kohn-Sham density functional theory (DFT) calculations for complex systems (up to 10^5 electrons) on modern exascale GPU architectures without the communication bottlenecks of plane-wave methods.

**Main result.** The development of a GPU-centric, finite-element-based PAW (PAW-FE) method that achieves up to 20x speedup on AMD GPUs and significantly reduces time-to-solution compared to plane-wave and norm-conserving FE methods, demonstrating scalability to 130,000 electrons.

**Method.** A finite-element discretization of the Projector Augmented-Wave (PAW) formalism using a residual-based Chebyshev Filtered Subspace Iteration (R-ChFSI) eigensolver, optimized with mixed-precision arithmetic (FP32/TF32/BF16) and computation-communication overlap.

**Summary.** This paper presents a new computational method (PAW-FE) for large-scale electronic structure calculations. By combining finite-element discretization with the Projector Augmented-Wave method, the authors avoid the global communication bottlenecks of traditional plane-wave codes. The implementation is specifically designed for modern GPUs, using mixed-precision arithmetic and optimized kernels to achieve massive speedups. This allows for the simulation of much larger and more complex material systems, such as moiré superlattices, on exascale supercomputers.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The framework uses Kohn-Sham DFT within the PAW formalism and the frozen-core approximation, applied to large-scale systems such as Pt nanoclusters, WS2 slabs, and twisted bilayer WTe2.

**Key observables.** Ground-state electronic structure, pseudo electron density, magnetization density, total energy, and ionic forces.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System sizes up to 130,000 electrons; use of GGA exchange-correlation; multi-resolution quadrature; and various GPU precision modes (FP32, TF32, BF16).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Uses the frozen-core approximation and assumes a collinear spin formalism; relies on the efficiency of the R-ChFSI algorithm's tolerance to inexact matrix-multivector products.

**Figures summary.** The paper includes diagrams of the operator action for the PAW Hamiltonian, algorithmic descriptions of the SCF cycle and subspace construction, and performance comparison plots (speedup and scaling) against Quantum ESPRESSO and previous FE implementations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the computational bottleneck of large-scale DFT, defines the PAW-FE mathematical framework and discretization, details the GPU-centric algorithmic innovations (R-ChFSI and precision strategies), presents performance benchmarks on various GPU architectures, and demonstrates scalability on massive systems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Accurate large-scale Kohn-Sham density functional theory (DFT) calculations are essential for modeling complex material systems, including interfaces, defects, nanoclusters, and twisted two-dimensional heterostructures. Achieving chemical accuracy at scales of $10^4$-$10^5$ electrons with practical time-to-solution, however, remains challenging for existing DFT implementations. We present GPU-centric computational methods and algorithmic innovations within a finite-element (FE) discretized projector augmented-wave (PAW) formulation (PAW-FE) for accurate, efficient, and scalable electronic-structure calculations on modern exascale systems. The FE discretization, developed within a collinear spin formalism, accommodates generic boundary conditions and employs multi-resolution quadrature for accurate evaluation of atom-centered PAW integrals on coarse grids. The resulting generalized Hermitian eigenproblem is solved using residual-based Chebyshev filtered subspace iteration (R-ChFSI). Exploiting R-ChFSI's tolerance to inexact matrix-multivector products, we employ an approximate inverse PAW overlap matrix, mixed-precision arithmetic (FP32/TF32), and low-precision nearest-neighbor communication (BF16) during filtered subspace construction, along with block-wise computation-communication overlap to reduce cost while preserving robustness. These strategies yield up to $8\times$ and $20\times$ CPU-GPU speedups on Intel and AMD GPU architectures, respectively. Compared to plane-wave PAW methods, PAW-FE achieves close to 8$\times$ reduction in time-to-solution for 10,000-electron systems on NVIDIA GPUs, with larger gains at scale, and around 6$\times$ over norm-conserving FE approaches. We demonstrate scalability to 130,000-electron systems, establishing PAW-FE as an exascale-ready method for chemically accurate first-principles simulations.

</details>

### [Achieving Large Uniaxial and Homogeneous Strain in Two-Dimensional Materials](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26164v1)

**Authors:** Yangchen He, Jessica Kienbaum, Wuzhang Fang, Hongrui Ma, Ying Wang, Ping Yuan, Daniel A. Rhodes  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26164v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26164_figures/2604.26164_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
