(D) Cubic I213 structure with isolated H2 units (ELF isovalue 0.85). (E) Hexagonal P6522 structure featuring a three-dimensional hydrogen framework and isolated H−species (ELF isovalue 0.65). (F) Hexagonal P6/mm structure with...</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The high computational cost of ab initio methods (DFT/QMC) makes characterizing electronic bonding and the liquid-liquid transition in dense hydrogen extremely difficult. There is a need for an efficient, geometry-based way to predict the Electron Localization Function (ELF) topology.

**Main result.** A neural-network framework achieves high accuracy (R2 > 0.99) in predicting the 3D ELF of dense hydrogen directly from atomic geometry, successfully transferring from fluid to crystalline phases and enabling high-throughput evaluation of hydrogen networking.

**Method.** A multilayer perceptron regressor uses rotation-invariant descriptors (based on Gaussian radial functions and spherical harmonics) trained on AIMD data, supplemented by a Fourier-based long-range correction in logit space to account for non-local electronic contributions.

**Summary.** This paper presents a machine-learning approach to predict the electronic bonding topology of dense hydrogen without expensive quantum mechanical simulations. By training a neural network on atomic geometries, the authors can accurately map the Electron Localization Function (ELF) across different pressure regimes. The model effectively captures the transition from molecular to metallic hydrogen and can even estimate superconducting critical temperatures. This provides a high-throughput tool for studying the complex phase diagrams of hydrogen-rich materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is pure, dense hydrogen in both fluid and crystalline phases under high pressure (7-138.5 GPa) and high temperature (1500 K). The model is a neural network regressor trained on PBE-level density functional theory data.

**Key observables.** Electron Localization Function (ELF) topology, networking value (phi), hydrogen-network connectivity, and estimated superconducting critical temperature (Tc).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pressure (76.0 to 138.5 GPa), Temperature (1500 K), radial cutoff (3.0 A), and angular momentum (l_max = 2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes that the residual error is dominated by smooth, long-wavelength components that can be captured via Fourier expansion; it may struggle with low-symmetry structures with large, unseen interstitial regions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows training convergence and predictive performance; Figure 2 displays 2D slices of the ELF field and error analysis; Figure 3 illustrates the model's performance on crystalline structures and the prediction of networking values and Tc.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the computational bottleneck of electronic structure calculations, describes the neural network architecture and descriptor implementation, presents error analysis using Fourier transforms to identify long-range residuals, demonstrates transferability to crystalline phases, and concludes with applications to superconductivity estimation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a machine-learning framework to predict the electron localization function (ELF) of pure, dense hydrogen directly from atomic geometry, bypassing explicit electronic-structure calculations. Trained on first-principles data spanning multiple pressure regimes in dense fluid hydrogen, the model achieves high accuracy ($R^2 > 0.99$) and faithfully reproduces the global distribution of the ELF. A combined real- and reciprocal-space analysis reveals that the residual error is dominated by smooth, long-wavelength components with correlation lengths exceeding typical H--H bonding scales, and that the magnitude of these components increases systematically with pressure. Despite being trained exclusively on dense fluid hydrogen networks, the model transfers robustly to crystalline hydrogen configurations, preserving key features of ELF topology, including critical points and hydrogen-network connectivity. Taken together, these results suggest a viable route toward geometry-based, high-throughput evaluation of hydrogen-networking characteristics in both fluid and crystalline hydrogen.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26100"></a>
### [Hidden Crossover and Relaxor-Like Response from Emerging Polar Skyrmion Correlations in Ferroelectric Superlattices](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26100v1)

**Authors:** Zhiyang Wang, Fei Yang, Long-Qing Chen  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26100v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26100_figures/2604.26100_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. (a) DC dielectric susceptibility (blue dash-dotted curve) and spatially averaged polarization magnitude (orange dashed curve with cross markers) of the (Pb𝑥Sr1−𝑥TiO3)16/(PbTiO3)16 superlattice as functions of temperature for 𝑥= 0.3. (b) AC and DC dielectric susceptibilities of the same superlattice as functions of temperature for 𝑥= 0.3. The period 𝑡𝑝of the applied AC electric field is an integer multiple of the fundamental period 𝑡0 (see Supplementary Material). The black arrow indicates the direction of the dielectric-peak shift.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26100_figures/2604.26100_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. (a) Polar structures of the (Pb𝑥Sr1−𝑥TiO3)16/(PTO)16 superlattice (x=0.3) at T = 700 K, 500 K, 300 K, and 100 K, illustrating the progressive spatial organization of polar textures upon cooling. (b) Temperature dependence of the out-of-plane skyrmion correlation function, the correlation length (blue circles in the inset), and the oscillation amplitude (orange crosses in the inset). The colors of the correlation-function curves indicate temperature. (c) Polarization ratio 𝛾between Pb𝑥Sr1−𝑥TiO3 and PTO layers (blue circles), together with its first derivative (orange crosses) and second derivative (inset) with respect to 𝑇.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26100_figures/2604.26100_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. The calculated temperature-composition regime map of (Pb𝑥Sr1−𝑥TiO3)16/(PTO)16 superlattices. The green, blue, and or- ange regions denote the paraelectric regime, uncorrelated skyrmion regime, and interlayer-correlated skyrmion regime, respectively. Di- amonds mark the characteristic crossover temperature between the uncorrelated and correlated skyrmion regimes, while squares denote the ferroelectric transition temperature extracted from simulations. The color shading is a guide to the eye.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether the emergence of interlayer correlations between polar skyrmions in ferroelectric superlattices constitutes a new phase transition or a thermal crossover, and identifying the origin of the resulting relaxor-like dielectric response.

**Main result.** The study discovers a hidden thermal crossover where uncorrelated, layer-resolved skyrmions evolve into an interlayer-correlated ensemble, producing a broad dielectric peak and relaxor-like frequency dependence without the need for quenched disorder.

**Method.** Large-scale phase-field simulations using the time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau equation and the MuPRO software package.

**Summary.** This paper uses large-scale phase-field simulations to show that polar skyrmions in ferroelectric superlattices can undergo a thermal crossover from isolated to interlayer-correlated states. This crossover creates a broad peak in dielectric susceptibility and a frequency-dependent response similar to relaxor ferroelectrics, even in the absence of disorder. The findings suggest that topological defect correlations can act as an organizing principle for thermodynamic anomalies. This provides a new, disorder-free mechanism for tuning the functional properties of ferroic materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A ferroelectric superlattice consisting of alternating layers of (Pb_xSr_{1-x}TiO_3) and PbTiO_3, modeled using a 6th-order Landau-Devonlar free energy functional including gradient, elastic, and electrostatic energy terms.

**Key observables.** Spatially averaged polarization magnitude, DC and AC dielectric susceptibility, out-of-plane polarization autocorrelation function, and polarization ratio between layers.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature, Pb concentration (x=0.3), AC driving frequency, and the polarization contrast between layers.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is structurally ordered and free of quenched disorder or polar nanoregions; the simulation uses a discretized grid with specific boundary conditions (substrate and air layer).

**Figures summary.** Fig 1(a) shows the temperature dependence of dielectric susceptibility and polarization highlighting the first-order transition; Fig 2(a) visualizes the evolution of polar structures from uncorrelated to correlated states; Fig 2(b) shows the temperature dependence of the out-of-plane correlation length.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of skyrmion correlations, describes the phase-field modeling approach, presents the discovery of the hidden crossover and the associated dielectric anomaly, analyzes the mechanism of the relaxor-like response, and concludes with the implications for engineering functional properties via topological defect correlations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Polar skyrmions in ferroelectric superlattices are nanoscale topological polarization textures typically regarded as weakly coupled objects confined to individual layers, with a role secondary to that of the underlying symmetry-breaking order parameter. Here using large-scale phase-field simulations of ferroelectric superlattices, we uncover a hidden thermal crossover deep inside the ferroelectric phase, where polar skyrmions evolve from an uncorrelated, layer-resolved state into an interlayer-correlated ensemble. This crossover occurs without additional symmetry breaking or a new order parameter, but produces a pronounced broad peak in the dielectric susceptibility. The anomaly originates from the competition between correlation-enhanced response, associated with the growth of interlayer skyrmion correlations, and polarization-induced stiffness, which suppresses dielectric fluctuations at low temperature. Under AC driving, the peak shifts with frequency, resembling relaxor ferroelectrics despite the absence of quenched disorder or polar nanoregions. Our results establish a disorder-free route to relaxor-like dielectric response and identify topological defect correlations as an organizing principle for thermodynamic anomalies, providing a mechanism distinct from conventional critical behavior associated with symmetry breaking and divergent order-parameter fluctuations.

</details>

The green bars (left y-axis) show the execution time in sec- onds, while the blue line with triangular markers (right y-axis) represents the parallel eﬀiciency as a percentage. The calcula- tion was performed on a dual-socket Intel® Xeon® Platinum 8358P system with 64 physical cores. The results demonstrate significant speedup with increasing core count, achieving an 11.8× acceleration from 4 to 64 processes, while parallel eﬀi- ciency gradually decreases from 100% to 73.57% due to com- munication overhead.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The high computational cost of implementing hybrid exchange-correlation functionals (like HSE06) in the SIESTA code, specifically the difficulty of evaluating non-local Hartree-Fock-type electron repulsion integrals for large-scale systems.

**Main result.** A new, efficient, and scalable implementation of hybrid functionals in SIESTA that uses a Gaussian-type orbital (GTO) expansion to enable analytical integral evaluation via the libint library, achieving near-linear scaling and improved band gap predictions.

**Method.** The method transforms strictly localized Numerical Atomic Orbitals (NAOs) into a fitted expansion of Gaussian-type orbitals (GTOs) to leverage the libint library for four-center integral evaluation, integrated with SIESTA's real-space grid and sparse-matrix infrastructure.

**Summary.** This paper presents a major update to the SIESTA density functional theory code, enabling the use of expensive but accurate hybrid exchange-correlation functionals for very large systems. By approximating numerical atomic orbitals with Gaussian functions, the authors allow the code to use highly optimized analytical integral libraries. This approach significantly improves the prediction of electronic band gaps in semiconductors and 2D materials while maintaining computational efficiency. The new implementation is highly scalable, making high-accuracy first-principles simulations of large-scale materials accessible.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The implementation is applied to periodic electronic structures, including semiconductors, insulators, and 2D materials (e.g., Si, Diamond, graphene, and SrTiO3) within a Kohn-Sham DFT framework.

**Key observables.** Electronic band gaps, total energy, structural properties (lattice constants, bulk moduli), and parallel scaling efficiency.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mixing parameter (alpha), screening parameter (omega), number of Gaussians in the expansion, and integral screening thresholds.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The radial part of the NAOs can be accurately approximated by a compact Gaussian expansion, and the Gaussians must be truncated to maintain the strict localization required by SIESTA.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the supercell construction for Brillouin zone sampling; Figure 2 illustrates the Gaussian fit of a Carbon 2s orbital and the smoothing strategy at the cutoff radius; Figure 5 demonstrates the strong scaling performance and parallel efficiency of the implementation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the computational challenge of hybrid functionals, details the mathematical framework for the NAO-to-GTO transformation and integral evaluation, describes the implementation of the fitting and localization algorithms, and concludes with validation through benchmark calculations and scaling analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present an efficient and accurate implementation of hybrid exchange-correlation (XC) functionals in the SIESTA code, enabling large-scale simulations based on Hartree-Fock-type exact exchange combined with strictly localized numerical atomic orbitals (NAOs). Our approach exploits a fitted representation of the NAOs in terms of Gaussian-type orbitals (GTOs), which allows for the analytical evaluation of four-center electron repulsion integrals (ERIs) via the LIBINT library. This framework is seamlessly integrated with SIESTA's real-space grid and sparse-matrix infrastructure, and is combined with multiple screening techniques to control the computational complexity. We also introduce a fully analytical formulation of hybrid-functional forces and a dynamic parallel distribution scheme that ensures excellent scalability. We validate our implementation through benchmark calculations on a broad set of systems (including semiconductors, insulators, and two-dimensional materials) and demonstrate that the HSE06 functional significantly improves the prediction of band gaps compared to PBE, in close agreement with G0W0 and experimental data. We analyze in detail the trade-offs between accuracy and computational efficiency as a function of the number of Gaussians, basis set range, and integral screening thresholds. Our results confirm that hybrid functional calculations in SIESTA are now feasible for large extended systems, making accurate first-principles predictions of electronic and structural properties accessible at scale.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26510"></a>
### [Influence of a graphene substrate on the stabilization of molecular systems with hydrogen bonds](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26510v1)

**Authors:** Alexander V. Savin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26510v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Main problem.** Investigating how a graphene substrate influences the thermal stability and structural integrity of molecular systems held together by hydrogen bonds.

**Main result.** The graphene substrate helps stabilize beta-sheets of polyglycine and parallel Kevlar molecules up to 800K, with Kevlar showing even higher thermal stability due to preserved hydrogen bond chains.

**Method.** Numerical simulation of the molecular dynamics of planar multi-layer structures.

**Summary.** This paper uses numerical simulations to study how a graphene substrate can stabilize molecular structures like polyglycine beta-sheets and Kevlar molecules. It finds that these structures can maintain their shape and hydrogen bond networks at temperatures as high as 800K. Specifically, the study suggests that adding graphene to Kevlar fibers could significantly improve their thermal resistance. This work highlights the potential for using 2D materials to enhance the properties of organic polymers.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Planar two- and three-layer molecular structures consisting of polyglycine peptide beta-sheets and parallel Kevlar (para-aramid) molecules placed on a graphene sheet.

**Key observables.** Structural stability and retention of shape/hydrogen bond chains as a function of temperature.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (up to 800K).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The simulation assumes the dynamics are governed by the interactions between the molecular layers and the graphene substrate.

**Paper structure.** The paper presents a numerical simulation study, followed by results regarding the thermal stability of peptide and Kevlar structures, and concludes with the implications for enhancing Kevlar fiber stability.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Numerical simulation of the dynamics of planar two- and three-layer molecular structures formed by $β$-sheets of polyglycine peptide chains and systems of parallel Kevlar (para-aramid) molecules placed on a graphene sheet has been performed. It is shown that in these structures the $β$-sheets retain their shape, due to the presence of parallel chains of hydrogen bonds, up to a temperature of $T=800$K. An even higher stability is exhibited by the system of parallel Kevlar molecules. Here, the parallel chains of hydrogen bonds between peptide groups of neighboring molecules are preserved even at higher temperatures. The performed modeling allows us to conclude that the addition of graphene to Kevlar fibers can significantly increase their thermal stability.

</details>

### [Influence of strain on the anomalous Hall and Nernst effects in Fe thin films](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26257v1)

**Authors:** Ao Nakagawa, Ryo Toyama, Keisuke Masuda, Weinan Zhou, Hirofumi Suto, Kodchakorn Simalaotao, Yoshio Miura, Yuya Sakuraba, Tetsunori Koda  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26257v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</details>

<img src="2604.26257_figures/2604.26257_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2: (a) Fitting results of a scaling analysis (𝜌"!</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26257_figures/2604.26257_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3: (a) Magnetic field H dependence of the Nernst electric field normalized by the applied temperature gradient 𝐸" ∕∇𝑇.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26257_figures/2604.26257_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 4: Theoretical energy dependence of (a) the intrinsic AHC 𝜎!"</sub>

**Main problem.** The study aims to disentangle whether the strain-induced changes in the anomalous Hall effect (AHE) and anomalous Nernst effect (ANE) in Fe thin films are driven by intrinsic Berry curvature modulation or extrinsic scattering mechanisms.

**Main result.** The AHE is found to be predominantly governed by intrinsic mechanisms that follow theoretical predictions of Berry curvature modulation, whereas the ANE is strongly influenced by extrinsic contributions, leading to a significant deviation from theoretical predictions.

**Method.** The researchers used epitaxial Fe thin films on MgO and MAO substrates to vary lattice strain, performed scaling law analysis on magnetotransport data, and compared experimental results with first-principles DFT calculations.

**Summary.** This paper investigates how epitaxial strain affects the anomalous Hall and Nernst effects in iron thin films. By comparing films with different c/a ratios to theoretical Berry curvature calculations, the authors show that the Hall effect is primarily driven by intrinsic electronic structure changes. However, they find that the Nernst effect deviates significantly from theory due to strong extrinsic contributions. This distinction highlights the complexity of using strain to tune transverse transport in magnetic materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of 20 nm thick (001)-oriented epitaxial Fe thin films grown on MgO (tensile strain) and MgAl2O4 (compressive strain) substrates, covering a range of c/a ratios from 0.9825 to 1.0108.

**Key observables.** Anomalous Hall conductivity (AHC), anomalous Nernst conductivity (ANC), longitudinal resistivity, Hall resistivity, Seebeck coefficient, and c/a ratio.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lattice strain (tensile vs. compressive), tetragonal distortion (c/a ratio), temperature (15 K to 300 K), and magnetic field.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that using a single-element ferromagnet like Fe minimizes complexities from multicomponent stoichiometry, though it notes that impurities from different sputtering systems can affect the skew scattering sign.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows XRD profiles demonstrating epitaxial growth and strain-induced lattice constant shifts; Figure 2 presents scaling analysis for separating AHE components; Figure 3 shows Nernst electric field and coefficients as a function of c/a ratio; Figure 4 compares experimental c/a dependence with theoretical energy dependence.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of strain-induced effects, describes the experimental preparation of Fe films, presents structural characterization via XRD, details the magnetotransport and scaling analysis, compares results to DFT calculations, and concludes with a discussion on the differing physical origins of AHE and ANE.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The anomalous Hall effect (AHE) and anomalous Nernst effect (ANE) are the transverse transport phenomena in magnetic materials, which reflect the Berry curvature arising from the electronic structure near the Fermi level. Lattice strain provides a direct means to tune these effects by modifying the electronic structure; however, disentangling the strain-induced effect through the Berry curvature modulations in multicomponent materials is challenging due to complexities arising from extrinsic contributions by impurities and disorder, as well as difficulties in simple direct comparison with first-principles calculations. In this study, we focus on Fe, a prototypical single element ferromagnet with a well-established electronic structure, and tune the sign and magnitude of the strain in epitaxial thin films of by varying the substrates and deposition conditions to investigate the strain effect on the AHE and ANE. Scaling law analysis revealed that the intrinsic anomalous Hall conductivity (AHC) exhibits a clear tetragonal distortion (c/a) dependence, in good agreement with theoretical calculations based on Berry curvature modification. In contrast, the anomalous Nernst conductivity (ANC) shows a pronounced deviation from the theoretical values and markedly different c/a dependence. These results demonstrate a crucial difference in the physical origin between the AHC and the ANC in the Fe films; the AHC is predominantly governed by intrinsic mechanisms, whereas the ANC is strongly influenced by the extrinsic contribution.

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating how topological modifications, specifically the introduction of internal loops, affect the kinetics and rate of segregation of ring polymers under cylindrical confinement.

**Main result.** Increasing the number of small loops decreases the segregation time due to enhanced entropic repulsion, although antiparallel initial configurations and certain loop sizes can significantly delay segregation.

**Method.** Langevin dynamics molecular dynamics simulations using the LAMMPS software package with a Velocity Verlet integration scheme.

**Summary.** This study explores how the presence of loops in ring polymers affects how quickly they separate when confined in a cylinder. By simulating modified polymers, the authors show that adding more loops increases entropic repulsion, which actually speeds up the segregation process. The research also highlights how the initial orientation of the polymers can create kinetic barriers that slow down separation. These findings provide insight into how bacteria might use loop-extruding proteins to control the timing of chromosome segregation during cell division.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Two unconcatenated, topologically modified (ToMo) ring polymers (Kremer-Grest bead-spring chains) confined within a cylindrical geometry, mimicking E. coli chromosome segregation.

**Key observables.** Time of segregation (tau_seg), normalized distance between centers of mass (delta z_COM/L), and monomer density distributions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of loops, contour length of loops, degree of confinement (2Rg/D), and initial relative orientation (parallel vs. antiparallel).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The polymers are topologically constrained (no chain crossing), which does not account for the presence of topoisomerases in vivo.

**Figures summary.** Schematics of various topologies (Arc-0 to Arc-1-10), visualization of initialization protocols, time evolution of center of mass separation, and monomer density distributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the biological motivation, defines the polymer models and topological modifications, details the simulation methods and initialization protocols, presents results on the effect of loop number and size, analyzes the impact of initial orientation, and discusses biological implications and limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In Escherichia coli (E. coli), entropic repulsion between the two daughter DNA ring polymers under cylindrical confinement is believed to be an important factor governing chromosomal segregation. The repulsion can be enhanced by topological modifications, i.e., by the introduction of internal loops at certain locations along the contour of the circular DNA. However, the effect of topological modifications on the rate of segregation of ring polymers remains unclear. Therefore, we systematically varied the number and the contour length of loops introduced at selected locations by crosslinking monomers. The appropriate crosslinking was motivated by observations that extruded loops are located mainly near the origin of replication (ori-proximal) region of the E. coli chromosome. This resulted in the chains becoming intrinsically anisotropic. Using Langevin dynamics simulations of these topologically modified bead-spring polymers, we calculated the time required for segregation under cylinder confinement. With certain caveats, we found that increasing the number of loops resulted in a decrease in the time of segregation. In line with past work, we propose that this is due to the increase in the entropic repulsion between the polymers upon increasing the number of loops. In addition to the number of loops, the contour length of the loops and the mutual orientation of the (anisotropic) chains in the initial configurations played a role in determining the time of segregation.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26464"></a>
### [Linear poroelastic response of thin permeable gel films](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26464v1)

**Authors:** Caroline Kopecz-Muller, Joshua D Mcgraw, Thomas Salez  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26464v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26464_figures/2604.26464_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1: Schematic of the system. A point force F0δ(r) is applied at the surface of a permeable poroelastic medium, of shear modulus G and Poisson ratio ν. A fluid of viscosity η is flowing through the pores of the gel, whose permeability is denoted by k. The resulting vertical deformation profile represented by the thick black line is denoted by w(r, t). We compute the poroelastic response function Gτ(r, t) of the material.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26464_figures/2604.26464_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26464_figures/2604.26464_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3: Deformation profile of a finite-size poroelastic medium, for various thicknesses of the layer. A: Deformation ˆwτ(s, t) scaled by (F0/G)5/2/(πDpe), as a function of the dimensionless spatial frequency s p</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26464_figures/2604.26464_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4: Deformation profile of a poroelastic medium in real space. A: Deformation wτ(s, t) scaled by p</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.26464_figures/2604.26464_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5: Example: relaxation of the deformation profile generated by a gate-shaped pressure field. Deformation wτ(r, t) scaled by p</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Deriving the linear poroelastic Green's function to predict the space- and time-dependent mechanical response of a thin, permeable gel layer bounded to a rigid substrate.

**Main result.** The study provides an analytical expression for the poroelastic response, showing that deformation is localized to a radius comparable to the layer thickness and transitions from an incompressible to a compressible elastic state over time.

**Method.** The authors use an analytical approach involving Hankel and Laplace transforms in reciprocal space to solve the coupled poroelasticity equations and numerical inversion (Talbot algorithm) for real-space profiles.

**Summary.** This paper develops a mathematical framework to model how thin, porous gel coatings deform when a force is applied. It specifically addresses the challenge of finite thickness, showing that the mechanical response is confined to a region near the contact point. The researchers derived a Green's function that allows for the prediction of how these coatings relax from an incompressible to a compressible state. These findings are highly applicable to microfluidics, soft robotics, and biological membrane studies.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A thin, hydrophilic, and permeable poroelastic gel layer of thickness tau, bounded at the bottom by a rigid, impermeable substrate and exposed to a solvent bath at the top.

**Key observables.** Surface normal deformation profile w(r, t) and the poroelastic Green's function G_tau(r, t).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Layer thickness (tau), shear modulus (G), permeability (k), Poisson ratio (nu), and solvent viscosity (eta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Linear poroelasticity (small deformations), isotropic material, incompressible solvent, and a rigid/impermeable substrate.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the system and applied force; Figure 2 displays deformation in reciprocal space across different temporal frequencies; Figure 3 shows scaled deformation vs. spatial frequency; Figure 4 shows real-space deformation profiles at different times/thicknesses; Figure 5 illustrates the relaxation of a gate-shaped pressure field.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with a system definition and governing poroelastic equations, moves to a mathematical derivation using potential functions and transforms in reciprocal space, presents asymptotic analyses of various physical limits, and concludes with numerical results and applications to axisymmetric pressure fields.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

When a hydrophilic and deformable porous material is immersed in a bath, it may absorb the solvent and expand by several times its volume, thus forming a highly soft and porous hydrogel. A stress applied on the soft hydrogel surface deforms it and forces the absorbed solvent to move by flowing through the network of pores. This coupled phenomenon sets the framework of poroelasticity. Moreover, polymeric gels are often used in ultra-thin coatings to tune surface properties. Together with the characteristic poroelastic coupling, this thinness challenges the modelling of their response. In this article, we derive the point-force mechanical response of a thin, permeable and poroelastic layer bounded to a rigid substrate. We show that the gel surface is only deformed around the indentation point, within a radius on the order of the layer thickness. The obtained Green's function can be directly used to predict the space- and time-dependent surface deformation of the gel. Our findings are relevant for a broad range of applications, such as indentation experiments on swollen gels, thin membranes or soft and living systems, as well as lubrication problems involving a soft and porous wall, for instance in microfluidics.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26143"></a>
### [Mixture of Experts Framework in Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials for Atomistic Simulations](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26143v1)

**Authors:** Gabriel de Miranda Nascimento, Marc L. Descoteaux, Laura Zichi, Chuin Wei Tan, William C. Witt, Nicola Molinari, Sriteja Mantha, Daniil Kitchaev, Mordechai Kornbluth, Karim Gadelrab, Charles Tuffile, Boris Kozinsky  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26143v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26143_figures/2604.26143_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1: Dataset and static domain decomposition. (a) Representative configurations from the training dataset, which consists of 2,446 DFT frames including surface slabs, free-standing nanoparticles, isolated atomic clusters and CO in gas phase. (b) Illustration of the heuristic assignment strategy used to partition the system.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26143_figures/2604.26143_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2: Learning dynamics of independent vs. co-trained models. The top row shows the validation Mean Absolute Error (MAE) against ground truth DFT labels for total energy (left) and atomic forces (right). The co-training schemes (red, blue) achieve an accuracy balance between the pure high-fidelity (gray) and low-fidelity (light gray) baselines. The bottom row displays the discrepancy between the high-fidelity and low-fidelity model predictions on the bulk agreement dataset. Unconstrained co-training (red) leads to significant divergence, while constrained co-training (blue lines) successfully minimizes physical disagreement at the interface. Lower α values (dark blue) impose stronger...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26143_figures/2604.26143_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3: Model performance on held-out test set. Top row: MAE on energies and forces against DFT ground truth. The introduction of agreement constraints (α &lt; 1) does not degrade predictive accuracy compared to the unconstrained baseline (α = 1). Bottom row: Mean absolute difference between high-fidelity and low-fidelity model predictions on bulk test structures. Constrained co-training drastically improves physical consistency at the interface on unseen data.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26143_figures/2604.26143_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4: Equation of State (EOS) for bulk Platinum predicted by high-fidelity (dark blue) and low-fidelity (light blue) models. Top row: Models trained independently or co-trained without constraints exhibit significant mismatch in equilibrium volume and bulk modulus (B0), leading to artificial interface stress. Bottom row: Co-training with agreement constraints (α = 0.8, 0.5) forces the models to learn a consistent physical description of the bulk, aligning the EOS curves and minimizing mechanical mismatch at the interface.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.26143_figures/2604.26143_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5: Computational efficiency benchmark. (a) The 1,040-atom Pt+CO slab used for MD benchmarking. The high-fidelity region comprises adsorbates and surface Pt layers (NA = 440), while the low-fidelity region comprises the bulk Pt core (NB = 600). (b) Average wall-clock time per MD timestep for pure low-fidelity, pure high-fidelity, and the combined Multifidelity (Mixture-of-Experts) scheme.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The high computational cost of deep equivariant machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) limits large-scale atomistic simulations, while multi-fidelity approaches often suffer from mechanical mismatch and artificial stresses at the interface of different models.

**Main result.** The authors developed a Mixture-of-Experts framework that achieves over 2x computational speedup compared to high-fidelity models while maintaining predictive accuracy and physical consistency (energy conservation and mechanical alignment) in a Pt+CO catalytic system.

**Method.** A multi-fidelity 'Mixture-of-Experts' framework using a co-training strategy with agreement constraints to penalize energy and force discrepancies between high-fidelity and low-fidelity models on shared bulk environments.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a way to speed up large-scale atomistic simulations by using different levels of machine learning model complexity for different parts of a system. By applying expensive, high-accuracy models only to chemically active regions (like surfaces) and cheaper, low-accuracy models to the bulk, the researchers achieved a 2x speedup. Crucially, they used a special training technique to ensure that the boundary between these two models does not create unphysical stresses or energy errors. This allows for much larger and longer simulations of complex materials like catalysts without losing the accuracy of high-end methods.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** An E(3)-equivariant Allegro architecture applied to a 1,040-atom Pt+CO catalytic system, where the domain is spatially partitioned into a high-fidelity region (reactive interface) and a low-fidelity region (bulk lattice).

**Key observables.** Per-atom energy, atomic forces, equation of state, bulk modulus, and energy conservation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Alpha (weight of the agreement loss), number of layers and tensor features in the Allegro models, and the energy shift correction (E_ghost).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The domain decomposition is static (atom assignments are fixed at initialization) and the system can be partitioned into disjoint subsets.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows training dataset diversity and the atom partitioning strategy; Figure 2 displays validation errors and model disagreement during training; Figure 3 shows performance on held-out test sets; Figure 4 illustrates the Pt+CO slab structure and compares wall-clock time efficiency.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the computational bottleneck of MLIPs, presents the Mixture-of-Experts framework and the co-training strategy, details the implementation on a Pt+CO system, validates accuracy and mechanical consistency, and concludes with efficiency benchmarks and future directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

First-principles atomistic simulations are essential for understanding complex material phenomena but are fundamentally limited by their computational cost. While Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials (MLIPs) have drastically improved cost for a given accuracy, their inference cost remains a bottleneck for massive systems or long timescales. To address this, we introduce a multifidelity "Mixture-of-Experts" framework based on the E(3)-equivariant Allegro architecture. Our method spatially partitions the simulation domain into a chemically complex region (e.g., reactive interfaces) and a simple region (e.g., bulk lattice), assigning models of varying capacity to each. Among the challenges in such static domain decomposition, the mechanical mismatch between models at the interface is particularly critical, as it can generate artificial stress fields and instability. We address this challenge with a co-training strategy in which the loss function includes agreement constraints -- penalties on per-atom energy and force discrepancies between models evaluated on shared bulk environments -- forcing the independent models to learn a consistent physical description of the bulk material. We validate this approach on a realistic Pt+CO catalytic system, demonstrating that the co-trained models maintain exact energy conservation, align their bulk mechanical response (e.g., equation of state and bulk modulus), and achieve predictive accuracy comparable to a full high-fidelity simulation at more than twice the computational speed.

</details>

**Main problem.** The study aims to validate the transferability of a machine-learning interatomic potential (NEP89) to liquid Al-Ti alloys and to characterize their structural and dynamical properties across various temperatures and compositions.

**Main result.** The ML potential accurately reproduces experimental density, structure factors, and viscosity, though it fails to capture the Darken approximation and Stokes-Einstein relation in Al-rich regimes due to decoupling of tracer dynamics.

**Method.** Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations using the Neuro-Evolution Potential (NEP89) in the LAMMPS software, benchmarked against experimental neutron diffraction and electromagnetic levitation data.

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that a machine-learning potential trained on solid-state data can accurately simulate the properties of liquid Al-Ti alloys. The researchers successfully modeled density, structure, and viscosity, providing new predictions for diffusion coefficients. However, they identified significant breakdowns in the Darken approximation and the Stokes-Einstein relation in Al-rich melts. This work highlights both the power of transferable ML potentials and the complexity of transport dynamics in multicomponent metallic liquids.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A binary aluminum-titanium (Al-Ti) liquid metallic alloy system studied in the temperature range of 1550 K to 2200 K with aluminum mole fractions ranging from 0.0 to 1.0.

**Key observables.** Mass density, molar volume, excess volume, pair-correlation functions (PCF), static structure factor S(q), viscosity, self-diffusion coefficients, and inter-diffusion coefficients.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (1550-2200 K), Aluminum mole fraction (x_Al from 0.0 to 1.0), and the thermodynamic factor (Phi).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The machine-learning potential, originally trained on solid-state properties, is assumed to be transferable to the liquid state; the use of the soft-sphere model as a reference for local packing.

**Figures summary.** Figures show density and molar volume comparisons (Fig 1), excess volume trends (Fig 2), static structure factor for Ti (Fig 3), coordination numbers and molar volume (Fig 5), radial distribution function peak positions (Fig 6), structure factor Scc(q) (Fig 7), diffusion coefficients and interdiffusion components (Fig 11), temperature dependence of diffusion (Fig 12), and hydrodynamic radius vs atomic radii (Fig 13).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the ML potential and system, presents structural and thermodynamic validation against experiment, analyzes composition-dependent structural changes, investigates transport properties (viscosity and diffusion), and discusses the breakdown of standard transport models in specific concentration regimes.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the structural and dynamical properties of binary aluminum-titanium liquid metallic alloys, as a function of temperature and composition. We make use of MD-simulations, using a transferable machine-learning potential developed by Song et al. [Nature Communications 15, 10208 (2024)], and compare our results to experimental data. Although this potential was initially trained on solid properties, we find good agreement between the experimental data and the simulation results for the liquid state. The excess volume and compositional changes of the structure are captured well by the machine-learned potential. The simulation allows to disentangle local packing from chemical-ordering effects; the latter are found to be weak in Al-Ti. Dynamical quantities like the viscosity and the diffusion coefficients are also discussed.

</details>

### [Negative magnetoresistance in strained $α$-Sn and $α$-SnGe films in an in-plane magnetic field](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26168v1)

**Authors:** Sunny Phan, Andrei Kogan, Jesse Thompson, Trent Johnson, Alexander Khaetskii, Arnold Kiefer  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26168v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

All samples show qualitatively the same MR behavior with in-plane field and Ge-alloying slightly reduces the field effects on the samples.</sub>

</details>

<sub>FIG. 8. Black trace: measured Hall resistivity of Ge-alloyed α-Sn with magnetic field B normal to the film plane. Red trace: linear fit, up to B = 1 T. The negative slope corre- sponds to n-type carriers. a) Sample S2, b) Sample S3.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The study aims to determine if the chiral anomaly is the physical origin of negative magnetoresistance (MR) in strained alpha-Sn and alpha-SnGe films.

**Main result.** The authors find that negative MR persists in both the Dirac semimetal and the 3D topological insulator phase, leading them to reject the chiral anomaly as the primary mechanism and suggest a mechanism related to strong spin-orbit interaction.

**Method.** Magnetotransport measurements were performed on MBE-grown epitaxial films at 5 K using a 9-Tesla solenoid magnet, with structural characterization via X-ray diffraction and RHEED.

**Summary.** This paper investigates the origin of negative magnetoresistance in strained alpha-Sn and alpha-SnGe films. By comparing the transport properties of the Dirac semimetal phase and the 3D topological insulator phase, the researchers demonstrate that the negative MR occurs even when the chiral anomaly is absent. This suggests that the effect is likely driven by strong spin-orbit coupling rather than the chiral anomaly. The study also highlights how material quality and phase impurities like beta-Sn can complicate the interpretation of topological transport phenomena.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of strained, epitaxial thin films of pure alpha-Sn (a Dirac semimetal) and alpha-SnGe alloy (a 3D topological insulator) grown on CdTe substrates.

**Key observables.** Magnetoresistance (MR), Hall resistivity, carrier density, and transport mobility.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature of 5 K, magnetic field strength up to 8 T, and in-plane vs. out-of-plane field orientations.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The films are assumed to be elastically strained with negligible relaxation, and the analysis assumes the absence of significant current shunting from the substrate.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the Dirac point schematic and Hall bar geometry; Figure 2 displays MR for out-of-plane and parallel magnetic fields; Figure 3 shows in-plane MR for different orientations; Figure 4 and 5 present XRD scans and reciprocal space maps for strain analysis.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of negative MR, describes the material growth and device fabrication, presents magnetotransport results for different field orientations, discusses the rejection of the chiral anomaly hypothesis, and concludes with an analysis of material quality and potential confounding factors like beta-Sn formation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

To test the hypothesis that the chiral anomaly is responsible for negative magnetoresitance (MR) in \atn{}, we have studied magnetotransport in strained, epitaxial films of pure \aSn{} and the alloy \aSnGe{} that are in the Dirac semimetal and 3D topological insulator state, respectively. We have observed for both states a negative MR with current either parallel or transverse to the in-plane magnetic field, but with a different dependence of MR on $\vec{B}$ strength. Our results are inconsistent with the chiral anomaly and suggest that other mechanisms may be responsible for negative MR in the Dirac/Weyl semimetal phase of \aSn{}. We also discuss several factors in sample design and material quality that may be contributing to the incongruous observations of MR reported in studies of strained \atn{} films.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

Lines 1 and 2 are the ﬁts of the nonlocal voltage (circles) in the range of 1.453−1.478 K by two functions VNL1(T) and VNL2(T), taking into account σ1AL correction to the conductivity and two σ1AL and σ1AMT corrections, respectively. Line 3 is the ﬁt of the other part of the nonlocal voltage (circles) in the range of 1.481−1.491 K by the function VNL3(T) = RNL3(T)IcNL2(T). Line 4 is the ﬁt of the local resis- tance (squares) in the range of 1.460 −1.484 K by the function VL4(T), taking into account both σ1AL and σ1AMT corrections. Top inset:...</sub>

</details>

Line 1 - B = 0, 2 - 8.3, 3 - 12.3, 4 - 20.6, 5 - 25.7, 6 - 30.0, 7 - 48.4 G. Lines 1-2 and 4-7 are displaced up and down vertically relative to line 3, respectively. Inset: a sketch of the structure. The arrow indicates the direction of the applied direct current.</sub>

</details>

Lines 1 and 2 are the ﬁts of the nonlocal critical current (closed and open triangles) by the functions IcNL1(T1, B) and IcNL2(T2, B), respectively. Inset: a sketch of the structure.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 10</summary>

Lines 1 and 2 are the ﬁts of negative nonlocal resistance (closed and open cir- cles) by the functions RNL1(T1, B) and RNL2(T2, B), respectively. Inset upper: a sketch of the structure. Inset below: dash-dotted and solid lines are magnetic- ﬁeld dependences of the resistance R(x0, T2, B) and the quasiparticle diﬀusion length λQ(T2, B) at T2, calculated within the framework of the nonequilibrium SBT model, respectively.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the origin of anomalous negative nonlocal and local voltages (and resistances) in a quasi-one-dimensional superconducting aluminum structure near the critical temperature.

**Main result.** The negative voltages are attributed to quasiparticle charge imbalance at the N-S interface, and the experimental temperature dependence is best described by incorporating equilibrium superconducting fluctuations (AL and MT corrections).

**Method.** Low-frequency DC measurements were performed on aluminum wire networks in zero and varying magnetic fields, with experimental data compared against the nonequilibrium SBT model and equilibrium fluctuation theories.

**Summary.** This paper explores the phenomenon of negative local and nonlocal resistances in aluminum superconducting wires. The researchers found that when a narrow wire is in a normal state while a wider wire is superconducting, injecting current creates a quasiparticle charge imbalance. This imbalance leads to voltages with a sign opposite to the applied current. The study successfully reconciles experimental observations with theories involving both nonequilibrium charge imbalance and equilibrium superconducting fluctuations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A quasi-one-dimensional aluminum structure consisting of a wide superconducting wire and adjacent narrow wires, operating in a heterogeneous normal-superconducting (N-S) regime where $T_{cn} < T < T_{cw}$.

**Key observables.** Nonlocal voltage ($V_{NL}$), local voltage ($V_L$), nonlocal resistance ($R_{NL}$), local resistance ($R_L$), and critical currents ($I_c$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Critical temperatures ($T_c \approx 1.486$ K), coherence length ($\xi$), quasiparticle diffusion length ($\lambda_Q$), and magnetic field ($B$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The structure is treated as quasi-one-dimensional ($d \ll w_n < w_w < 2\xi(T)$) and the aluminum is in the dirty superconductor regime.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the resistive transition and structure sketch; Figure 2 displays $V(I)$ curves highlighting negative voltage peaks; Figure 3 shows nonlocal $V(I)$ curves; Figure 4 shows temperature dependence of negative resistances; Figure 5 shows critical current temperature dependence; Figure 9 shows critical current vs. magnetic field; Figure 10 shows negative resistance vs. magnetic field.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the experimental setup and the phenomenon of negative voltage, describes the measurement of resistive transitions and voltage peaks, analyzes the temperature and magnetic field dependence of resistances and critical currents, and concludes by comparing experimental results with nonequilibrium and equilibrium fluctuation models.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

To study a nonlocal electron transport in an aluminum superconducting quasi-one-dimensional structure, we measured negative nonlocal (local) direct current voltages in the structure in a magnetic field near the critical temperature. The structure is a normal-superconducting at $T_{cn}<T<T_{cw}$ ($T_{cn}$ and $T_{cw}$ are the critical temperatures for narrow and wide wires, respectively, making up this structure). Negative voltage arises due to a quasiparticle current flowing through the N-S interface. We plotted the experimental and theoretical temperature and magnetic-field dependences of current, resistance and voltage corresponding to the peak of negative voltage, taking into account either equilibrium or nonequilibrium superconducting fluctuations.

</details>

**Authors:** Micha C. J. Philipp, Lukas Köbbing, Alexander Karger, Andreas Jossen, Arnulf Latz, Birger Horstmann

**Main problem.** The study aims to understand and model the complex degradation mechanisms, specifically particle cracking and SEI growth, in lithium-ion batteries with silicon-graphite composite anodes during both cyclic and calendar aging.

**Main result.** The physics-based model accurately predicts capacity loss and silicon health across various temperatures and protocols, identifying that instantaneous SEI growth following crack formation is a dominant degradation driver during check-up cycles.

**Method.** The researchers used a combination of long-term experimental battery testing (up to 96 weeks) and a physics-based Single-Particle-Model with electrolyte effects (SPMe) implemented via the PyBaMM framework.

**Summary.** This paper presents a physics-based modeling approach to predict the degradation of next-generation lithium-ion batteries using silicon-graphite anodes. By coupling electrochemical and mechanical models, the authors can disentangle how particle cracking and SEI growth contribute to capacity loss. The model successfully captures battery behavior during both active cycling and long-term storage under various temperatures and usage protocols. This work is significant for optimizing the design and usage of high-energy-density batteries.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system is a lithium-ion battery with a silicon-graphite (Si-Gr) composite anode and an NCA cathode. The model uses coupled partial differential equations to describe SEI growth, mechanical particle cracking, and loss of active material.

**Key observables.** Capacity loss (CL), State of Health of Silicon (SoH_Si), Loss of Lithium Inventory (LLI), and Loss of Active Material (LAM).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (20C, 35C, 50C), State of Charge (SoC), Depth of Discharge (DoD), C-rate, and check-up (CU) frequency.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model focuses on moderate temperatures (primarily 20C) and assumes thermal dependencies are outside the current scope; it also uses a dimensionality reduction to SPMe for computational efficiency.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of silicon degradation mechanisms; Figure 2 compares simulated vs. measured capacity loss and SoH_Si; Figure 3 breaks down individual degradation contributions; Figures 5-7 show degradation trends across different SoC, DoD, and temperature regimes; Figure 9 shows storage degradation relative to CU frequency.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of Si-anode degradation, describes the physics-based model and its implementation, presents validation against cycling and storage experimental data, and concludes with a discussion on model limitations and future directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Higher energy density and longer lifetime are the requirements for next-generation lithium-ion batteries. A promising anode material is silicon, which offers high specific capacity, but its significant volume change during lithiation and delithiation enormously reduces battery lifetime. A physical understanding of the processes degrading the battery is key to mitigate this effect and advance in the field. We develop a physics-based model to describe degradation during battery cycling under various protocols and storage conditions, with varying check-up (CU) frequencies. The model can disentangle basic degradation mechanisms, such as the growth of the Solid-Electrolyte Interphase (SEI), from silicon mechanisms, such as particle cracking, SEI growth on cracks, and loss of active material (LAM). We investigate the impact of CUs on the observed storage degradation and the reason behind the increased degradation in batteries, including silicon in the anode. Additionally, we relate the observed degradation to operating conditions, enabling future optimization of battery use and design.

</details>

### [Polaron Conductivity in $α$-Fe2O3 Quenched by Adsorbed NO2](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26372v1)

**Authors:** Tushar K. Ghosh, Elvar Ö. Jónsson, Stephan Steinhauer, Panagiotis Grammatikopoulos, Hannes Jónsson  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26372v1>

</details>

<img src="2604.26372_figures/2604.26372_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3. Minimum energy path found using NEB calculations for polaron migration between two adjacent Fe</sub>

</details>

<img src="2604.26372_figures/2604.26372_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4. (a) Electron density difference, 𝜌𝜌diff surf = 𝜌𝜌Fe തതതത2O3−𝜌𝜌Fe2O3, between a charged and a neutral (0001)-Fe2O3</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Understanding the atomistic mechanism by which NO2 adsorption quenches polaron-mediated conductivity in hematite (alpha-Fe2O3) gas sensors.

**Main result.** NO2 adsorption induces a 0.72 e- electron transfer from the oxide to the molecule, which eliminates the localized Fe2+ polaron state and increases electrical resistance.

**Method.** Density Functional Theory (DFT) using the PBE+U approach combined with Nudged Elastic Band (NEB) calculations and Bader charge analysis.

**Summary.** This study provides a microscopic explanation for how the oxidizing gas NO2 affects the conductivity of hematite-based sensors. Using DFT+U calculations, the authors show that polarons preferentially accumulate at the surface and that NO2 adsorption pulls electrons away from the oxide. This electron transfer destroys the localized Fe2+ states, effectively quenching the charge carriers and increasing the material's resistance. These findings offer design principles for improving the sensitivity of iron oxide gas sensors.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of the Fe-terminated alpha-Fe2O3 (0001) surface and bulk, focusing on small-polaron hopping between Fe sites.

**Key observables.** Activation energy for polaron hopping, charge transfer amount, magnetic moments, and lattice parameters.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hubbard U = 4.3 eV, activation energy of 0.12 eV, and electron transfer of 0.72 e-.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Use of the PBE+U functional to correct self-interaction errors and the assumption that small-polaron hopping is the dominant transport mechanism.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1a shows the atomic structure; Figure 2 displays the atomic structure and electron density difference of the polaron; Figure 3 illustrates the minimum energy path for polaron migration.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the gas-sensing problem, validates the DFT+U approach against experimental lattice parameters and activation energies, details the polaron migration energy at the surface, analyzes the charge transfer during NO2 adsorption, and concludes with implications for sensor design.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Polaron-mediated charge transport in α-Fe2O3 plays a central role in its performance as a gas-sensing material, yet the atomistic interaction between surface adsorbates and polarons remains insufficiently understood. Here, density functional theory with Hubbard-U correction (DFT+U) combined with nudged elastic band calculations is used to investigate polaron formation, migration, and quenching at the Fe-terminated α-Fe2O3 (0001) surface. The calculated activation energy for small-polaron hopping in bulk α-Fe2O3 is found to be 0.12 eV, in excellent agreement with experimental measurements, confirming the validity of the computational approach. Slab calculations show that migration of the polaron from bulk to the surface lowers the energy by 0.12 eV, indicating preferential localization of charge carriers at the gas-solid interface. Adsorption of NO2 induces substantial electron transfer (0.72 e-) from the oxide to the molecule, eliminating the localized Fe2+ polaron state and thereby suppressing polaronic conductivity. These results provide a direct microscopic explanation for the resistance increase of hematite-based sensors upon exposure to oxidizing gases. More broadly, the study establishes how surface adsorption can modulate charge transport α-Fe2O3 through control of polaron populations, offering design principles for improved iron oxide gas sensors.

</details>

### [Predicting massive helium-3 release from metal tritides using simple mechanical modeling](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26697v1)

**Authors:** Berengere Evin, Dorian Gaboriau, Mathieu Segard, Sylvain Challet, Arnaud Fabre, Stephanie Thiebaut  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26697v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26697_figures/2604.26697_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

<img src="2604.26697_figures/2604.26697_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26697_figures/2604.26697_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26697_figures/2604.26697_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.26697_figures/2604.26697_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4 – He/Pd ratio evolution as a function of the theoretical He/Pd (no helium release). New dislocations modeling (solid line) is compared to semi-empirical modeling (dotted line) and experimental measurements of gas analysis (cross) [18].</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Explaining the sudden transition from long-term helium-3 retention to massive desorption in metal tritides during tritium aging.

**Main result.** A new dislocation-based mechanical model successfully predicts the helium desorption threshold and bubble growth evolution, matching experimental data more accurately than previous semi-empirical models.

**Method.** The study combines continuum mechanics for bubble growth with a dislocation-based mechanical model, comparing the results against experimental TEM, STEM, EELS, and NMR data.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the mechanism behind the sudden release of helium-3 from metal tritides like palladium tritide. The authors propose that as helium bubbles grow, they act as precipitates that impede dislocation motion, eventually reaching a hardness threshold that triggers massive gas release. By integrating dislocation physics into a continuum mechanics model, they accurately predict the timing and scale of this desorption. This mechanistic approach replaces older empirical models with a more predictive framework based on fundamental material properties.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system is palladium tritide (PdTx) where helium-3 produced by tritium decay is trapped in nanobubbles that act as solid-state precipitates.

**Key observables.** Bubble radius, bubble pressure, helium-to-palladium ratio (He/Pd), and dislocation mobility/strains.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Shear modulus (47 GPa), Burgers vector (0.27 nm), yield strength (170 MPa), and critical precipitate radius (~1.4 nm).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The desorption threshold is reached when dislocation mobility is blocked due to reaching maximum material hardness (equality of Orowan and Friedel strains).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows TEM images of palladium powder; Figure 2 shows bubble radius evolution; Figure 3 shows bubble pressure evolution; Figure 4 shows the He/Pd ratio vs. theoretical ratio.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of helium release, presents a new dislocation-based modeling approach, compares this model to previous semi-empirical methods, validates the model against experimental measurements, and discusses limitations regarding pressure estimation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This letter is presenting a simple but effective mechanism that explains why ,during tritium aging, metal tritides retain most helium-3 for years and then suddenly release massive amount. The mechanism is based on the hypothesis that dislocations blocking could explain the sudden change of behavior. The modeling of this phenomenon combine a mechanical and microstructural approach. The calculations made with this mechanism fit all the aging data acquired on aged palladium tritide

</details>

<sub>Fig. 14 compares the XMCD of the Ti3+ and Ti4+ within the D3h point group for different values of the crystal field parameters ∆µ and ∆ν. The experimental spectra is consistent with the presence of Ti3+, rather than Ti4+. Taking ∆µ and ∆ν for Ti3+ as +0.3 and -0.3 eV, respectively, qualitatively reproduces the experimental XMCD spectra. On the other hand, the simulated Ti4+ XMCD spectra is barely dependent on the crystal field parameters.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The study aims to understand the origin of complex magnetism, spin-flip transitions, and the anomalous Hall effect in the distorted kagome RTi3Bi4 (R = Nd, Sm, Gd) family, specifically disentangling the magnetic contributions of the rare-earth ions from the Ti kagome sublattice.

**Main result.** The researchers identified that the Ti kagome layer in GdTi3Bi4 is polarized by the Gd zigzag chains via Gd 5d electrons, and they found that the Sm3+ 4f electrons lift the Dirac cone above the Fermi level, making it a hole-type carrier.

**Method.** The study combines experimental techniques including ARPES, XAS, and XMCD with theoretical methods such as Density Functional Theory (DFT) and atomic multiplet theory simulations.

**Summary.** This paper investigates the electronic and magnetic properties of a specific family of distorted kagome metals. By using a combination of ARPES and X-ray spectroscopy, the authors show how the rare-earth zigzag chains interact with the Ti kagome layers to induce magnetism in the Ti sites. They also identify how the rare-earth 4f electrons influence the topology of the band structure, such as the position of Dirac cones. This work provides crucial insights into the mechanisms driving the anomalous Hall effect in these materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is the 134-kagome family of materials (R = Nd, Sm, Gd) characterized by an orthorhombic distorted kagome lattice of Ti atoms, Bi hexagonal/triangular lattices, and interleaved zigzag chains of rare-earth atoms.

**Key observables.** Electronic band structure, Fermi surfaces, Dirac points, van Hove singularities, spin and orbital magnetic moments, and oxidation states.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperatures as low as 2 K, magnetic fields up to 6 T, and the R3+ oxidation state.

**Assumptions / limitations.** DFT simulations neglected spin-orbit coupling in some instances, and XAS/XMCD simulations used reduced Slater integrals and spin-orbit coupling (80% of atomic values).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the unit cell and Brillouin zones; Figure 2 displays valence band dispersions and DFT-calculated band structures; Figure 3 presents XAS spectra and magnetic hysteresis curves.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the kagome material family, presents experimental electronic structure mapping via ARPES, details the magnetic characterization via XAS and XMCD, compares results with DFT and multiplet theory simulations, and concludes with the implications for understanding the anomalous Hall effect.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Kagome materials are known for hosting emergent quantum phenomena driven by the interaction between different lattice, charge and spin orders. Here, we present a detailed angle resolved photoemission (ARPES), density functional theory (DFT) and x-ray magnetic circular dichroism (XMCD) study of the electronic and magnetic structure of $R$Ti$_3$Bi$_4$ ($R$ = Nd, Sm, Gd). ARPES and DFT demonstrate that the bulk electronic band structure is dominated by the hybridization of the Ti bands, and the weak electron-like pocket at $Γ$ is identified as a surface state. The isotropic XAS profile of the $M_{4,5}$-edge of the rare earth is consistent with the presence of $R^{3+}$ oxidation state. Using the XMCD sum rules, backed by the atomic multiplet theory calculations, we obtain the spin and orbital magnetic moments. The Ti $L_{2,3}$-edge XMCD reveals the presence of a small magnetic moment in GdTi$_3$Bi$_4$, presumably driven by the proximity of the {Ti} kagome layers to the $zigzag$ chains of Gd, while the total magnetic moment of Gd is shared by the $f$ and $d$ electrons. Our combined XMCD, ARPES and DFT study brings an important piece of information to understand the spin flip transitions and anomalous Hall effect observed in the $R$Ti$_3$Bi$_4$ kagome metals.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26300"></a>
### [Strain and Twist Engineering of Interfacial Thermal Transport in Homo- and Hetero-Interfaces of Graphene and Hexagonal Boron Nitride](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26300v1)

**Authors:** Wenwu Jiang, Huasong Qin, Yilun Liu, Wengen Ouyang, Oded Hod, Michael Urbakh  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26300v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26300_figures/2604.26300_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the model system and setup for thermal transport simulations in homogeneous</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26300_figures/2604.26300_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Frequency resolved thermal conductance (𝐺(𝜔)) of homogeneous graphene (a) and h-BN (b)</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26300_figures/2604.26300_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3(a) shows the calculated vertical thermal conductivity through the graphene/h-BN stack (⊥)</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26300_figures/2604.26300_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. (a) Strain (black circle, lower horizontal axis) and twist-angle (red triangle, upper horizontal</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating how mechanical perturbations, specifically in-plane strain and interfacial twist, affect the vertical thermal conductance of both homogeneous (graphene/graphene, h-BN/h-BN) and heterogeneous (graphene/h-BN) van der Waals interfaces.

**Main result.** Homogeneous interfaces show reduced thermal conductance under both strain and twist, whereas heterogeneous graphene/h-BN interfaces are insensitive to twist but show significant conductance changes under strain (increase under compression, decrease under tension).

**Method.** Atomistic simulations using Non-Equilibrium Molecular Dynamics (NEMD) with the LAMMPS package, supplemented by Fermi's golden rule and density of phonon modes analysis.

**Summary.** This paper explores how mechanical strain and twisting can be used to engineer heat transport in 2D material interfaces. It reveals a fundamental difference between similar material stacks (graphene/graphene) and different material stacks (graphene/h-BN), where the latter is uniquely sensitive to strain but not twist. The researchers provide a simple model based on local atomic distances and stacking to predict these thermal properties. This work is crucial for designing efficient heat dissipation strategies in future nanoscale electronic and spintronic devices.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on 16-layer van der Waals stacks of graphene and hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN), utilizing anisotropic interlayer potentials and REBO/Tersoff intralayer potentials.

**Key observables.** Vertical thermal conductivity (kappa_perp), interfacial thermal conductance (ITC), frequency-resolved thermal conductance G(omega), and local registry index (LRI).

**Important parameters / regimes.** In-plane biaxial lateral strain (epsilon), interfacial twist angle (theta), and local interlayer distance (d_i).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the validity of the atomistic potentials (REBO and Tersoff) and uses a phenomenological model based on local geometric characteristics to capture the transport behavior.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the simulation setup, vertical thermal conductivity as a function of strain, and normalized ITC comparisons. Figure 2 displays frequency-resolved thermal conductance under various strains. Figure 3 shows the dependence of vertical thermal conductivity on specific parameters.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of thermal management in vdW heterostructures, describes the simulation methodology and physical models, presents results for homogeneous and heterogeneous interfaces, provides a physical mechanism via phonon analysis, and concludes with a validated phenomenological model.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

A dramatic difference between the vertical thermal conductance response of homogeneous and heterogeneous graphene/h-BN interfaces to external mechanical perturbations, is predicted. Homogeneous graphene and h-BN interfaces exhibit strong conductance reduction for both in-plane strain and interfacial twist. Conversely, the vertical thermal conductance of the heterogeneous graphene/h-BN junction is insensitive to twist deformations but shows significant increase or decrease under compressive or tensile strains, respectively. Our atomistic simulations predictions are rationalized by Fermi's golden rule and density of phonon modes analyses, indicating that vertical phonons and local stacking configurations have a central role in the interlayer heat transport behavior. A simple phenomenological model, based on local interlayer distance and stacking, captures well the dependence of vertical heat conductance on strain and twist deformations.

</details>
