<a id="paper-2604.26665"></a>
### [Third-order intrinsic anomalous Hall effect as a transport fingerprint of altermagnets](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26665v1)

**Authors:** Longjun Xiang, Hao Jin, Jian Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26665v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26665_figures/2604.26665_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. The hierarchy of IAHE across collinear quantum magnets. (a) The ferromagnet (FM) with a spin-polarized Fermi-surface can be identified by the linear IAHE. (b) The PT -symmetric antiferromag- net (AFM) with a spin degenerate Fermi surface can be identified by the second-order IAHE. (c) The altermagnet (AM) with an alterna- tive spin-splitting Fermi surface can be identified by the third-order IAHE (This work). Note that the opposite-spin sublattices in AFM are connected with PT or T τ1/2 symmetry, while the opposite-spin sublattices in AM are connected with CnT or MaT symmetry2.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.26665_figures/2604.26665_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Third-order IAHE in 2D Lieb-lattice altermagnet. (a) The 2D Lieb-lattice. Here the blue dashed square labels the unit cell. (b) The spin-polarized band dispersions of Eq. (4) with λz = 0. (c) The band dispersions of Eq. (4) with λz = 0.01t1. Here the colorbar denotes the expectation value of σz. (d) The dependence of σ(0/2) yxxx and σ(0/2) yxyy on the chemical potential µ. (e) The quantum- geometric decompositions of σ(0) yxxx. (f) The normalized k-resolved integrand (contributed by BCQ) for the resonant peak µ1 of σ(0) yxxx. Parameters: 4t1 = −0.4eV, 2t2 = +0.025eV, 2td = +0.125eV, J = −0.165eV, T = 100K, and τ = 10−14s.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.26665_figures/2604.26665_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Third-order IAHE in van der Waals altermagnet V2Se2O. (a) and (b): The band dispersions of V2Se2O2 without and with SOC, respectively. (c) The dependence of σ(0/2) yxxx and σ(0/2) yxyy on the chem- ical potential µ. To evaluate σ(2) abcd, we choose τ = 10−14s. (d) The quantum-geometric decomposition of σ(0) yxxx. (e) and (f): The nor- malized k-resolved integrand (contributed by BCQ) for the µ1 and µ2 resonant peaks of σ(0) yxxx.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26665_figures/2604.26665_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Third-order IAHE in 2D Lieb-lattice altermagnets with a spin-flip SOC. (a) The band dispersions of Eq. (4) with an additional spin-flip SOC (λ∥= 0.2t1). (b) The quantum-geometric decompo- sitions of σ(0) yxxx. (c-d) The normalized k-resolved integrand (con- tributed by AQMD) for σ(0) yxxx for the µ1 and µ2 peaks, respectively.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** Determining whether an intrinsic counterpart to the third-order extrinsic anomalous Hall effect exists to serve as a diagnostic transport fingerprint for altermagnets.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that the third-order intrinsic anomalous Hall effect (IAHE) is generically allowed in altermagnetic spin Laue groups and identifies the Berry curvature quadrupole as its microscopic quantum geometric origin.

**Method.** The authors use spin-group symmetry analysis, semiclassical theory, and quantum-geometric decomposition of the conductivity tensor into Berry curvature and quantum metric contributions.

**Summary.** This paper identifies the third-order intrinsic anomalous Hall effect as a unique transport fingerprint for altermagnets, distinguishing them from ferromagnets and antiferromagnets. By using symmetry analysis and quantum geometry, the authors show that this effect arises from the Berry curvature quadrupole. They demonstrate a resonant enhancement of the effect near altermagnetic band crossings and suggest it is experimentally detectable in materials like V2Se2O. This work establishes a new hierarchy of Hall effects across different classes of collinear quantum magnets.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on altermagnets, specifically utilizing a 2D Lieb-lattice model and the experimentally realized van der Waals altermagnet V2Se2O.

**Key observables.** Third-order intrinsic anomalous Hall conductivity, Hall voltage, Berry curvature quadrupole (BCQ), and acceleration quantum metric dipole (AQMD).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spin-orbit coupling (SOC) strength, chemical potential near band crossings, and the moderately dirty regime (scattering time ~ 10^-14 s).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis focuses on the intrinsic nature of the effect and assumes a moderately dirty metallic regime; it does not discuss the T-even third-order extrinsic effect.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the hierarchy of IAHE across FM, AFM, and AM; Figure 2 displays the Lieb-lattice structure and band dispersions; Figure 4 shows band dispersions with spin-flip SOC and the quantum-geometric decomposition of conductivity.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by establishing the hierarchy of IAHE, moves to symmetry analysis of the third-order IAHE tensor, provides a quantum-geometric decomposition of the effect, analyzes specific models (Lieb-lattice and V2Se2O), and concludes with experimental feasibility estimates.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The intrinsic anomalous Hall effect (IAHE) provides a powerful transport fingerprint of quantum magnets, with its linear and second-order responses distinguishing ferromagnets and $\mathcal{P}\mathcal{T}$-symmetric antiferromagnets, respectively. Altermagnets, as an emergent class of quantum magnets, have recently been shown to host a third-order extrinsic anomalous Hall effect, raising a question of whether an \textit{intrinsic} counterpart can serve as a diagnostic of altermagnetic order. Based on spin-group symmetry analysis, we demonstrate that the third-order IAHE is generically allowed in the ten spin Laue groups relevant to altermagnets when spin-orbit coupling (SOC) is taken into account. By combining these symmetry constraints with the anomalous velocity induced by the second-order Berry curvature, we uncover a resonant third-order IAHE arising near the altermagnetic band crossings at generic momenta in both the Lieb-lattice altermagnet and the experimentally realized altermagnet V$_2$Se$_2$O. Notably, we identify the Berry curvature quadrupole, encoded in the second-order Berry curvature and activated by finite SOC, as the microscopic quantum geometric origin of this resonance. Our results establish the third-order IAHE as an intrinsic quantum geometric transport fingerprint of altermagnets and extend the hierarchy of IAHE across collinear quantum magnets.

</details>

### [Tracking visible pulsed laser annealing of Hf0.5Zr0.5O2 heterostructures with in situ transmission electron microscopy](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26718v1)

**Authors:** Aida Amini, Shruti Verma, Katharina Kohlmann, Sebastian Obernberger, Jean-Christof Lamanque, Andreas Rüdiger, Kenneth R. Beyerlein  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26718v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

SAED pattern of 15- nm-thick HZO heterostructure (e) before and (f) after laser titration PLA at 183 mJ/cm2.</sub>

</details>

<img src="2604.26718_figures/2604.26718_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3. Measured SAED radial distribution intensity profiles and best-fit model patterns are shown for the (a) 7-nm (𝜒2 = 0.032), (b) 8-nm (𝜒2 = 0.021), (c) 15-nm (𝜒2 = 0.060) HZO heterostructure samples. As-deposited and annealed profiles correspond to measurements made on the same area of the sample before and after laser intensity titration PLA experiments. **Main problem.** Understanding the crystallization dynamics and phase evolution of Hf0.5Zr0.5O2 (HZO) thin films during pulsed laser annealing to maximize the ferroelectric orthorhombic phase.

**Main result.** The study identified an optimal 8-nm HZO thickness and a single-shot laser energy density of 177 mJ/cm2 that produces an 86% ferroelectric orthorhombic phase fraction, following a kinetic crystallization pathway.

**Method.** In situ transmission electron microscopy (TEM) combined with nanosecond pulsed visible laser irradiation and finite element simulations to model heat transfer.

**Summary.** This paper investigates how visible pulsed laser annealing can be used to control the phase formation in HZO thin films. By using in situ TEM, the researchers tracked the crystallization process in real-time. They found that an 8-nm thick film reaches a high ferroelectric phase fraction when pulsed with a specific energy density. The study also reveals that the melting of the substrate helps regulate the peak temperature, supporting a specific kinetic pathway for phase transformation. This work provides a roadmap for using laser-driven engineering to create high-performance ferroelectric materials for advanced electronics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A Si3N4/TiN/Hf0.5Zr0.5O2 thin-film heterostructure designed to mimic CMOS device structures, using TiN as a visible-light absorbing heat source.

**Key observables.** HZO crystallinity, phase fraction (orthorhombic, tetragonal, and monoclinic), and SAED ring patterns.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Laser energy density (fluence), HZO film thickness (7, 8, and 15 nm), heating rates (up to 3e11 C/s), and peak temperatures (up to 1900 C).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The heat transfer model assumes a Gaussian spatial and flat-top temporal heat source and incorporates the partial melting of the Si3N4 substrate.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows SAED images and TEM boundaries of crystalline/non-crystalline regions; Figure 2 plots crystallization threshold fluence vs. thickness; Figure 3 shows SAED radial distribution intensity profiles and phase models; Figure 6 shows SAED radial distribution profiles.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of HZO phase stabilization, describes the in situ TEM/laser experimental setup, presents quantitative phase analysis of SAED patterns, discusses thickness-dependent threshold behavior, provides thermal modeling via finite element simulations, and concludes with implications for scalable electronic applications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Laser annealing offers a promising route to back end of the line fabrication of ferroelectric thin film transistors based on hafnium-zirconium oxide (HZO). Due to the wide band gap of this material, previous reports have studied the crystallization of HZO using ultraviolet or infrared light. In contrast, we monitor its crystallization in a Si3N4/TiN/Hf0.5Zr0.5O2 thin film heterostructure upon irradiation with visible nanosecond laser pulses. This geometry mimics the structure of CMOS devices and harnesses the absorption of TiN in the visible regime to generate the heat necessary for the transformation. Through a series of local in situ measurements using a modified transmission electron microscope, we quantify the relationship between the HZO film thickness, critical laser energy density and the ferroelectric HZO phase fraction, finding a sharp threshold behavior in the laser pulse energy necessary to crystallize HZO. The optimal condition of irradiating an 8-nm HZO film with a single laser pulse with an energy density of 177 mJ/cm2 is found to produce 86% of the ferroelectric orthorhombic phase. Heat transfer dynamics within the heterostructure during laser annealing are revealed by finite element simulations, where the partial melting of the silicon nitride substrate is found to play an important role limiting the temperature to 1900 °C. This finding as well as the observed laser pulse energy threshold behavior support a kinetic crystallization pathway involving the tetragonal phase. More generally, these findings show how laser-driven phase engineering can lead to scalable design and enhanced performance of ferroelectric materials in advanced electronic applications.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.26643"></a>
### [Tunable high-Chern-number Chern insulators in rhombohedral tetralayer graphene/hBN moiré superlattices](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26643v1)

**Authors:** Chuanqi Zheng, Chushan Li, Ke Huang, Chenyu Zhang, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Hao Yang, Dandan Guan, Liang Liu, Shiyong Wang, Yaoyi Li, Hao Zheng, Canhua Liu, Jinfeng Jia, Xueyang Song, Zhiwen Shi, Guorui Chen, Xiao Li, Tingxin Li, Xiaoxue Liu  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26643v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Main problem.** The study aims to investigate the emergence and tunability of high-Chern-number Chern insulating states in hole-doped rhombohedral tetralayer graphene/hBN moiré superlattices.

**Main result.** The researchers discovered multiple high-Chern-number Chern insulators, including new symmetry-broken states with C = +3, ±2, and ±1 at fractional fillings, and demonstrated that these states are highly tunable via moiré wavelength, electric field, and magnetic field.

**Method.** The study uses transport property measurements across a range of twist angles and alignment orientations in rhombohedral tetralayer graphene/hBN heterostructures.

**Summary.** This paper explores the discovery of various high-Chern-number Chern insulators in rhombohedral tetralayer graphene/hBN moiré superlattices. By investigating the hole-doped side, the authors identified new symmetry-broken states with Chern numbers such as +3, ±2, and ±1 at fractional fillings. They demonstrate that these topological phases can be precisely controlled using the moiré wavelength, displacement electric fields, and magnetic fields. This work highlights the high degree of tunability available in these specific moiré systems for engineering topological landscapes.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is a rhombohedral tetralayer graphene (RTG) moiré superlattice formed by alignment with hexagonal boron nitride (hBN), specifically focusing on the hole-doped regime.

**Key observables.** Chern numbers (C), moiré filling factors (v), and transport properties.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Moiré wavelength, displacement electric field, external magnetic field, twist angle, and alignment orientation.

**Paper structure.** The paper presents an investigation of transport properties in RTG/hBN moiré superlattices, detailing the observation of various Chern insulating states at specific filling factors and discussing their dependence on external tuning parameters.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Moiré superlattices based on rhombohedral multilayer graphene have emerged as a highly tunable platform for engineering correlated topological phases. Here, we systematically investigate the transport properties of the hole-doped side in rhombohedral tetralayer graphene/ hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) moiré superlattices across a range of twist angles and alignment orientations. Notably, we observed multiple high-Chern-number Chern insulators, including the previously reported integer Chern insulator with Chern number C = -4 at moiré filling factor v = -1 and newly discovered symmetry-broken Chern insulating states with C = +3, $\pm$2, $\pm$1 at fractional moiré fillings of v = -2.5 or -2.6. These Chern insulating states emerge in both hBN alignment, but exhibit a sensitive moiré wavelength dependence. Our findings demonstrate the exceptional tunability of these high-Chern-number states via moiré wavelength, displacement electric field and external magnetic field, underscoring the distinct topological landscape realized in hole-doped RTG/hBN moiré superlattices.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.26739"></a>
### [Universal magnetotunnel conductance at a Weyl semimetal-layered Chern insulator junction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26739v1)

**Authors:** Nirnoy Basak, Sumathi Rao, Faruk Abdulla  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.26739v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.26739_figures/2604.26739_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. (a) Schematic diagram of the set up: A junc- tion of a WSM (bottom) and a LCI slab (top) is placed in a perpendicular magnetic field. In the WSM slab, currents carried by only the chiral Landau level states (green arrows) can transmits to LCI slab. The interface states (see Fig. 2) which are localized along z but are propagating in the plane of the interface redirects the current carried by the chiral LL states to the chiral edge states (green arrows on the y-z sur- faces) of the LCI slab. (b) Summary of the main result: There are two regimes for magneto-tunnel conductance G- a linear regime which is followed by saturation to a universal value Gsat = e2</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

(a) When κ = 0, the interface hosts the Fermi arc surface states from the WSM side and the chiral egde states from the LCI side. At finite coupling, they hybridize. The interface-localized states form continuous open contour that connects the projected Weyl nodes on the WSM side through the boundary of the the interface BZ. The first and second row are for two different values of the bulk parameter vr = 0.2 and 1 respectively.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

In (a) the bulk parameter vr = vy/vx=1.0 and in (b) it is 0.2. The system size along the x and y direction are Lx = Ly = 100. The chemical potential (measured relative to the energy of the Weyl point) is µ = 0.1. Unless interface coupling parameter κ is small (see main text for an explanation), the conductance saturates to a universal value Gsat = e2</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.26739_figures/2604.26739_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.26739_figures/2604.26739_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. (a) Magnetic field induced gap in the bulk of the WSM slabs and (b) the universal magneto-tunnel conduc- tance across the junction. For the junction, we adopt the model introduced in Eq. 1 of our previous work [22] with momentum space separation between the nodes Q = 2k0 = π and 5.6 for bottom and top slabs respectively. We assumed tunnel coupling strength κ = 1.0 and the system size along x, y directions to be Lx = 100 and Ly = 50 respectively. For a chemical potential µ = 0.1, the junction effectively behaves like a WSM-LCI junction for flux values beyond ϕ/ϕ0 ≈0.011 (marked by a blue dot in (a)). (b) As expected, the conduc- tance saturates to universal value Gsat at the...</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.** The study investigates electronic transport across a junction between a Weyl semimetal (WSM) and a layered Chern insulator (LCI) under a perpendicular magnetic field, specifically seeking the origin of universal conductance saturation.

**Main result.** The researchers find that magnetotunnel conductance increases linearly with the magnetic field at low fields and saturates to a universal value independent of microscopic interface details, driven by topological charge pumping from the Chern layers.

**Method.** The study employs exact diagonalization of a coupled Hamiltonian to find the interface spectrum and uses KWANT simulations to compute the magnetotunnel conductance.

**Summary.** This paper explores how transport behaves at the interface of a Weyl semimetal and a layered Chern insulator. It reveals that while conductance initially grows with a magnetic field, it eventually reaches a constant, universal plateau. This plateau is determined by the topological properties of the Chern insulator rather than the specific details of the interface. The findings suggest that this universal behavior is a robust feature of topological charge pumping.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A junction between a gapless Weyl semimetal and a gapped layered Chern insulator in a slab geometry, subject to a perpendicular magnetic field. The model uses a two-band lattice Hamiltonian for the WSM and a stack of 2D Chern insulators for the LCI.

**Key observables.** Magnetotunnel conductance (G), interface-localized band dispersion, and the critical magnetic field (Bc).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field (B), interface coupling strength (kappa), Weyl node separation (k0), velocity anisotropy (vr), and system dimensions (Lx, Ly).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system assumes a specific slab geometry and a 90-degree rotation of the LCI lattice to ensure orthogonal crossing of surface states at zero coupling.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the WSM/LCI junction and a summary of conductance results; Figure 3 displays magnetotunnel conductance under different velocity anisotropies; Figure 4 demonstrates the robustness of the conductance plateau.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the WSM-LCI junction problem, describes the Hamiltonian and physical model, presents the numerical transport results and the discovery of universal conductance, compares the results to WSM-WSM junctions, and discusses the robustness against disorder.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate electronic transport across a junction between a Weyl semimetal (WSM) and a layered Chern insulator (LCI) in the presence of a magnetic field perpendicular to the interface. The topological mismatch between the gapless Weyl semimetal and the momentum-resolved chiral edge modes of the layered Chern insulator leads to interface Fermi-arc states with a qualitatively distinct connectivity: unlike WSM-WSM junctions, the interface Fermi arcs are forced to reconnect through the Brillouin-zone boundary rather than terminating at the projections of the Weyl nodes. We analyze the spectrum and compute the magneto tunnel conductance mediated by the interface-localized states. We find that the conductance increases linearly with magnetic field at low fields and saturates beyond a critical field to a constant value that is independent of microscopic details such as interface coupling, arc geometry, and lattice-scale parameters. This universal saturation reflects a transport mechanism governed by the topological charge pumping associated with the Chern layers, rather than magnetic breakdown between Fermi arcs. We further show that, under specific conditions, a junction between two distinct Weyl semimetals can exhibit a similar saturation behavior, thereby clarifying the topological origin of the observed universality.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

**Main problem.** The study aims to correct the established crystal structure of the high-temperature ceramic Al5C3N, specifically resolving whether it possesses a non-centrosymmetric (P63mc) or centrosymmetric (P63/mmc) space group.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that Al5C3N crystallizes in the centrosymmetric P63/mmc space group with a disordered C/N distribution, supported by X-ray/neutron diffraction, DFT energy stability, and STEM imaging.

**Method.** The research employs a combination of high-temperature synthesis, single-crystal X-ray diffraction, neutron powder diffraction, STEM (HAADF/ABF), and Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations.

**Summary.** This paper provides a definitive correction to the crystal structure of the Al5C3N ceramic. By combining experimental diffraction data with computational DFT, the authors prove that the material is centrosymmetric (P63/mmc) rather than non-centrosymmetric. They identify a disordered distribution of carbon and nitrogen atoms as the stable configuration. This structural clarification is crucial for predicting the material's electronic and physical properties.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is the ternary Al-C-N ceramic compound Al5C3N, characterized by a nanolaminate-like stacking sequence of Al2C, Al(C/N), and Al2(C/N)2 layers.

**Key observables.** Space group symmetry, atomic occupancies of C and N, lattice constants (a and c), band gaps (direct and indirect), and vibrational modes (Raman/IR).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (up to 2000 C), N2 partial pressure, formation energy difference (0.2 eV/f.u.), and neutron scattering lengths for C and N.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the observed centrosymmetry might arise from a disordered supercell or lattice modulation rather than simple structural twinning.

**Figures summary.** Figures include comparisons of crystal structures, XRD/neutron diffraction patterns, STEM intensity profiles, EDS chemical mapping, and electronic band structures/DOS.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the motivation regarding structural disorder, describes the synthesis and characterization techniques, presents the rejection of the P63mc model via diffraction and DFT, proposes the new P63/mmc disordered model, and concludes with electronic property implications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The high-temperature ceramic compound Al5C3N with promising application usage belongs to the scarcely studied Al-C-N system. It was originally reported as an ordered compound in the non-centrosymmetric space group P63mc and described as a nanolaminate with an -Al2C-AlN-Al2C2- stacking sequence. The recently reported structural disorder in the related compound Al4SiC4 led us to question this proposed structure for Al5C3N and investigate the possibility of a disordered structure in the centrosymmetric space group P63/mmc. In the present work, we employed different synthesis routes to maximize the yield and quality of the desired phase, and applied a variety of techniques to probe the Al5C3N crystal structure. Our single-crystal X-ray diffraction analysis clearly indicates that the non-centrosymmetric space group P63mc must be rejected. From a joint refinement of single-crystal X-ray and powder neutron diffraction data, the occupancies of C and N were refined at two sites in P63/mmc resulting in the stacking sequence -Al2C-Al(C/N)-Al2(C/N)2-. Furthermore, DFT calculations show that a centrosymmetric disordered structure described in a supercell has the lowest energy, 0.2 eV per formula unit, relative to the previously reported P63mc structure. The calculated band structure shows both direct and indirect band gaps which lead to implications for the physical properties. Finally, STEM analysis provides additional evidence that the crystal structure of Al5C3N is better described in the centrosymmetric space group P63/mmc.

</details>

**Main problem.** Understanding the relationship between settling velocity and porosity for complex, porous, non-spherical objects and determining if a universal scaling law exists.

**Main result.** The study finds a power-law relationship between settling speed and solid fraction, with an exponent of 0.43 in bounded containers and 0.30 in an unbounded domain after correcting for wall effects.

**Method.** The researchers used 3D-printed Bravais lattice unit-cells in a viscous fluid, combined with high-speed imaging, Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV), Stokesian Dynamics (SD) simulations, and Boundary Integral Methods (BIM).

**Summary.** This paper investigates how the shape and porosity of complex, lattice-like structures affect their settling speed in a viscous fluid. By studying various Bravais lattice unit-cells, the authors discovered a power-law scaling between settling velocity and solid volume fraction. They specifically highlight how container walls significantly bias experimental measurements and demonstrate that applying Faxén corrections reveals a more universal scaling exponent. This work provides insights into the sedimentation of complex aggregates, which is crucial for understanding transport in oceans and the atmosphere.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of various 3D-printed Bravais lattice unit-cells (such as SC, BCC, and FCC) made of spheres connected by slender rods, settling in a highly viscous silicone oil within a glass container.

**Key observables.** Terminal settling velocity (U), solid volume fraction (phi_s), and fluid velocity fields.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low Reynolds number (Re approx 10^-4), lattice spacing (d), sphere radius (a), and the ratio of particle size to container size.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is in the Stokesian regime; the SC lattice analysis assumes a far-field limit (a/d << 1) for certain models; and slender-body theory assumes rods do not interact hydrodynamically with each other.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the experimental setup and lattice types; Figure 2 shows PIV setup and streamlines; Figure 3 and 4 show normalized velocity vs. solid fraction in bounded and unbounded domains; Figure 5 illustrates the SD model; Figure 6 shows fluid velocity fields; Figure 7 compares experimental data to various models; Figure 9 shows the influence of rods on settling speed.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of porous object settling, describes the experimental setup and lattice geometries, presents experimental scaling results, details the numerical/theoretical models used to account for wall effects and rod interactions, and concludes with a comparison of the findings to existing theories like the Darcy-Brinkman model.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study experimentally and theoretically the Stokesian settling of a well-known class of porous shapes: Bravais lattice unit-cells, whose porosity we vary controllably by changing their lattice spacing. In our experiments, conducted in a square cuboidal container with its long-axis aligned along gravity, we find that the settling speed U and the solid fraction φ of these lattice units obey a power-law relationship U $\propto$ φ^γ , with an exponent γ = 0.43 independent of their shape. To understand the observed scaling exponent, we analytically and numerically investigate the settling of the simple cubic structure under different approximations. We find that the walls of the container, though far from the sinking object, have a defining effect. Our Stokesian boundary integral simulations show that the Faxen's boundary correction captures the wall-effects accurately and enables us to discount the wall-effect from the experimental data, yielding a power-law exponent γ = 0.30 for settling in an unbounded domain. The power-law relating sinking speed and porosity is a step towards predictively understanding the sedimentation fluxes of complex objects in the clouds and the oceans. However, the applicability of this universal scaling to irregular and biologically richer aggregates found in nature remains an open direction.

</details>
